lessons.lesson_title: Lesson 1 – Program Launch & Game Readiness Review

metadata.title: Program Launch & Game Readiness Review
core_topics: How you take ownership of decisions
I used my own judgement to make decisions
ai_focus: Please complete it under strict exam conditions: no use of AI tools or web search is allowed. Your results will be shared with you by your teacher.
Mandatory AI Literacy Diagnostic Test
AI involvement – where AI helped and what still needs human review.
Reflect: AI Responsibility
Take a moment to reflect on your current understanding of AI responsibility. Use the questions below to guide your written reflection.
What do you already understand about AI responsibility? (Example: risks of AI, fairness, privacy, honesty when using AI tools, etc.)
I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI
🤖  AI Literacy Reflection
Reflect: AI in Game Readiness
As you review your game’s readiness and set goals for Term 3, take a moment to reflect on how AI should be used responsibly in this process.
Write 2–3 sentences describing your current awareness of how AI should be used ethically and responsibly.
One part of evaluating my game or setting goals that must remain a human decision is:
I justified a professional launch readiness decision using player evidence, risk analysis, and responsible AI judgement.
Extension / Adaptive Task – Create a Personal AI Responsibility Code
Produce a one-page document titled:📁 “My Responsible AI Pledge”
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
Code: K4.1 (AI generates outputs but does not understand context, goals, or standards unless guided by humans.)
learning_objectives: Evaluation of Game Readiness: Assess your game objectively by identifying strengths, gaps, and areas needing improvement before launch.
Setting Goals for Term 3: Define specific objectives that guide your iteration and professional growth throughout the term.
I identified strengths and weaknesses in my game and set at least one realistic improvement goal.
I analysed my game using structured evaluation and set SMART goals supported by gameplay evidence.
I justified a professional launch readiness decision using player evidence, risk analysis, and responsible AI judgement.
metadata.activity_description:
What happens when a game launches too early? Why do professional studios delay releases? Who is responsible for quality at launch?

Discuss as class some things you would fix or improve before releasing your game.

Why is it important for the player?

Explore: Game State Review

It's time to take a comprehensive look at your current game build. This review will help you understand exactly where your project stands and what needs attention before real players interact with it.

🔗Recap from Term 2’s Core Pillars

Do the main mechanics work as intended? Are the controls responsive and reliable?

Do the core gameplay loops function without breaking?

Design & Dynamics (Player Experience)

Is the game engaging or frustrating? Is difficulty balanced and fair?

Does the game feel enjoyable to play, even in short sessions?

Narrative (Clarity & Flow / Guidance)

Is it clear what the player should do next? Does the game guide players naturally?Are goals, rules, and progression easy to understand?

Portfolio Entry 17– Identify Gaps

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Problem Solving| 🟥 Critical Thinking

-Technical bugs appearing close to launch

-Game Objective is unclear

-Introduce new mechanics gradually

Use a SWOT analysis to evaluate your current game honestly and plan improvements.

Use bullet points, see example in the graphic. *Modify examples to suit your game.

Strengths: What is already working well in your game

Weaknesses: What needs fixing or improving

Opportunities: New ideas or ways to make the game better

Threats: Risks or problems that could stop success

Portfolio Entry 17– Identify Gaps

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Problem Solving| 🟥 Critical Thinking

-Technical bugs appearing close to launch

-Game Objective is unclear

-Introduce new mechanics gradually

Use a SWOT analysis to evaluate your current game honestly and plan improvements.

Use bullet points, see example in the graphic. *Modify examples to suit your game.

Strengths: What is already working well in your game

Weaknesses: What needs fixing or improving

Opportunities: New ideas or ways to make the game better

Threats: Risks or problems that could stop success

Activity 1.1— Selecting Goals for Term 3

Choose 2–3 areas from the table below, or replace one with your own area, and write a SMART goal for each selected area. Each goal must be one sentence and follow the Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound criteria.

|  | Goal | Steps to Achieve It | Deadline |

| Player Experience & Polish |  |  |  |

| Testing & Feedback |  |  |  |

| AI Workflow & Decision-Making |  |  |  |

| Technical Stability / Mechanics |  |  |  |

Footer note: these goals define how you will refine, validate, and present your game during Term 3.

🔗 Review SMART goals from Term 2 – Reflection & Planning

Revisit “Portfolio Entry 14B” and analyse your previously set goals.

Stretch Activity – Studio Readiness Audit: Evaluating Your Game for Launch

Make a go/no-go decision for your game project. Decide whether your game is ready to release, needs more improvements, or is not ready yet.

Explain your choice using real game industry criteria, considering player expectations, technical quality, and how well the game meets its goals.

Is your main mechanic immediately clear to new players? Can they understand the goal without explanation?

Does your game run without major bugs, crashes, or game-blocking issues? Even minor stability problems can damage player trust and studio reputation at launch.

External validation is critical. Have you conducted playtests with people outside your development team? Do players understand what to do and why they're doing it?

Document where AI tools supported your development. Identify AI-generated content that needs review or refinement to meet quality standards. Transparency about AI use builds credibility.

Identify your single biggest risk if launching today. Consider technical vulnerabilities, ethical concerns, potential player experience issues, or threats to studio reputation. This honest evaluation protects your long-term success.

Stretch Activity – Studio Readiness Audit: Making Final Decision

Make a final launch decision for your game project.

Based on your current progress, choose one option and explain why.

Your explanation should reference: Player experience, Stability or risk, Evidence from testing or review

Needs improvement before release

Extension / Adaptive Task – Create a Personal AI Responsibility Code

Design your own “Student AI Use Code” for this course.

Create a structured list that includes:

5 rules you will personally follow when using AI

3 things you will never do with AI

2 ways you will check AI outputs for accuracy

Produce a one-page document titled:📁 “My Responsible AI Pledge”

Teaching note: Before starting any work please have the students complete the diagnostic here. They will repeat the same set of questions at the end of term.

Use this question to shift students from student mindset to studio mindset.

Display this slide immediately after the Learning Objectives. Revisit it mid-lesson to check which criteria students feel confident about. Inspectors should see learners able to recall one or more criteria in their own words. This links directly to the Product Development Cycle and IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.Links to IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.

Use this starter to introduce a professional game studio mindset. Ask students to think about what happens when games launch too early (bugs, poor reviews, player frustration) and why studios delay releases (testing, polish, player experience).

Explain that this activity helps students take an honest snapshot of where their game currently stands before real players experience it.

Start with Core Mechanics. Ask students to check whether the main mechanics work reliably, controls respond properly, and gameplay loops don’t break. Emphasise that if the mechanics fail, the rest of the game cannot succeed.

Move to Design & Dynamics (Player Experience). Prompt students to think about how the game feels to play. Is it engaging or frustrating? Is the difficulty fair and enjoyable, even in short play sessions?

Finish with Narrative (Clarity & Flow). Ask students to consider whether players know what to do next. Are goals, rules, and progression clear, or does the player feel lost?

Ensure that students keep these points concise, in their own words.

Explain that this portfolio entry asks students to evaluate their game honestly using a SWOT analysis. Emphasise that this is about reflection and planning, not criticism.

Ensure that students keep these points concise, in their own words.

Explain that this portfolio entry asks students to evaluate their game honestly using a SWOT analysis. Emphasise that this is about reflection and planning, not criticism.

Explain that students are now setting clear, focused goals to guide their work in Term 3. They should choose 2–3 areas from the table, or replace one with an area that better fits their game.

This activity is not part of the portfolio or assessment. Please skip if time is short during the lesson.

Frame this as a real studio go / no-go decision, not a checklist task.

Emphasise justification: students must explain why they would launch or delay.

This activity is not part of the portfolio or assessment. Please skip if there is no time.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

This question checks students’ understanding of human judgment when using AI.
artifacts: Portfolio Entry 14B” and analyse your previously set goals.
assessment_signals: I can clearly explain what works well in my game and what needs improvement, using specific examples from gameplay.
I can establish realistic, focused improvement goals that will guide my work this term and measure progress toward launch readiness.

lessons.big_question: How ready is my game — and am I ready — to run this like a real studio?
lessons.uae_link: This term focuses on preparing digital products for release in a way that reflects the UAE’s emphasis on quality, responsibility, and innovation in technology and media. Reflection Question: How can careful preparation before release improve how players experience and trust a digital product?
success_criteria: Articulate Game Status
I can clearly explain what works well in my game and what needs improvement, using specific examples from gameplay.
Define Improvement Goals
I can establish realistic, focused improvement goals that will guide my work this term and measure progress toward launch readiness.
Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.
I identified strengths and weaknesses in my game and set at least one realistic improvement goal.
I analysed my game using structured evaluation and set SMART goals supported by gameplay evidence.
I justified a professional launch readiness decision using player evidence, risk analysis, and responsible AI judgement.

teacher_notes.notes: Teaching note: Before starting any work please have the students complete the diagnostic here. They will repeat the same set of questions at the end of term.

Set the tone for Term 3 as a shift toward release readiness, not just building.
Emphasise reflection on current game state and identifying gaps.
Frame goal-setting as preparation for real players and real expectations.
Link to UAE values: quality, responsibility, and innovation in digital products.
Use the reflection question to focus on player trust and experience, not features.

Use this question to shift students from student mindset to studio mindset.
Emphasise that readiness includes both the game and the team behind it.
Encourage thinking beyond features: planning, testing, decisions, and responsibility.
Reinforce that real studios are judged on how they prepare, not just ideas.

Curriculum Alignment
Analysing an existing digital product as a system aligns with UK D&T KS3 (1c – Systems & Control), AQA 3.1.3 (Systems Architecture – system components and limitations), IB Design Criterion C (analysis before creating a solution), and CSTA 3A-CS-02 (evaluating computing system behaviour and constraints).

Display this slide immediately after the Learning Objectives. Revisit it mid-lesson to check which criteria students feel confident about. Inspectors should see learners able to recall one or more criteria in their own words. This links directly to the Product Development Cycle and IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.Links to IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.

Use this starter to introduce a professional game studio mindset. Ask students to think about what happens when games launch too early (bugs, poor reviews, player frustration) and why studios delay releases (testing, polish, player experience).
Discuss who is responsible for quality at launch, highlighting that quality is a shared responsibility across a team.

Explain that this activity helps students take an honest snapshot of where their game currently stands before real players experience it.
Start with Core Mechanics. Ask students to check whether the main mechanics work reliably, controls respond properly, and gameplay loops don’t break. Emphasise that if the mechanics fail, the rest of the game cannot succeed.
Move to Design & Dynamics (Player Experience). Prompt students to think about how the game feels to play. Is it engaging or frustrating? Is the difficulty fair and enjoyable, even in short play sessions?
Finish with Narrative (Clarity & Flow). Ask students to consider whether players know what to do next. Are goals, rules, and progression clear, or does the player feel lost?

Ensure that students keep these points concise, in their own words.
**** Make sure exemplar entries are removed. 

Explain that this portfolio entry asks students to evaluate their game honestly using a SWOT analysis. Emphasise that this is about reflection and planning, not criticism.
Remind students what each part means:
Strengths: what already works well and should be kept
Weaknesses: areas that need fixing or improvement
Opportunities: new ideas or changes that could improve the game
Threats: risks that could affect success, such as bugs, confusion, or time limits
Highlight the skills involved: Computational Thinking (breaking the game into parts), Problem Solving (deciding what to improve), and Critical Thinking (evaluating honestly).

Ensure that students keep these points concise, in their own words.
**** Make sure exemplar entries are removed.

Remind students what each part means:
Strengths: what already works well and should be kept
Weaknesses: areas that need fixing or improvement
Opportunities: new ideas or changes that could improve the game
Threats: risks that could affect success, such as bugs, confusion, or time limits
Highlight the skills involved: Computational Thinking (breaking the game into parts), Problem Solving (deciding what to improve), and Critical Thinking (evaluating honestly).

Emphasise that each student will set three clear goals, with each one focusing on a different responsibility such as game improvement, testing and feedback, or AI decision-making.
Use the example goals to highlight what a strong goal looks like: it is specific, measurable, and linked to player experience or responsible AI use. Reinforce that these goals will guide their actions throughout Term 3, not just this lesson.

Link back to goals they created in Term 2. See do they still align .

This activity is not part of the portfolio or assessment. Please skip if time is short during the lesson. 

Frame this as a real studio go / no-go decision, not a checklist task.
Emphasise justification: students must explain why they would launch or delay.
Guide focus across five professional criteria:
Core experience – is the main mechanic clear immediately?
Player perspective – do external players understand and enjoy it?
Stability – are bugs or crashes harming trust?
AI involvement – where AI helped and what still needs human review.
Risk assessment – identify the single biggest launch risk honestly.
Reinforce that strong studios delay launches when needed to protect quality and reputation.
This activity is not part of the portfolio or assessment. Please skip if there is no time.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

This question checks students’ understanding of human judgment when using AI.
It reinforces that AI can inform decisions, but humans must evaluate purpose and impact.
This is a practice question only, to be completed in Classkick.
Answer key: C

Teaching note: This question targets the misconception that confidence equals correctness in AI outputs.
It reinforces the need to verify AI suggestions using human judgment.
This is a practice question only, to be completed in Classkick.
Answer key: B

Teaching note: This question highlights that AI outputs often need human refinement to meet clear standards (e.g. SMART goals).
It reinforces that AI can generate ideas but cannot define quality or context on its own.

Answer key: B

slides.content:
Please scan the QR code or click the link below to begin your diagnostic test. This assessment helps us understand your current knowledge and is required for all students. (15-20 mins)

Important: This test is not part of your grade, but it is mandatory. 
Please complete it under strict exam conditions: no use of AI tools or web search is allowed. Your results will be shared with you by your teacher.

Click here to start the assessment

The Explorer's Programme

Mandatory AI Literacy Diagnostic Test

‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 1

‹#›

Today, we'll reflect on your progress, evaluate where your game stands, and set clear goals for the exciting work ahead.

Lesson 1: Program Launch & Game Readiness Review

Evaluating game readiness

‹#›

UAE Link: This term focuses on preparing digital products for release in a way that reflects the UAE’s emphasis on quality, responsibility, and innovation in technology and media.
Reflection Question: How can careful preparation before release improve how players experience and trust a digital product?

Welcome to Term 3! This lesson marks an important milestone in your game development journey.

Big Question / Big Picture

Game Design

‹#›

How ready is my game — and am I ready — to run this like a real studio?

Lesson 1: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Evaluation of Game Readiness
Assess your game objectively by identifying strengths, gaps, and areas needing improvement before launch.

Setting Goals for Term 3
Define specific objectives that guide your iteration and professional growth throughout the term.

Lesson 1: Success Criteria – Game Readiness Review

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Articulate Game Status
I can clearly explain what works well in my game and what needs improvement, using specific examples from gameplay.

Define Improvement Goals
I can establish realistic, focused improvement goals that will guide my work this term and measure progress toward launch readiness.

Starter: Studio Mindset

‹#›

Discussion Questions

What happens when a game launches too early? Why do professional studios delay releases? Who is responsible for quality at launch?

Connection Task

Discuss as class some things you would fix or improve before releasing your game.

What is the improvement?
Why is it important for the player?

Explore: Game State Review

‹#›

It's time to take a comprehensive look at your current game build. This review will help you understand exactly where your project stands and what needs attention before real players interact with it.

🔗Recap from Term 2’s Core Pillars

Core Mechanics
Do the main mechanics work as intended? Are the controls responsive and reliable?
Do the core gameplay loops function without breaking?

1

Design & Dynamics (Player Experience) 
Is the game engaging or frustrating? Is difficulty balanced and fair?
Does the game feel enjoyable to play, even in short sessions?

2

Narrative (Clarity & Flow / Guidance)
Is it clear what the player should do next? Does the game guide players naturally?Are goals, rules, and progression easy to understand?

3

Portfolio Entry 17– Identify Gaps

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Problem Solving| 🟥 Critical Thinking

-Technical bugs appearing close to launch
-

-Game Objective is unclear
-

-

Use a SWOT analysis to evaluate your current game honestly and plan improvements.

Use bullet points, see example in the graphic. *Modify examples to suit your game.


Strengths: What is already working well in your game
Weaknesses: What needs fixing or improving
Opportunities: New ideas or ways to make the game better
Threats: Risks or problems that could stop success

Weaknesses: What needs fixing or improving
Opportunities: New ideas or ways to make the game better
Threats: Risks or problems that could stop success

Analyse–Term 3 Goals

‹#›

In Term 3, you are no longer just building a game , you are running a studio. 
Your goals should guide:
How you improve your game
How you use AI responsibly
How you take ownership of decisions
You will set three goals, each focusing on a different responsibility.

Example Goals 
“By Week 3, I will improve player experience by testing my game with at least three players and making two changes based on their feedback.”
“During Term 3, I will use AI to support testing and planning, but I will check all AI suggestions before deciding what to implement.”

Activity 1.1— Selecting Goals for Term 3

‹#›

Choose 2–3 areas from the table below, or replace one with your own area, and write a SMART goal for each selected area. Each goal must be one sentence and follow the Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound criteria.


|  | Goal | Steps to Achieve It | Deadline |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Player Experience & Polish |  |  |  |
| Testing & Feedback |  |  |  |
| AI Workflow & Decision-Making |  |  |  |
| Technical Stability / Mechanics |  |  |  |

Footer note: these goals define how you will refine, validate, and present your game during Term 3.

🟦🟥

🔗 Review SMART goals from Term 2 – Reflection & Planning 
Revisit “Portfolio Entry 14B” and analyse your previously set goals.

Stretch Activity – Studio Readiness Audit: Evaluating Your Game for Launch

‹#›

Make a go/no-go decision for your game project. Decide whether your game is ready to release, needs more improvements, or is not ready yet.
Explain your choice using real game industry criteria, considering player expectations, technical quality, and how well the game meets its goals.

1

Core Experience

Is your main mechanic immediately clear to new players? Can they understand the goal without explanation?

2

Stability & Functionality

Does your game run without major bugs, crashes, or game-blocking issues? Even minor stability problems can damage player trust and studio reputation at launch.

3

Player Perspective

External validation is critical. Have you conducted playtests with people outside your development team? Do players understand what to do and why they're doing it?

AI Involvement

Document where AI tools supported your development. Identify AI-generated content that needs review or refinement to meet quality standards. Transparency about AI use builds credibility.

5

Studio Risk Assessment

Identify your single biggest risk if launching today. Consider technical vulnerabilities, ethical concerns, potential player experience issues, or threats to studio reputation. This honest evaluation protects your long-term success.

4

Stretch Activity – Studio Readiness Audit: Making Final Decision

Make a final launch decision for your game project.
Based on your current progress, choose one option and explain why.

I chose this because…

Your explanation should reference: Player experience, Stability or risk, Evidence from testing or review

Justification

Release Decision
Ready to release soon
Needs improvement before release
Not ready for release

Reflect: AI Responsibility

‹#›

Take a moment to reflect on your current understanding of AI responsibility. Use the questions below to guide your written reflection.

What do you already understand about AI responsibility? (Example: risks of AI, fairness, privacy, honesty when using AI tools, etc.)
 What examples of responsible or irresponsible AI use have you seen? (Example: AI used to help learning, AI spreading false information, biased systems, etc.)
What rules do you think people should follow when using AI?

My Reflection:

Write 2–3 sentences focusing on your current understanding of AI responsibility. This reflection helps you capture your starting point before moving into new learning for Term 3.

I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI
I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away
I used my own judgement to make decisions

✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:

Today AI helped me by…

I still needed to decide…

🤖  AI Literacy Reflection

Reflect: AI in Game Readiness

As you review your game’s readiness and set goals for Term 3, take a moment to reflect on how AI should be used responsibly in this process.


Write 2–3 sentences describing your current awareness of how AI should be used ethically and responsibly.

AI Responsibility
One way AI could support reviewing my game’s readiness or identifying gaps is:
_________________________________________________________________________________
Think about where AI can assist, not decide.

Human Judgement
One part of evaluating my game or setting goals that must remain a human decision is:
_________________________________________________________________________________
Think about decisions that require values, priorities, or accountability.

Risks & Concerns
One risk of relying too much on AI when planning improvements or goals is:
_________________________________________________________________________________
Think about over-reliance, bias, or losing creative control.

📌Each response must include a reason or example from your own game.

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I identified strengths and weaknesses in my game and set at least one realistic improvement goal.

Most

I analysed my game using structured evaluation and set SMART goals supported by gameplay evidence.

Some

I justified a professional launch readiness decision using player evidence, risk analysis, and responsible AI judgement.

Extension / Adaptive Task – Create a Personal AI Responsibility Code

‹#›

Your Challenge
Design your own “Student AI Use Code” for this course.
Task Instructions
Create a structured list that includes:
5 rules you will personally follow when using AI
3 things you will never do with AI
2 ways you will check AI outputs for accuracy

Final Output
Produce a one-page document titled:📁 “My Responsible AI Pledge”

Lesson 1 – Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 1.1

A student uses AI to analyze playtest feedback. The AI suggests removing a difficult puzzle because many players skip it. However, the puzzle is a key learning moment in the game.

What should the student do?

A. Remove the puzzle because AI analyzed more data

B.  Ignore the AI’s recommendations

C. Use human judgment to weigh the AI’s advice against the game’s purpose

D. Let the AI decide what parts to remove.

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

During reflection, a student believes that because AI gives confident suggestions, those suggestions must always be correct.

What is the most appropriate action?

A. Follow the AI advice without checking

B. Use AI suggestions but verify them with human judgement

C. Stop using AI completely

D. Let AI decide all improvements

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 1.2

Lesson 1 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
nowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

A student uses an AI tool to help set their Term 3 goals. The AI suggests the goal:“Improve your game next term.”

What is the most significant problem with this goal?

A. The AI suggestion is too creative

B. The student did not apply human judgement to refine the AI output

C. The AI tool is not allowed for goal setting

D. The goal focuses too much on teamwork

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 1.3

Lesson 1 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (AI generates outputs but does not understand context, goals, or standards unless guided by humans.)

metadata.activity_description:
Lesson 2: Defining your Launch Strategy

UAE Link: Roblox was banned in the UAE from 2018-2021 due to content and safety concerns, showing why digital products must be carefully planned, reviewed, and aligned with local values before release.

Reflection Question: How can careful planning and responsibility before release help a game avoid problems after launch?

Today you’ll identify what to improve, who your game is for, and how success will be measured.

Use AI to support planning,

By the end, you’ll have a clear plan to guide your game’s launch and next steps.

Lesson 2: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

Define the Launch ProblemsClearly identify and explain the key issues or risks that could affect your game at launch, such as clarity, balance, performance, or player understanding.

Allocate AI and Human Tasks

Decide which parts of the launch and improvement process should be supported by AI and which require human judgement.

Starter: Problems First. Solutions Second.

What happens when studios rush changes without clearly defining the problem? Why do professional teams define goals before improving features?

Below are four statements about a game in development:

“Players quit during the first level.”

“Add a tutorial popup at the start.”

“The game feels confusing to new players.”

“Increase difficulty in level two.”

In pairs, sort each statement into Problem or Solution and discuss Why does confusing problem and solution lead to poor decisions?"

Explore: Why Pre-Launch Planning Matters

Games that launched with clear goals and understood player expectations from day one.

Games that struggled due to unclear goals, misaligned features, or unmet player expectations.

Choose one successful game and one failed game.

Use AI to summarise player feedback or reviews.

As a team, analyse the feedback using the questions below and agree on ONE main reason for each outcome.

Did the game deliver what players expected?

What problems or frustrations did players mention most?

What is ONE main reason the launch went well or badly?

Activity 2.1: AI Brainstorming

Follow this three-step process to use AI effectively while maintaining human judgment and decision-making.

Use AI to list possible improvements for your game.

Cross out weak, unrealistic, or irrelevant ideas.

Select your top 3 improvements and explain why they matter.

AI suggests ideas — you are responsible for choosing the right ones. – OECD K3.3 — Human judgment and responsibility

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Critical Thinking || 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟥 Computational Thinking

“I am working on a game about [briefly describe your game].

Suggest possible improvements related to gameplay, player experience, or clarity.

Activity 2.1– A.I Brainstorm

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Critical Thinking || 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟥 Computational Thinking

—-----------------------------------------------------------

—-----------------------------------------------------------

—-----------------------------------------------------------

Analyse–Automation vs. Human Judgment

Not all tasks are created equal. Understanding which work benefits from AI assistance and which requires human oversight is a critical skill in modern game development.

Brainstorming possibilities

Summarizing player feedback

Prioritization and strategy

Key Question: Just because AI can do something, should it?

Activity 2.2-Game Launch Task Allocation

| Category | Task | Allocation(Human, AI, Shared) | Justification |

| Build & Tech | Fix bugs & test performance |  |  |

| Build & Tech | Check controls & playability |  |  |

| Build & Tech | Create & upload final build |  |  |

| Design | Decide what features are “finished” |  |  |

| Design | Balance difficulty & progression |  |  |

| Design | Judge fun & player experience |  |  |

| Testing | Collect & summarise feedback |  |  |

| Testing | Decide which feedback to act on |  |  |

| Communication | Write description & launch post |  |  |

| Communication | Create visuals (thumbnail/banner/promo) |  |  |

| Responsibility | Review fairness & bias |  |  |

| Responsibility | Final ethics & quality check |  |  |

Sort each launch task into the correct category and decide whether it should be handled by AI, humans, or both, explaining your reasoning to show responsible decision-making.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Critical Thinking | 🟥 Computational Thinking

Portfolio Entry 18– Launch Brief

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟥 Computational Thinking

Now it’s time to create your own Launch Brief. This planning template will guide your first game release and demonstrate your understanding of professional game development and responsible launch practices.

Download the 🔗Launch Brief Template

Complete the Launch Brief based on your game.

Screenshot both Launch Brief pages and paste below.

Portfolio Entry 18– Launch Brief

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟥 Computational Thinking

Now it’s time to create your own Launch Brief. This planning template will guide your first game release and demonstrate your understanding of professional game development and responsible launch practices.

Download the 🔗Launch Brief Template

Complete the Launch Brief based on your game.

Screenshot both Launch Brief pages and paste below.

Portfolio Entry 18– Launch Brief

Game Title: _______________________

Short Gameplay Description: In this game, the player will _____________________________________

What is the most important problem to fix before release?

Age Range of Players: ________

🛠 Planned Improvements (Before Launch)

List up to 3 changes you will make:

1. ______________________________

2. ______________________________

3. ______________________________

Choose 2–3 measurable signs of success:

Players understand the objective without help

Review all three pillars, then identify your main focus for this launch.

Mechanics – what the player does

Design/ Dynamics  – how the game feels

Narrative / Theme – why the game matters

Why this pillar needs the most attention right now?_________________________

Ask at least one other person to play

Check instructions or guidance

Confirm the game has a clear ending

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟥 Computational Thinking

Now it’s time to create your own Launch Brief. This planning template will guide your first game release and demonstrate your understanding of professional game development and responsible launch practices.

Players don’t understand what to do

Core mechanic isn’t fun or clear

Progression feels confusing or slow

Feedback (sound / visuals / response) is weak

Game feels unfinished or unpolished

Portfolio Entry 18– Launch Brief Continued

How Will Players Hear About the Game?

☐ Class playtest session☐ Teacher or peer demo☐ Showcase or exhibition☐ Short presentation☐ Shared link or post

How I will reduce this risk:

__________________________________________________

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟥 Computational Thinking

Needs improvements before launch

Reflect: AI Suggestion - When to say no

During today’s AI brainstorming task, AI generated possible improvements for your game design and gameplay experience.

A key AI literacy skill is knowing when to accept an AI suggestion - and when to reject it using human judgement.

Choose ONE AI suggestion you decided NOT to use

Declined AI Game Improvement : __________________

Write your justification using ONE specific detail from your game design (example: player goal, mechanic, level design, or playtest feedback).

_________________________________________________________________________________________

What might have gone wrong if you had followed this AI suggestion?

_________________________________________________________________________________________

Why I Rejected This AI-Brainstormed Improvement

☐ It didn’t fit the target players for my game☐ It didn’t match my game’s vision or style

☐ It was unrealistic or too complex to build

☐ It didn’t solve the real gameplay problem

☐ It would make the game less fun or less clear

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

I clearly defined a key launch problem and completed a basic Launch Brief.

I differentiated between problems and solutions, set measurable success criteria, and allocate AI vs human tasks appropriately.

I critically evaluated AI limitations and justified which launch decisions must remain human-led.

Extension / Adaptive Task –Responsible AI Launch Review

Imagine your game is about to be released publicly.

1.  Review your Launch BriefLook at:

AI vs Human task allocation

2. Evaluate AI UseAnswer the following:

Where did AI provide useful support?

Where would using AI be risky or inappropriate?

Which launch decision must never be made by AI?

3.  Create a “Launch Responsibility Statement”Write 2–3 sentences that explain:

How AI supported your launch decisions, and Why humans remain responsible for the final release.

Which task is most appropriate for AI support during the launch brief?

A. Deciding whether the game is ready to release

B. Setting the final success criteria

C. Generating ideas for possible improvements

D. Taking responsibility for launch decisions

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles

Code: K3.2 (Decide which tasks AI can handle and which need humans).

Ask students to keep it in mind for the whole lesson

Defining system goals and allocating tasks aligns with UK D&T KS3 1c (defining system purpose), AQA 3.1.3 (role of software and processes), IB Criterion C (planning the creation of a solution), and CSTA 3A-CS-02 (decomposing problems within a computing system).

Guide students to clearly define what to improve, who the game is for, and how success will be judged

Reinforce that AI suggests ideas, but students make final decisions

Discuss which tasks suit AI support and which need human judgment

Purpose Students decide which launch tasks should be done by AI, humans, or both, and justify their choices to show responsible AI use.

Sort each task and write a brief justification.

This portfolio task simulates a real studio launch planning document.

Students must focus on fixing key problems, not adding new features.

Emphasise that AI can highlight issues, but students choose priorities.

Look for evidence that students questioned AI suggestions, not accepted them blindly.

Emphasise that AI can highlight issues, but students choose priorities.

Look for evidence that students questioned AI suggestions, not accepted them blindly.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

This task only to be completed if time allows.

This task pushes students to think like a studio before public release.

Students revisit their Launch Brief to check consistency and readiness.

It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.

This question helps students recognise that AI is best used for idea generation, not ownership of decisions.

Students must understand that humans are accountable for decisions made using AI support, especially after launch.
assessment_signals: I can establish realistic, focused launch goals that guide final preparation and measure readiness for the first release.

lessons.detailed_activities:
Lesson 2: Defining your Launch Strategy

UAE Link: Roblox was banned in the UAE from 2018-2021 due to content and safety concerns, showing why digital products must be carefully planned, reviewed, and aligned with local values before release.

Reflection Question: How can careful planning and responsibility before release help a game avoid problems after launch?

Today you’ll identify what to improve, who your game is for, and how success will be measured.

Use AI to support planning,

By the end, you’ll have a clear plan to guide your game’s launch and next steps.

Lesson 2: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

Define the Launch ProblemsClearly identify and explain the key issues or risks that could affect your game at launch, such as clarity, balance, performance, or player understanding.

Allocate AI and Human Tasks

Decide which parts of the launch and improvement process should be supported by AI and which require human judgement.

Starter: Problems First. Solutions Second.

What happens when studios rush changes without clearly defining the problem? Why do professional teams define goals before improving features?

Below are four statements about a game in development:

“Players quit during the first level.”

“Add a tutorial popup at the start.”

“The game feels confusing to new players.”

“Increase difficulty in level two.”

In pairs, sort each statement into Problem or Solution and discuss Why does confusing problem and solution lead to poor decisions?"

Explore: Why Pre-Launch Planning Matters

Games that launched with clear goals and understood player expectations from day one.

Games that struggled due to unclear goals, misaligned features, or unmet player expectations.

Choose one successful game and one failed game.

Use AI to summarise player feedback or reviews.

As a team, analyse the feedback using the questions below and agree on ONE main reason for each outcome.

Did the game deliver what players expected?

What problems or frustrations did players mention most?

What is ONE main reason the launch went well or badly?

Activity 2.1: AI Brainstorming

Follow this three-step process to use AI effectively while maintaining human judgment and decision-making.

Use AI to list possible improvements for your game.

Cross out weak, unrealistic, or irrelevant ideas.

Select your top 3 improvements and explain why they matter.

AI suggests ideas — you are responsible for choosing the right ones. – OECD K3.3 — Human judgment and responsibility

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Critical Thinking || 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟥 Computational Thinking

“I am working on a game about [briefly describe your game].

Suggest possible improvements related to gameplay, player experience, or clarity.

—-----------------------------------------------------------

—-----------------------------------------------------------

Analyse–Automation vs. Human Judgment

Not all tasks are created equal. Understanding which work benefits from AI assistance and which requires human oversight is a critical skill in modern game development.

Brainstorming possibilities

Summarizing player feedback

Prioritization and strategy

Key Question: Just because AI can do something, should it?

| Responsibility | Review fairness & bias |  |  |

| Responsibility | Final ethics & quality check |  |  |

Sort each launch task into the correct category and decide whether it should be handled by AI, humans, or both, explaining your reasoning to show responsible decision-making.

Term 3

Lesson 2

‹#›

Lesson 2: Defining your Launch Strategy

Evaluating game readiness

‹#›

UAE Link: Roblox was banned in the UAE from 2018-2021 due to content and safety concerns, showing why digital products must be carefully planned, reviewed, and aligned with local values before release.
Reflection Question: How can careful planning and responsibility before release help a game avoid problems after launch?

Today you’ll identify what to improve, who your game is for, and how success will be measured. 

Use AI to support planning, 
Decide AI vs human tasks
Create a Launch Brief.

By the end, you’ll have a clear plan to guide your game’s launch and next steps.

Big Question / Big Picture

Game Design

‹#›

How do we clearly define what to improve in our game, who it's for, and how we'll know it's successful?

Lesson 2: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Define the Launch ProblemsClearly identify and explain the key issues or risks that could affect your game at launch, such as clarity, balance, performance, or player understanding.

Allocate AI and Human Tasks
Decide which parts of the launch and improvement process should be supported by AI and which require human judgement.

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Define Improvement Goals
I can establish realistic, focused launch goals that guide final preparation and measure readiness for the first release.

Clear Problem Definition
Clearly define the game’s launch problem(s), their impact on players, and what must be addressed to ensure a successful first release.

Lesson 2: Success Criteria – Problem Definition & Launch Brief

Starter: Problems First. Solutions Second.

‹#›

Discussion Questions
What happens when studios rush changes without clearly defining the problem? Why do professional teams define goals before improving features?

Pair Task

Below are four statements about a game in development:
“Players quit during the first level.”
“Add a tutorial popup at the start.”
“The game feels confusing to new players.”
“Increase difficulty in level two.”

In pairs, sort each statement into Problem or Solution and discuss Why does confusing problem and solution lead to poor decisions?"

Explore: Why Pre-Launch Planning Matters

‹#›

Successful Launches
Games that launched with clear goals and understood player expectations from day one.

Failed Launches
Games that struggled due to unclear goals, misaligned features, or unmet player expectations.

Mini Task
Choose one successful game and one failed game.
Use AI to summarise player feedback or reviews.
As a team, analyse the feedback using the questions below and agree on ONE main reason for each outcome.

Did the game deliver what players expected?
What problems or frustrations did players mention most?
What is ONE main reason the launch went well or badly?

Activity 2.1: AI Brainstorming

Follow this three-step process to use AI effectively while maintaining human judgment and decision-making.

Step 1 — Generate (AI)

Use AI to list possible improvements for your game.

Step 2 — Filter (Human)

Cross out weak, unrealistic, or irrelevant ideas.

Step 3 — Decide (Human)

Select your top 3 improvements and explain why they matter.

AI suggests ideas — you are responsible for choosing the right ones. – OECD K3.3 — Human judgment and responsibility

https://ideamap.ai

“I am working on a game about [briefly describe your game].
Suggest possible improvements related to gameplay, player experience, or clarity.

‹#›

Analyse–Automation vs. Human Judgment

‹#›

Not all tasks are created equal. Understanding which work benefits from AI assistance and which requires human oversight is a critical skill in modern game development.

AI-Assisted Tasks

Brainstorming possibilities

Summarizing player feedback

Organizing data and notes

Generating initial drafts

Human-Led Tasks

Final decision-making

Ethical considerations

Prioritization and strategy

Creative direction

Key Question: Just because AI can do something, should it?

Activity 2.2-Game Launch Task Allocation


| Category | Task | Allocation(Human, AI, Shared) | Justification |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Build & Tech | Fix bugs & test performance |  |  |
| Build & Tech | Check controls & playability |  |  |
| Build & Tech | Create & upload final build |  |  |
| Design | Decide what features are “finished” |  |  |
| Design | Balance difficulty & progression |  |  |
| Design | Judge fun & player experience |  |  |
| Testing | Collect & summarise feedback |  |  |

| Communication | Create visuals (thumbnail/banner/promo) |  |  |
| Responsibility | Review fairness & bias |  |  |
| Responsibility | Final ethics & quality check |  |  |

Sort each launch task into the correct category and decide whether it should be handled by AI, humans, or both, explaining your reasoning to show responsible decision-making.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Critical Thinking | 🟥 Computational Thinking

‹#›

Deepen– Planning Like a Studio

What Is a Launch Brief?

A Launch Brief is a planning document used by professional studios to align teams before release. It helps guide final decisions and ensures the game is ready to launch responsibly.

Align Teams

Ensure designers, developers, artists, and marketers understand the shared vision and priorities.

Define Success

Establish clear, measurable criteria for what "successful improvement" looks like.

Prevent Wasted Effort

Without a clear brief, teams waste weeks building features players don't want. A Launch Brief keeps everyone focused on changes that truly matter.

‹#›

During today’s AI brainstorming task, AI generated possible improvements for your game design and gameplay experience.

A key AI literacy skill is knowing when to accept an AI suggestion - and when to reject it using human judgement.



Choose ONE AI suggestion you decided NOT to use

Declined AI Game Improvement : __________________











Write your justification using ONE specific detail from your game design (example: player goal, mechanic, level design, or playtest feedback).
_________________________________________________________________________________________

What might have gone wrong if you had followed this AI suggestion?

_________________________________________________________________________________________

Why I Rejected This AI-Brainstormed Improvement
☐ It didn’t fit the target players for my game☐ It didn’t match my game’s vision or style
☐ It was unrealistic or too complex to build
☐ It didn’t solve the real gameplay problem
☐ It would make the game less fun or less clear

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I clearly defined a key launch problem and completed a basic Launch Brief.

Most

I differentiated between problems and solutions, set measurable success criteria, and allocate AI vs human tasks appropriately.

Some

I critically evaluated AI limitations and justified which launch decisions must remain human-led.

Extension / Adaptive Task –Responsible AI Launch Review

‹#›

Imagine your game is about to be released publicly.

1.  Review your Launch BriefLook at:
Launch problem(s)
AI vs Human task allocation
Launch goals

2. Evaluate AI UseAnswer the following:
Where did AI provide useful support?
Where would using AI be risky or inappropriate?
Which launch decision must never be made by AI?

3.  Create a “Launch Responsibility Statement”Write 2–3 sentences that explain:
How AI supported your launch decisions, and Why humans remain responsible for the final release.

Lesson 2 – Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 2.1

A student uses AI to suggest several problems with their game, including unclear goals and confusing controls.

What is the most responsible next step?

A. Fix every issue the AI suggests

B. Choose the problem that sounds most technical

C. Review the suggestions and decide which problems matter most to players

D. Let AI decide which issue should be fixed first

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

‹#›

Which task is most appropriate for AI support during the launch brief?

A. Deciding whether the game is ready to release

B. Setting the final success criteria

C. Generating ideas for possible improvements

D. Taking responsibility for launch decisions

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 2.2

Lesson 2 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.2 (Decide which tasks AI can handle and which need humans).

‹#›

A team follows an AI-generated improvement plan that causes players to lose interest after launch.

What does this outcome most likely demonstrate about AI?

A. AI systems are unreliable and should never be used for game planning

B. AI lacks understanding of player emotions, context, and what makes games enjoyable

C. AI-generated plans will always fail when applied to creative projects

D. AI needs more training data before it can help with game design

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 2.3

Lesson 2 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (AI generates outputs but does not understand context, goals, or standards unless guided by humans.)

‹#›

Endless Studios Session

Let’s Build. Create. Experiment.Make.You Choose.

Jump into Endstar
Build and experiment
Test with your team
Fix bugs together
Try new ideas
Explore AI tools
Complete unfinished content
Choose how you want to work

Schools with 2 lessons/week: this can be self-directed learning from home. Schools with 3 lessons/week: teachers may run this as an open studio build sessions.

metadata.activity_description:
Starter: Same Prompt, Different Behavior

”Players are confused in Level 1 — improve the tutorial experience.”

In pairs, predict how each of the following would respond to the same prompt above.

What would ChatGPT generate?

What would a human developer decide?

What would an AI agent do if it could act repeatedly?

No definitions yet. Just predictions.

Today we learn: Agents don’t just respond — they can take actions

Explore: Types of AI Systems

Understanding the different types of AI helps you recognize their capabilities, limitations, and appropriate use cases. Each type serves different purposes and operates on distinct principles.

Follows fixed, predetermined instructions without adaptation. Examples include thermostats, spam filters, and system response mechanisms. Predictable but inflexible.

Learns patterns from data through machine learning algorithms. Can improve performance over time and recognize complex patterns humans might miss.

Creates new content including text, images, code, and audio. Responds to prompts and produces novel outputs based on training data patterns.

Works toward defined goals using multiple tools and rules. Operates across multiple steps and can adjust strategies, but requires human oversight.

⭐  Interesting Learning Based AI Game

🔗 https://quickdraw.withgoogle.com/

Portfolio Entry 19A : AI Characteristic Hunt – Round 1

For each scenario, identify the AI system type and highlight the characteristics that helped you decide.

| Scenario | AI System Type | Evidence / Characteristics |

| A login system blocks access after three failed password attempts. | Rule-Based | Follows a fixed rule, does not learn or predict |

| An AI composes new music that fits the atmosphere of a game scene. |  |  |

| An AI generates concept art for a game world from a short text description. |  |  |

| A system improves its accuracy at detecting spam emails as it processes more data. |  |  |

| A chatbot answers FAQs using a fixed list of scripted responses. |  |  |

| AI automatically prioritises bugs and creates tasks for developers |  |  |

| An AI playtests a game, finds bugs, suggests fixes, applies changes, and re-tests automatically. |  |  |

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

One task flowExample: “Test my game for bugs”

Different rolesExample: Design Agent + Test Agent + Launch Agent

Analyze: Single and Multi Agent AI

Would a single agent or a multi-agent system be better for launching your game? Why?

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

Agent goal (1 sentence): ______________________                          ( Eg. Help us find the top 3 launch risks before release)

Tools it would use (pick 2): Launch Brief / Playtest notes / Bug list/ Game rules checklist / Web (optional) / Code (optional)

Actions agent is permitted to do automatically:  1) ________ 2) _________3) ________

Human-only decisions (final judgement, ethics, creativity):: 1) ________ 2) _________3) ________

When an AI agent is NOT needed:  1) ___________       2) __________

Level:  ☐ Single-agent (one role) |  ☐ Multi-agent (specialists)

If the job is one clear goal + one data source → Single-agent

If it needs to handle different types of jobs at the same time (like bugs and balance and marketing)) → Multi-agent

Portfolio Entry 19B: AI Characteristic Hunt – Round 2

Pick ONE scenario from below and complete the “Agent Specification”

🚀 Launch Review Agent (checks readiness + risks from Launch Brief only)

🐞 Bug Sorting Agent (sorts bug reports + suggests priority order)

📋 Feedback Summariser Agent (turns playtest notes into themes + next actions)

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

If the job is one clear goal + one data source → Single-agent

If it needs to handle different types of jobs at the same time (like bugs and balance and marketing)) → Multi-agent

Portfolio Entry 19B: AI Characteristic Hunt – Round 2

Agent goal (1 sentence): ______________________                               ( Eg. Help us find the top 3 launch risks before release)

Tools it would use (pick 2): Launch Brief / Playtest notes / Bug list/ Game rules checklist / Web (optional) / Code (optional)

Actions agent is permitted to do automatically:

Human-only decisions (final judgement, ethics, creativity):

When an AI agent is NOT needed:

🚀 Launch Review Agent (checks readiness + risks from Launch Brief only)

🐞 Bug Sorting Agent (sorts bug reports + suggests priority order)

📋 Feedback Summariser Agent (turns playtest notes into themes + next actions)

Pick ONE scenario from below and complete the “Agent Specification”:

Would you trust either system to make a final launch decision?

Stretch Activity: AI Comparison Table

| Feature | Generative AI (Chatbots) | Agentic AI (AI Agents) |

| Main role | __________________ | __________________ |

| Responds or acts? | __________________ | __________________ |

| Can work across steps? | ☐ Yes ☐ No | ☐ Yes ☐ No |

| Uses tools automatically? | ☐ Yes ☐ No | ☐ Yes ☐ No |

| Needs human control? Why? | __________________ | __________________ |

| Example from game development | __________________ | __________________ |

Complete the table below to compare Generative AI tools and AI agents.

Reminder: If something goes wrong, humans are accountable — not AI.

Deepen: When to Use AI vs Human Judgment

AI is useful for speed, scale, and pattern detection.

Humans are essential for judgment, creativity, and accountability.

If the task needs values, meaning, or responsibility → a human decides.

If the task needs speed, scale, or pattern-finding → AI can help.

Activity 3.3: Task Allocation – AI or Human

Skill Boxes Key: 🟥 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟦 Communication

A developer wants a first draft of patch notes explaining bug fixes.☐ AI

Decide who should lead the task: AI, Human, or AI + Human

Justify your choice using the AI Decision Rule

A team has 200 playtest comments and wants to find common problems quickly.☐ AI

Justification: _______________________________________

A studio must decide whether a game update is fair and appropriate for younger players.☐ AI

Justification: _______________________________________

A final decision is needed on whether to delay a public game launch.☐ AI

Justification: _______________________________________

Justification: _______________________________________

Reflect: Agentic AI, Control & Responsibility

In this lesson, you learned that not all AI systems work the same way.

Rule-based systems, generative AI tools, and AI agents have different capabilities and limits.

AI agents can carry out multi-step tasks toward a goal, but humans must control scope, decisions, and accountability.

AI’s Role in My Game Project

Identify ONE type of AI from this lesson that could support your game.

Which type of AI was involved? ☐ Rule-Based AI ☐ Generative AI (Chatbot) ☐ Agentic AI (Multi-Step Helper)

What was this AI allowed to do in your game project? Describe the specific task(s) it supported and why this type of AI was suitable.

__________________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Humans Were Responsible For

What decisions stayed in human hands — and why?

Explain one decision in your project that:

Could not be trusted to an AI agent

________________________________________________

Required human judgment, values, creativity, or responsibility

________________________________________________

Final Accountability Statement:

If an AI agent made a mistake in my game project, the final responsibility belongs to the designer/team because ________________________________________________________________________________.”

Extension / Adaptive Task – Debug a Bad AI Agent

A student team created an AI Launch Review Agent.

After following its advice exactly, their game launch failed.

Players complained about unfair difficulty and unclear goals.

1️⃣ Identify the problem (choose 2):

Agent was given too much control

Human judgement was removed

The goal was unclear or too broad

The agent used the wrong data

The agent was used when it wasn’t needed

2️⃣ Rewrite ONE part of the agent design

Improve one of the following:

3️⃣ Accountability checkComplete this sentence:

“Even with AI agents, the final responsibility belongs to ______ because ______.”

The team says: “The AI told us this was the best decision.”

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

An AI system is set up to automatically test a game, report bugs, and re-test after fixes.

What best describes this system?

B. A rule-based calculator

Knowledge Area: The Nature of AI

Code: K1.4 (AI purposes and tasks)

Which task should always require human judgment, even when using AI agents?

A. Generating multiple design ideas

C. Deciding what changes align with the game’s vision

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles

Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

Welcome students and set the tone for critical thinking about AIEmphasize that this lesson is about making responsible choices, not just learning definitionsConnect to UAE's vision for responsible technology useUse the reflection question to gauge prior understanding and concernsCurriculum Alignment:UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-studyAQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specificationIB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

These criteria are measurable and observableEmphasize the importance of avoiding anthropomorphism - AI doesn't "think" or "feel"Students should be able to justify their decisions with evidence and reasoningConnect to OECD AI Literacy framework for credibilityCurriculum Alignment:UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-studyAQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specificationIB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

This is the core conceptual framework for the entire lesson.Use concrete examples for each type (calculator, ChatGPT, autonomous testing bot).Emphasize the key message: more sophisticated ≠ better choice.Check for understanding by asking students to classify additional examples.The three main types of AI systems are AI tools, Generative AI, and Agentic AI.AI tools are rule-based, perform specific tasks when activated, and have no decision-making capability. Examples include calculators and spell checkers.Generative AI can generate text, images, or code, but it responds to user instructions and has no autonomous goals. Examples include ChatGPT and DALL-E.Agentic AI has goals, plans, and autonomy. It pursues objectives independently, plans multiple steps, and uses tools and self-corrects. Examples include autonomous testing bots.The key message is that more powerful AI isn't always the best choice. Selecting the appropriate level of AI capability for each task is important for good design thinking.Curriculum Alignment:UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-studyAQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specificationIB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

This activity builds understanding of how different AI systems behave, not just what they are called.

Students must justify choices using specific characteristics from each scenario.

Example Characteristics to Prompt Students

Encourage students to quote or point to evidence in the scenario (e.g. “improves over time”, “automatically re-tests”).

AI prioritises bugs and creates developer tasks → Agentic AI: makes decisions and automates workflow actions.

Single vs Multi-Agent:“A single agent handles one clear goal and one task flow, like testing a game for bugs.A multi-agent system splits roles — for example, one agent tests, another balances gameplay, another prepares launch feedback.”

Guide students to link their choice to project size, tasks, and responsibility, not “AI being smarter.”

Remind students: Generative AI mainly responds, while AI agents can take actions and complete workflows.

Main role: Chatbot supports thinking/content; Agent supports automation/task completion.

Push students to say: Final launch decisions must stay human because AI can miss context, ethics, or real-world consequences.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment.Students choose All, Most, or Some, explain their choice, and record one improvement target.

Observers should see students articulating progress and next steps using accurate AI language.

Purpose (say briefly):This task checks whether students really understand responsibility, limits, and control when using AI agents — not just how to create one.

Encourage students to focus on why the agent failed, not blaming the AI

Teaching note: The questions here are to help students prepare for the end of unit assessment. These questions are practice only to be completed in Classkick at the end of each lesson

Teaching note: The questions here are to help students prepare for the end of unit assessment. These questions are practice only to be completed in Classkick at the end of each lesson

Teaching note: The questions here are to help students prepare for the end of unit assessment. These questions are practice only to be completed in Classkick at the end of each lesson.

This is the core conceptual framework for the entire lesson.Use concrete examples for each type (calculator, ChatGPT, autonomous testing bot).Emphasize the key message: more sophisticated ≠ better choice.Check for understanding by asking students to classify additional examples.The three main types of AI systems are AI tools, Generative AI, and Agentic AI.AI tools are rule-based, perform specific tasks when activated, and have no decision-making capability. Examples include calculators and spell checkers.Generative AI can generate text, images, or code, but it responds to user instructions and has no autonomous goals. Examples include ChatGPT and DALL-E.Agentic AI has goals, plans, and autonomy. It pursues objectives independently, plans multiple steps, and uses tools and self-corrects. Examples include autonomous testing bots.The key message is that more powerful AI isn't always the best choice. Selecting the appropriate level of AI capability for each task is important for good design thinking.Curriculum Alignment:UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-studyAQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specificationIB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

This activity builds understanding of how different AI systems behave, not just what they are called.

Students must justify choices using specific characteristics from each scenario.

Example Characteristics to Prompt Students

Encourage students to quote or point to evidence in the scenario (e.g. “improves over time”, “automatically re-tests”).

AI prioritises bugs and creates developer tasks → Agentic AI: makes decisions and automates workflow actions.

Single vs Multi-Agent:“A single agent handles one clear goal and one task flow, like testing a game for bugs.A multi-agent system splits roles — for example, one agent tests, another balances gameplay, another prepares launch feedback.”

Guide students to link their choice to project size, tasks, and responsibility, not “AI being smarter.”

Remind students: Generative AI mainly responds, while AI agents can take actions and complete workflows.

Main role: Chatbot supports thinking/content; Agent supports automation/task completion.

Push students to say: Final launch decisions must stay human because AI can miss context, ethics, or real-world consequences.

Observers should see students articulating progress and next steps using accurate AI language.

lessons.big_question: When does AI become an "agent"—and when is that unnecessary or irresponsible?
lessons.uae_link: The UAE leads in AI across smart cities, transport, education, and hospitals. Understanding AI systems is key for responsible nation-building. 💬 Reflection Question: Should AI always be allowed to act independently, or should humans remain in control? Why?
success_criteria: Accurate Explanation
Explain AI without saying it “thinks” like a human. AI works by finding patterns, not human reasoning
Justify AI Use Decisions
Justify whether an AI agent should be used or not, referencing limitations and human responsibility
Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.
I can explain the difference between AI types and avoid describing AI as thinking like a human.
I compared generative AI and agentic AI and justified when AI should or should not be used in game development.
I critically evaluated AI agents’ limitations and defended why final decisions must remain human-led.
