lessons.detailed_activities:
Starter: Same Prompt, Different Behavior

”Players are confused in Level 1 — improve the tutorial experience.”

In pairs, predict how each of the following would respond to the same prompt above.

What would ChatGPT generate?

What would a human developer decide?

What would an AI agent do if it could act repeatedly?

No definitions yet. Just predictions.

Today we learn: Agents don’t just respond — they can take actions

Explore: Types of AI Systems

Understanding the different types of AI helps you recognize their capabilities, limitations, and appropriate use cases. Each type serves different purposes and operates on distinct principles.

Follows fixed, predetermined instructions without adaptation. Examples include thermostats, spam filters, and system response mechanisms. Predictable but inflexible.

Learns patterns from data through machine learning algorithms. Can improve performance over time and recognize complex patterns humans might miss.

Creates new content including text, images, code, and audio. Responds to prompts and produces novel outputs based on training data patterns.

Works toward defined goals using multiple tools and rules. Operates across multiple steps and can adjust strategies, but requires human oversight.

⭐  Interesting Learning Based AI Game

🔗 https://quickdraw.withgoogle.com/

Portfolio Entry 19A : AI Characteristic Hunt – Round 1

For each scenario, identify the AI system type and highlight the characteristics that helped you decide.

| Scenario | AI System Type | Evidence / Characteristics |

| A login system blocks access after three failed password attempts. | Rule-Based | Follows a fixed rule, does not learn or predict |

| An AI composes new music that fits the atmosphere of a game scene. |  |  |

| An AI generates concept art for a game world from a short text description. |  |  |

| A system improves its accuracy at detecting spam emails as it processes more data. |  |  |

| A chatbot answers FAQs using a fixed list of scripted responses. |  |  |

| AI automatically prioritises bugs and creates tasks for developers |  |  |

| An AI playtests a game, finds bugs, suggests fixes, applies changes, and re-tests automatically. |  |  |

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

One task flowExample: “Test my game for bugs”

Different rolesExample: Design Agent + Test Agent + Launch Agent

Analyze: Single and Multi Agent AI

Would a single agent or a multi-agent system be better for launching your game? Why?

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

Agent goal (1 sentence): ______________________                          ( Eg. Help us find the top 3 launch risks before release)

Tools it would use (pick 2): Launch Brief / Playtest notes / Bug list/ Game rules checklist / Web (optional) / Code (optional)

Actions agent is permitted to do automatically:  1) ________ 2) _________3) ________

Human-only decisions (final judgement, ethics, creativity):: 1) ________ 2) _________3) ________

When an AI agent is NOT needed:  1) ___________       2) __________

Level:  ☐ Single-agent (one role) |  ☐ Multi-agent (specialists)

If the job is one clear goal + one data source → Single-agent

If it needs to handle different types of jobs at the same time (like bugs and balance and marketing)) → Multi-agent

Portfolio Entry 19B: AI Characteristic Hunt – Round 2

Pick ONE scenario from below and complete the “Agent Specification”

🚀 Launch Review Agent (checks readiness + risks from Launch Brief only)

🐞 Bug Sorting Agent (sorts bug reports + suggests priority order)

📋 Feedback Summariser Agent (turns playtest notes into themes + next actions)

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

If the job is one clear goal + one data source → Single-agent

If it needs to handle different types of jobs at the same time (like bugs and balance and marketing)) → Multi-agent

Portfolio Entry 19B: AI Characteristic Hunt – Round 2

Agent goal (1 sentence): ______________________                               ( Eg. Help us find the top 3 launch risks before release)

Tools it would use (pick 2): Launch Brief / Playtest notes / Bug list/ Game rules checklist / Web (optional) / Code (optional)

Actions agent is permitted to do automatically:

Human-only decisions (final judgement, ethics, creativity):

When an AI agent is NOT needed:

🚀 Launch Review Agent (checks readiness + risks from Launch Brief only)

🐞 Bug Sorting Agent (sorts bug reports + suggests priority order)

📋 Feedback Summariser Agent (turns playtest notes into themes + next actions)

Pick ONE scenario from below and complete the “Agent Specification”:

Would you trust either system to make a final launch decision?

Stretch Activity: AI Comparison Table

| Feature | Generative AI (Chatbots) | Agentic AI (AI Agents) |

| Main role | __________________ | __________________ |

| Responds or acts? | __________________ | __________________ |

| Can work across steps? | ☐ Yes ☐ No | ☐ Yes ☐ No |

| Uses tools automatically? | ☐ Yes ☐ No | ☐ Yes ☐ No |

| Needs human control? Why? | __________________ | __________________ |

| Example from game development | __________________ | __________________ |

Complete the table below to compare Generative AI tools and AI agents.

Reminder: If something goes wrong, humans are accountable — not AI.

Deepen: When to Use AI vs Human Judgment

AI is useful for speed, scale, and pattern detection.

Humans are essential for judgment, creativity, and accountability.

If the task needs values, meaning, or responsibility → a human decides.

If the task needs speed, scale, or pattern-finding → AI can help.

Activity 3.3: Task Allocation – AI or Human

Skill Boxes Key: 🟥 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟦 Communication

A developer wants a first draft of patch notes explaining bug fixes.☐ AI

Decide who should lead the task: AI, Human, or AI + Human

Justify your choice using the AI Decision Rule

A team has 200 playtest comments and wants to find common problems quickly.☐ AI

Justification: _______________________________________

A studio must decide whether a game update is fair and appropriate for younger players.☐ AI

Justification: _______________________________________

A final decision is needed on whether to delay a public game launch.☐ AI

Justification: _______________________________________

Justification: _______________________________________

Reflect: Agentic AI, Control & Responsibility

In this lesson, you learned that not all AI systems work the same way.

Rule-based systems, generative AI tools, and AI agents have different capabilities and limits.

AI agents can carry out multi-step tasks toward a goal, but humans must control scope, decisions, and accountability.

AI’s Role in My Game Project

Identify ONE type of AI from this lesson that could support your game.

Which type of AI was involved? ☐ Rule-Based AI ☐ Generative AI (Chatbot) ☐ Agentic AI (Multi-Step Helper)

What was this AI allowed to do in your game project? Describe the specific task(s) it supported and why this type of AI was suitable.

__________________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Humans Were Responsible For

What decisions stayed in human hands — and why?

Explain one decision in your project that:

Could not be trusted to an AI agent

________________________________________________

Required human judgment, values, creativity, or responsibility

________________________________________________

Final Accountability Statement:

If an AI agent made a mistake in my game project, the final responsibility belongs to the designer/team because ________________________________________________________________________________.”

Extension / Adaptive Task – Debug a Bad AI Agent

A student team created an AI Launch Review Agent.

After following its advice exactly, their game launch failed.

Players complained about unfair difficulty and unclear goals.

1️⃣ Identify the problem (choose 2):

Agent was given too much control

Human judgement was removed

The goal was unclear or too broad

The agent used the wrong data

The agent was used when it wasn’t needed

2️⃣ Rewrite ONE part of the agent design

Improve one of the following:

3️⃣ Accountability checkComplete this sentence:

“Even with AI agents, the final responsibility belongs to ______ because ______.”

The team says: “The AI told us this was the best decision.”

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

An AI system is set up to automatically test a game, report bugs, and re-test after fixes.

What best describes this system?

B. A rule-based calculator

Knowledge Area: The Nature of AI

Code: K1.4 (AI purposes and tasks)

Which task should always require human judgment, even when using AI agents?

A. Generating multiple design ideas

C. Deciding what changes align with the game’s vision

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

teacher_notes.notes: Welcome students and set the tone for critical thinking about AIEmphasize that this lesson is about making responsible choices, not just learning definitionsConnect to UAE's vision for responsible technology useUse the reflection question to gauge prior understanding and concernsCurriculum Alignment:UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-studyAQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specificationIB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

Our key question today: When does AI become an agent?
Agents act, not just respond.
We also consider when using an agent is unnecessary or irresponsible.
Keep this question in mind throughout the lesson.

Clearly state the two main learning objectivesEmphasize that understanding AI types is foundational for responsible useHighlight that decision-making skills are more important than memorizing definitionsPreview how these objectives connect to real-world applicationsCurriculum Alignment:Comparing rule-based and learning-based systems aligns with UK D&T KS3 1c (understanding control systems), AQA 3.1.3 (types of systems and limitations), IB Criterion C (justifying design choices), and CSTA 3A-CS-02 (comparing system abstractions and behaviours).

These criteria are measurable and observableEmphasize the importance of avoiding anthropomorphism - AI doesn't "think" or "feel"Students should be able to justify their decisions with evidence and reasoningConnect to OECD AI Literacy framework for credibilityCurriculum Alignment:UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-studyAQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specificationIB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

Today we explore how the same prompt can lead to different outcomes.
ChatGPT gives suggestions as a tool.
Humans make final design decisions.
AI agents could take repeated actions toward a goal.

AI tool – Learning based AI - https://quickdraw.withgoogle.com/
This is the core conceptual framework for the entire lesson.Use concrete examples for each type (calculator, ChatGPT, autonomous testing bot).Emphasize the key message: more sophisticated ≠ better choice.Check for understanding by asking students to classify additional examples.The three main types of AI systems are AI tools, Generative AI, and Agentic AI.AI tools are rule-based, perform specific tasks when activated, and have no decision-making capability. Examples include calculators and spell checkers.Generative AI can generate text, images, or code, but it responds to user instructions and has no autonomous goals. Examples include ChatGPT and DALL-E.Agentic AI has goals, plans, and autonomy. It pursues objectives independently, plans multiple steps, and uses tools and self-corrects. Examples include autonomous testing bots.The key message is that more powerful AI isn't always the best choice. Selecting the appropriate level of AI capability for each task is important for good design thinking.Curriculum Alignment:UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-studyAQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specificationIB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

This activity builds understanding of how different AI systems behave, not just what they are called.
Students must justify choices using specific characteristics from each scenario.
Example Characteristics to Prompt Students
Rule-Based System
Follows fixed IF–THEN rules
Does not learn or adapt
Same input always gives the same output
Learning-Based AI
Improves accuracy over time
Uses data and feedback
Performance changes as more examples are processed
Generative AI
Creates new content (text, images, music, code)
Responds to prompts rather than rules
Outputs are not pre-written or scripted
Agentic AI
Completes multi-step tasks autonomously
Makes decisions and takes actions without constant human input
Monitors outcomes and adjusts behaviour
Encourage students to quote or point to evidence in the scenario (e.g. “improves over time”, “automatically re-tests”).


Answer Key. 
A login system blocks access after three failed attempts → Rule-Based (Not AI): fixed security rule, no learning.
An AI creates background music to match a game mood → Generative AI: creates new music from prompts.
An AI generates concept art from a text description → Generative AI: creates new images from prompts.
A spam filter improves accuracy as it processes more data → Machine Learning AI: learns and improves over time.
A chatbot answers FAQs using scripted responses → Rule-Based (Not AI): pre-written keyword responses.
AI prioritises bugs and creates developer tasks → Agentic AI: makes decisions and automates workflow actions.
An AI playtests, fixes bugs, and re-tests automatically → Agentic AI: full autonomous test–fix–repeat loop.

Top section:“Generative AI responds to prompts — it gives answers, ideas, or text, but it doesn’t act on its own and can sound confident even when it’s wrong.Agentic AI is goal-driven — it can plan steps, use tools, and work toward an outcome, but it still needs human oversight.”
Key distinction to stress:“Tools answer questions. Agents try to achieve goals.”
Single vs Multi-Agent:“A single agent handles one clear goal and one task flow, like testing a game for bugs.A multi-agent system splits roles — for example, one agent tests, another balances gameplay, another prepares launch feedback.”
Big takeaway:“As agents get more capable, human responsibility increases, not decreases. You always make the final decision.”

Discussion Activity

There is no single correct answer — focus on reasoning, not the choice.
Single agent suits small projects: one goal, simpler setup, lower cost, easier control.
Multi-agent systems suit complex launches: different agents for testing, marketing, analytics, and updates.
Guide students to link their choice to project size, tasks, and responsibility, not “AI being smarter.”
Emphasise that humans remain responsible for final decisions, even when agents assist.

Remind students: Generative AI mainly responds, while AI agents can take actions and complete workflows.
Guide them to key distinctions:
Chatbots = answer, suggest, create content when asked.
Agents = plan, execute steps, use tools, and work toward a goal.
Expected answers:
Main role: Chatbot supports thinking/content; Agent supports automation/task completion.
Responds or acts: Chatbot responds; Agent acts.
Across steps: Chatbot usually one-step; Agent can manage multi-step processes.
Tools automatically: Chatbot rarely; Agents often can.
Human control: Both need oversight, but agents especially because they can trigger real actions.
Game dev examples:
Chatbot = generate dialogue or brainstorm ideas.
Agent = triage bugs, prepare launch checklist, schedule updates.
Discussion Prompt Support
Push students to say: Final launch decisions must stay human because AI can miss context, ethics, or real-world consequences.

AI does not think like a human — it predicts patterns from data.
Believing it “thinks” can lead to overtrust and mistakes.
AI can be wrong, biased, or misleading if not checked.
Responsibility stays with humans: designers, organizations, and users.
Key message: AI supports decisions, but people are accountable.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment.Students choose All, Most, or Some, explain their choice, and record one improvement target.
Ask:
“What will you do differently next time?”
Observers should see students articulating progress and next steps using accurate AI language.

Purpose (say briefly):This task checks whether students really understand responsibility, limits, and control when using AI agents — not just how to create one.
Set the scene (30 sec):“Imagine a real studio scenario. The team followed an AI agent’s advice exactly… and the launch failed.”
Key message to emphasise:AI agents can support decisions, but they do not replace human judgement.
During the task:
Encourage students to focus on why the agent failed, not blaming the AI
Listen for phrases like:
“The agent had too much control”
“Humans didn’t review the decision”
“The goal was too vague”
Prompt if groups get stuck:
“What should the agent not be allowed to do?”
“Where should a human step back in?”

slides.content:
Term 3

Lesson 3

‹#›

Lesson 3: Introduction to Agentic AI

Understanding AI, and human oversight

‹#›

UAE Link: The UAE leads in AI across smart cities, transport, education, and hospitals. Understanding AI systems is key for responsible nation-building.
💬 Reflection Question: Should AI always be allowed to act independently, or should humans remain in control? Why?

In this lesson, you will:

Compare AI tools, generative AI, and AI agents
Learn how AI agents work toward goals across multiple steps
Decide when using an AI agent is useful, unnecessary, or risky

By the end of the lesson, you will understand what AI can and cannot do, and where human judgment is required.

Want to learn more about exactly how agentic AI can works ? Check out this video  🔗 Agentic AI in Healthcare

Big Question / Big Picture

AI & Game Development

‹#›

When does AI become an "agent"—and when is that unnecessary or irresponsible?

Lesson 3: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Understand Types of AIExplain the difference between rule-based, learning-based AI, generative AI, and agentic AI, including what each type can and cannot do.

Make Responsible AI Decisions
Apply understanding of AI systems to decide when AI tools or agents should or should not be used.

‹#›

We will know we are successful when we can …

Lesson 3: Success Criteria – Introduction to Agentic AI

Accurate Explanation
Explain AI without saying it “thinks” like a human. AI works by finding patterns, not human reasoning

Justify AI Use Decisions
Justify whether an AI agent should be used or not, referencing limitations and human responsibility

Starter: Same Prompt, Different Behavior

‹#›

Prompt Example
”Players are confused in Level 1 — improve the tutorial experience.”

Mini Task

In pairs, predict how each of the following would respond to the same prompt above.
What would ChatGPT generate?
What would a human developer decide?
What would an AI agent do if it could act repeatedly?
No definitions yet. Just predictions.

Today we learn: Agents don’t just respond — they can take actions

Explore: Types of AI Systems

‹#›

Understanding the different types of AI helps you recognize their capabilities, limitations, and appropriate use cases. Each type serves different purposes and operates on distinct principles.

Rule-Based Systems

Follows fixed, predetermined instructions without adaptation. Examples include thermostats, spam filters, and system response mechanisms. Predictable but inflexible.

Learning-Based AI

Learns patterns from data through machine learning algorithms. Can improve performance over time and recognize complex patterns humans might miss.

Generative AI

Creates new content including text, images, code, and audio. Responds to prompts and produces novel outputs based on training data patterns.

Agentic AI

Works toward defined goals using multiple tools and rules. Operates across multiple steps and can adjust strategies, but requires human oversight.

⭐  Interesting Learning Based AI Game 
🔗 https://quickdraw.withgoogle.com/

Portfolio Entry 19A : AI Characteristic Hunt – Round 1

For each scenario, identify the AI system type and highlight the characteristics that helped you decide.

Rule-Based

Learning-Based AI

Generative AI

Agentic AI


| Scenario | AI System Type | Evidence / Characteristics |
| --- | --- | --- |
| A login system blocks access after three failed password attempts. | Rule-Based | Follows a fixed rule, does not learn or predict |
| An AI composes new music that fits the atmosphere of a game scene. |  |  |
| An AI generates concept art for a game world from a short text description. |  |  |
| A system improves its accuracy at detecting spam emails as it processes more data. |  |  |
| A chatbot answers FAQs using a fixed list of scripted responses. |  |  |
| AI automatically prioritises bugs and creates tasks for developers |  |  |
| An AI playtests a game, finds bugs, suggests fixes, applies changes, and re-tests automatically. |  |  |

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

‹#›

Analyze: Generative AI vs Agentic AI

Generative AI and agentic AI operate fundamentally differently, each with unique limitations that teams must consider when designing systems and setting user expectations.

Generative AI (Chatbots)

🗨  Responds to prompts
🎲 Can sound confident but be wrong
❌ Cannot act on its own

Common use case:
Marketing copy, game dialogue, brainstorming—where human review is standard.

Agentic AI (AI Agents)

🎯  Works toward goals
🧰  Uses tools + multiple steps
⚠️ Still needs human control

Common use case:
Playtesting levels, checking bugs, balancing difficulty, preparing launch checklist

‹#›

Stretch: New EndStar in Game Agent

Check out the new Endstar in-game chat agent — it can help you code features, design improvements, debug issues, generate ideas, and give smart gameplay tips in real time.

🧩 Single and Multi-Agent

Single Agent
One goal
One task flowExample: “Test my game for bugs”

Multi-Agent System
Multiple goals
Different rolesExample: Design Agent + Test Agent + Launch Agent

Analyze: Single and Multi Agent AI

Discussion Prompt

Would a single agent or a multi-agent system be better for launching your game? Why?

‹#›

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

Agent Specification 

Agent goal (1 sentence): ______________________                          ( Eg. Help us find the top 3 launch risks before release)
Tools it would use (pick 2): Launch Brief / Playtest notes / Bug list/ Game rules checklist / Web (optional) / Code (optional)
Actions agent is permitted to do automatically:  1) ________ 2) _________3) ________
Human-only decisions (final judgement, ethics, creativity):: 1) ________ 2) _________3) ________
When an AI agent is NOT needed:  1) ___________       2) __________
Level:  ☐ Single-agent (one role) |  ☐ Multi-agent (specialists)

Single vs Multi Agent
If the job is one clear goal + one data source → Single-agent
If it needs to handle different types of jobs at the same time (like bugs and balance and marketing)) → Multi-agent

Portfolio Entry 19B: AI Characteristic Hunt – Round 2

Group Task
Pick ONE scenario from below and complete the “Agent Specification”
Scenarios
🚀 Launch Review Agent (checks readiness + risks from Launch Brief only)
🐞 Bug Sorting Agent (sorts bug reports + suggests priority order)
📋 Feedback Summariser Agent (turns playtest notes into themes + next actions)

‹#›

‹#›

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking |

Agent Specification

Single vs Multi Agent
If the job is one clear goal + one data source → Single-agent
If it needs to handle different types of jobs at the same time (like bugs and balance and marketing)) → Multi-agent

Single-agent (one role) 
Multi-agent (specialists)

🐞 Bug Sorting Agent (sorts bug reports + suggests priority order)
📋 Feedback Summariser Agent (turns playtest notes into themes + next actions)

Pick ONE scenario from below and complete the “Agent Specification”:

Discussion
Would you trust either system to make a final launch decision?

Stretch Activity: AI Comparison Table


| Feature | Generative AI (Chatbots) | Agentic AI (AI Agents) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Main role | __________________ | __________________ |
| Responds or acts? | __________________ | __________________ |
| Can work across steps? | ☐ Yes ☐ No | ☐ Yes ☐ No |
| Uses tools automatically? | ☐ Yes ☐ No | ☐ Yes ☐ No |
| Needs human control? Why? | __________________ | __________________ |
| Example from game development | __________________ | __________________ |

Complete the table below to compare Generative AI tools and AI agents.

‹#›

Deepen: AI Doesn’t “Think” Like Us

AI systems do not think, feel, understand, or make decisions like humans.

Discussion Questions
What could go wrong if people believe AI is thinking like a human?
Who is responsible for AI outputs — the system or the humans using it?

What AI is:
A system that follows rules
Trained on data and examples
Produces outputs by matching patterns

❌ Inaccurate Language
"The AI thinks this answer is correct"
"The agent decided to prioritize speed"
"The system understands player behavior"
"The AI knows what users expect”

✅ Accurate Language
"The AI generated output based on training patterns"
“The system found patterns in player data.”
“The AI followed the instructions it was given
"The model was trained on user interaction data"

‹#›

Reminder: If something goes wrong, humans are accountable — not AI.

Deepen: When to Use AI vs Human Judgment

AI is useful for speed, scale, and pattern detection.
Humans are essential for judgment, creativity, and accountability.

If the task needs values, meaning, or responsibility → a human decides.
If the task needs speed, scale, or pattern-finding → AI can help.

🔑 AI Decision Rule

‹#›

‹#›

Activity 3.3: Task Allocation – AI or Human

Skill Boxes Key: 🟥 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟦 Communication

A developer wants a first draft of patch notes explaining bug fixes.☐ AI 
☐ Human 
☐ AI + Human

For each scenario below:
Decide who should lead the task: AI, Human, or AI + Human
Justify your choice using the AI Decision Rule

Scenarios

A team has 200 playtest comments and wants to find common problems quickly.☐ AI 
☐ Human 
☐ AI + Human

Justification: _______________________________________

A studio must decide whether a game update is fair and appropriate for younger players.☐ AI 
☐ Human 
☐ AI + Human

Justification: _______________________________________

A final decision is needed on whether to delay a public game launch.☐ AI 
☐ Human 
☐ AI + Human

Justification: _______________________________________

Justification: _______________________________________

Reflect: Agentic AI, Control & Responsibility

In this lesson, you learned that not all AI systems work the same way.
Rule-based systems, generative AI tools, and AI agents have different capabilities and limits.
AI agents can carry out multi-step tasks toward a goal, but humans must control scope, decisions, and accountability.

AI’s Role in My Game Project
Identify ONE type of AI from this lesson that could support your game. 
Which type of AI was involved? ☐ Rule-Based AI ☐ Generative AI (Chatbot) ☐ Agentic AI (Multi-Step Helper)
What was this AI allowed to do in your game project? Describe the specific task(s) it supported and why this type of AI was suitable.
__________________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Humans Were Responsible For

What decisions stayed in human hands — and why?
Explain one decision in your project that:
Could not be trusted to an AI agent
          ________________________________________________
Required human judgment, values, creativity, or responsibility
          ________________________________________________

Final Accountability Statement:  
If an AI agent made a mistake in my game project, the final responsibility belongs to the designer/team because ________________________________________________________________________________.”

‹#›

Reflect: Curiosity & Responsibility in AI Use

‹#›

Reflect on how AI helped your thinking today and what decisions you were responsible for.

What surprised me about AI or AI agents today?                                                                     (e.g. what agents can/can’t do, rule-based vs learning-based AI)
Who is responsible when an AI agent makes a mistake? Why?                                                (e.g. the student, the designer, the prompt writer, not the AI itself)
Why is it misleading to say an AI agent “thinks” or “decides                                                          (What is it actually doing instead?)

My Reflection:

Write 2–3 sentences explaining what AI helped with and what decisions you were responsible for.

✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:

Today I was surprised that AI…

I am responsible for…

🤖  AI Literacy Reflection

I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI
I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away
I used my own judgement to make decisions

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I can explain the difference between AI types and avoid describing AI as thinking like a human.

Most

I compared generative AI and agentic AI and justified when AI should or should not be used in game development.

Some

I critically evaluated AI agents’ limitations and defended why final decisions must remain human-led.

Extension / Adaptive Task – Debug a Bad AI Agent

Scenario 
A student team created an AI Launch Review Agent.
After following its advice exactly, their game launch failed.
Players complained about unfair difficulty and unclear goals.

In pairs or small groups:

1️⃣ Identify the problem (choose 2):
Agent was given too much control
Human judgement was removed
The goal was unclear or too broad
The agent used the wrong data
The agent was used when it wasn’t needed

2️⃣ Rewrite ONE part of the agent design
Improve one of the following:
Agent goal
Allowed actions
Human-only decisions

3️⃣ Accountability checkComplete this sentence:
“Even with AI agents, the final responsibility belongs to ______ because ______.”

The team says: “The AI told us this was the best decision.”

‹#›

Lesson 3 – Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 3.1

An AI system is set up to automatically test a game, report bugs, and re-test after fixes.

What best describes this system?

A. A generative AI tool

B. A rule-based calculator

C. An AI agent

D. A database

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: The Nature of AI
Code: K1.4 (AI purposes and tasks)

‹#›

Which task should always require human judgment, even when using AI agents?

A. Generating multiple design ideas

B. Running repeated tests

C. Deciding what changes align with the game’s vision

D. Sorting bug reports

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 3.2

Lesson 3 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

‹#›

Which statement describes AI accurately and avoids anthropomorphism?

A. “The AI understands what players want”

B. “The AI decides what is best for the game”

C. “The AI follows patterns to generate outputs”

D. “The AI feels when something is wrong”

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 3.3

Lesson 3 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Role in Society
Code: K1.3 / Competence 2.5 (Avoid anthropomorphism.)

ai_focus: Lesson 4: AI Workspace Setup
Today's Focus: Controlling Your AI Agent
How to set up and manage an AI workspace
Lesson 4 focuses on building a controlled AI workspace and learning how to manage an AI agent responsibly.
Lesson 4: Success Criteria – AI Workspace Setup
NotebookLM is an AI workspace that only uses the documents you upload. It helps you work with your own sources instead of guessing from the internet.
NotebookLM is not a chatbot  - it behaves more like an AI agent working inside your uploaded documents.
System settings are your primary tool for shaping how AI interprets and responds to your data.
Human Decision - 4 MUST-PASS Criteria

lessons.big_question: How can we control what AI knows and what it produces?
lessons.uae_link: The UAE is investing heavily in AI across government, schools, and healthcare, with a national focus on trusted data and clear governance - showing why well-structured inputs, instructions, and evidence are essential for safe, reliable AI systems. Reflection Question: What could go wrong if AI is given incorrect or incomplete information? Consider implications for student learning, business decisions, or public communication. Lesson 4 focuses on building a controlled AI workspace and learning how to manage an AI agent responsibly. Students will understand that strong AI performance depends on how well the workspace is structured and governed. Introduce the three key components that determine AI reliability: Inputs, Instructions, and Outputs. Explain that AI can only respond based on what it is given, so poor inputs lead to poor or misleading results. Highlight that clear instructions reduce confusion and help keep the agent within safe boundaries. Outputs must be checked by humans, with evidence and reasoning, not accepted automatically. Connect to the UAE’s national AI strategy, where trusted data and governance are essential in high-impact sectors like healthcare and education. Use the reflection question to prompt critical thinking: incorrect or incomplete information can lead to serious consequences in learning, business, or public communication.

Students will understand that strong AI performance depends on how well the workspace is structured and governed.

Highlight that clear instructions reduce confusion and help keep the agent within safe boundaries.
Outputs must be checked by humans, with evidence and reasoning, not accepted automatically.

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 4

Lesson 4: AI Workspace Setup

‹#›

Today's Focus: Controlling Your AI Agent
How to set up and manage an AI workspace 
Three fundamental components that determine the quality and reliability of AI outputs:
Inputs
Instructions
Outputs

UAE Link: The UAE is investing heavily in AI across government, schools, and healthcare, with a national focus on trusted data and clear governance - showing why well-structured inputs, instructions, and evidence are essential for safe, reliable AI systems.
Reflection Question: What could go wrong if AI is given incorrect or incomplete information? Consider implications for student learning, business decisions, or public communication.

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How can we control what AI knows and what it produces?

Lesson 4: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Directing AI Outputs
Learn how to control AI behaviour using instructions and settings to produce accurate, useful responses for your purpose..

Managing AI Inputs
Learn how to upload, link, and organise sources in NotebookLM, and understand what information the AI can access.

Data Control
I can upload and link multiple sources in NotebookLM, and explain what information the AI can and cannot access.

Lesson 4: Success Criteria – AI Workspace Setup

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Responsible Output
I can guide the AI to produce clear, accurate notes and choose the best response format for my task.

Starter: Who’s Responsible When AI Is Wrong?

‹#›

Starter Discussion
An AI gives a confident answer that turns out to be wrong. What do you think caused the problem?
The AI system itself
The information it was given
The way the question was asked
A human trusting it too quickly

🤝 Think–Pair–Share 
ThinkChoose ONE cause above you think is most likely.
PairGive one real example from school or game design(e.g. homework answers, summaries, or game suggestions).
ShareBe ready to explain your reasoning to the class.

Many AI mistakes come from poor data or unclear instructions — not the AI “thinking” wrong.

Explore : Notebook LM as a Workspace

NotebookLM is an AI workspace that only uses the documents you upload. It helps you work with your own sources instead of guessing from the internet.

NotebookLM is not a chatbot  - it behaves more like an AI agent working inside your uploaded documents.

Controlled Access

AI can ONLY use documents you upload—no internet browsing, no external databases.

No Guessing

NotebookLM is less likely to make things up because it stays focused on your uploaded sources.

Source-Based - Citations

All responses can be traced back to specific uploaded documents

⚠️

📚

🔒

1

Upload Your Documents

Your Launch Brief
Screenshots or photos of your game
Project Portfolio’s (Term 2, Term 3)
Playtest feedback, bug lists etc.

2

Analyze Access + Gaps

Identify what information the AI can now access from these sources
What did we forget to upload?

📌 Notebook LM does not support Powerpoint files, save them as a PDF first.  See this 🔗video tutorial

Activity 4.1: Convert +Load the Data

Navigate to 🔗Notebook LM and create a Notebook for your Game Development Project 🡪

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Problem Solving

3

Convert your Documents to PDF

Powerpoint files are not accepted. 
Save your files as PDF files first.
Video Demonstration - Converting and loading files

Analyze: Configure System Settings

System settings are your primary tool for shaping how AI interprets and responds to your data. 
These configurations act as instructions that define the AI's role, communication style, and boundaries.

Role Assignment

Define the AI’s job: reviewer, summariser, launch agent, bug checker.

Tone Configuration

Set communication style: neutral and objective, simple and accessible, or structured and formal

Operational Limits

Set boundaries so the agent stays reliable: Use only the uploaded documents, do not guess or invent details, flag missing information

🧠 Core Understanding: Instructions shape behavior. The more specific and clear your system settings, the more reliable and appropriate your AI outputs will be. Vague instructions lead to unpredictable results.
Video Demo- Configuring Agent Settings

Activity 4.2: Create Launch Review Agent

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking

Your Task
You will turn your Launch Brief into an AI Launch Review Agent using Notebook LM.
Your agent will help with Design Review, QA / Test Review, and Launch Readiness Review

Agent Role Template
You are a Launch Review Agent.Your role is to evaluate this game’s launch readiness.Only use the information in the uploaded Launch Brief.Flag risks, unclear goals, and missing details.
Flag possible bias or assumptions in the brief.Do not invent features or make decisions.

Steps
1️⃣ Upload your 🔗 Launch Brief into 🔗 NotebookLM2️⃣ Configure the agent role using the Agent Role Template →
3️⃣ Ask the agent to review your launch plan

Human Decision - 4 MUST-PASS Criteria    
✅ All 4 pass → GO (Clean Launch)       ⚠ Any fail → CONDITIONAL Launch                
① Game is playable start to finish② No game-breaking bugs③ Core mechanic works as intended④ Game has been tested by at least one person

Justification (1–2 sentences):Explain one place where you agreed or disagreed with the agent — and why.
__________________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Critical Thinking

Your Task

4️⃣ Ask the agent: "Review my launch plan"
5️⃣ In the Agent Chat, answer the 4 final questions and save the output as a note + source

Need help? Follow this video tutorial 🎥

Portfolio Entry 20: Launch Decision + Agent Findings

Add the main findings from your Notebook LM Launch Review Agent below:

Human Launch Decision    
✅ All 4 pass → GO (Clean Launch)       
⚠ Any fail → CONDITIONAL Launch                
Game is playable start to finish
No game-breaking bugs
Core mechanic works as intended
Game has been tested by at least one person
🚦 Launch Decision (Choose One)
🚀 GO Launch — Ready to release
⚠️ Conditional Launch — Release with disclaimer
Why? (1 sentence): ______________________________________

Deepen: Linking Files

One of NotebookLM's most powerful features is the ability to work with multiple sources simultaneously. However, this capability introduces new considerations for accuracy and interpretation.

What File Linking Enables

Multiple Sources

Process information from various documents simultaneously

Cross-Reference

Compare information across sources to identify patterns or agreements

Gap Analysis

Spot missing information, contradictions, or inconsistencies between documents

Human Responsibility

⚠ Important: Linking files does NOT mean the AI understands your project.

🔍 Mini Example 
Launch Brief says: “Game is ready.”Bug List shows: “Level 3 crashes.”NotebookLM can flag the conflict — but you decide what to fix first.

NotebookLM helps you connect documents. You are responsible for interpreting them.
Video Demo - Linking Files for Cross Reference

Activity 4.3: Multi-Source Review Agent

You will use an AI review agent to check how closely your final game matches your original plan.

Upload to a Workspace of Notebook LM
 Initial  🔗Design Brief

One improvement you would still make before launch

Agent Instructions
You are a Development Review Agent.
Only use the uploaded documents.
What matched the  original design brief plan

What still needs work before launch
Do not invent features or assumptions.

Portfolio Entry 21: Gap Analysis

___________________________________________________________________________________

Compare the initial game Design Brief to your current development progress.

One improvement you would still make before launch

___________________________________________________________________________________

Deepen : Converting sources to notes

NotebookLM helps you move between your documents and your thinking.
Upload trusted sources (briefs, portfolios, bug lists).
Turn key information into organized notes.
Every note stays connected to the original document.

Notes are faster to use, but sources keep them trustworthy.

To store a useful chat, save it as a note

All notes can be viewed on the right panel, for quick access

Notes can be also converted to a source and other formats

Extension Task: Output Formats in Notebook LM

Notebook LM can output information in multiple formats such as :
Summaries / reports, 
Structured notes and quizzes
Bullet lists, comparison tables,
Timelines
Audio/video-style overviews

Mini Task: Video Generation

Upload your project portfolio to NotebookLM.


Select Video Overview  →



Select a “brief” video overview. →



 Add the prompt for the video →

Example Prompt
Using only the information in my uploaded portfolio, generate a 1-minute video-style summary that explains my game project, the key features, the main improvements made, and why it is ready for launch
Video Demonstration – Selecting Output Formats

Reflect: Controlling AI, Owning Decisions

In a real game studio, AI can support the work — but humans own the decisions.This reflection asks you to justify how you used AI, what you controlled, and what you chose not to let AI decide.

Write 2–3 sentences for each prompt below to show how your workspace setup shaped AI behaviour - and where human responsibility remained.

What Enabled AI
Because I uploaded ___________________, the AI was able to ______________________________.
Example: "Because I uploaded detailed player personas, the AI was able to suggest features that matched their interests and skill levels."

What Limited the AI
Because _____________ was missing or unclear, the AI output _____________________________.                                   
Example: "Because budget constraints weren't specified, the AI output included expensive features that weren't realistic."

Your Decision
One decision I still had to make myself was: ___________________ because __________________________.
What choice required your unique judgment, values, or understanding of the game  that an AI system could not provide?

1

2

3

📌Each response must include a reason or example from your own game.

Reflect: AI Control & Responsibility

‹#›

Think about how AI helped you today and what choices you had to make yourself.

What did I learn about how AI is limited by the data and instructions it is given?     (e.g. what happens if data is missing, unclear, or poorly structured)
What decisions was I responsible for when using AI in this lesson — and why?                     (e.g. choosing sources, checking outputs, deciding what to trust or change)
Why is it misleading to say an AI agent “understands” or “decides”?(What is the AI actually doing instead?)

My Reflection:

Write 2–3 sentences describing the AI’s role and your role.

✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:

Today I was surprised that AI…

I am responsible for…

🤖  AI Literacy Reflection

I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI
I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away
I used my own judgement to make decisions

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I  can upload and organise sources in NotebookLM and explain what information the AI can access.

Most

I can configure AI instructions and system settings to guide accurate, source-based outputs.

Some

I can evaluate how input quality, missing data, and instructions affect AI reliability and justify human accountability for final decisions.

Lesson 4 – Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 4.1

If NotebookLM gives an incorrect answer, what is the MOST likely cause?

A. The AI is broken or malfunctioning

B. The AI searched the internet for additional information

C. The AI was given poor, incomplete, or missing data

D. The AI deliberately ignored its instructions

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: The Nature of AI
Code: K1.3 (AI generates outputs based on training/data, not understanding)

Why should AI-generated summaries always be checked against original sources?

A. AI is always biased and cannot be trusted

B. Summaries necessarily remove context and can change meaning

C. AI truly understands meaning and makes perfect choices

D. Shorter text is always worse quality than longer text

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 4.2

Lesson 4– Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: The Nature of AI
Code: K1.4 (AI only works within the tasks and data humans define.)

When using NotebookLM as an AI agent, why is it important to upload only trusted and relevant documents?

A. Because AI always knows which information is correct

B. Because the agent’s output depends on the training data it was built with

C. Because the AI will reflect the sources you provide, including any bias or errors

D. Because shorter documents produce better answers

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 4.3

Lesson 4 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reflects Human Choices
Code: K2.2 (training data) + K2.5 (bias)

Endless Studios Session

Let’s Build. Create. Experiment.Make.You Choose.

Jump into Endstar
Build and experiment
Test with your team
Fix bugs together
Try new ideas
Explore AI tools
Complete unfinished content
Choose how you want to work

Schools with 2 lessons/week: this can be self-directed learning from home. Schools with 3 lessons/week: teachers may run this as an open studio build sessions.

Your game's official name—keep it clear and memorable
The short phrase you selected/edited from Step 1
AI decides what is okay to promise

ai_focus: Emphasize honest marketing and responsible AI use
Deepen: AI in Marketing – Help or Risk?
AI tools like Gemini can speed up marketing writing and generate creative ideas.
But AI does not understand ethics, player trust, or long-term reputation.
Which of the following best describes your responsibility when using AI for marketing?
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
Code: K4.1 (AI supports drafting, not decisions)
learning_objectives: Apply Ethical Judgment: Evaluate marketing language to ensure it is accurate, fair, and does not overpromise beyond the game’s actual experience.
Structured Launch Announcement: Create a clear launch announcement using a provided template that communicates what the game is and who it is for.

metadata.activity_description:
Starter: Launch Decision Recap

In Lesson 4 , you made a Human Launch Decision- deciding whether your game was ready for a clean launch or required a conditional launch with disclaimers.

Mini Task: Launch Decision Reminder

Before creating your launch announcement, complete this quick check:

My Lesson 4 launch decision: ☐ 🚀 Clean Launch  ☐ ⚠️ Conditional Launch

If you have “Conditional Launch”, what are the known issues:

_______________________________________

ONE claim my announcement must be honest about:

(Even a clean launch has limits — what should you be careful not to overclaim?)

_______________________________________

_______________________________________

Remember: AI can help write — you are responsible for what is promised.

Explore: What Is a Launch Announcement?

A launch announcement is not just advertising - it’s a promise to players.It shapes what players expect before they ever play your game.

Have you ever played a game that wasn’t what the trailer promised?

Key purposes of a launch announcement

✅ Tell players what your game truly offers

✅ Attract the right audience

✅ Highlight real features (not imagined ones)

✅ Build trust through honest marketing

Why do players lose trust when marketing exaggerates features? Think about games you've been excited about that didn't deliver on their promises. How did that feel?

Explore: Game Tagline Importance

A tagline is a short, memorable phrase that captures the core idea or feeling of a game.

🎯 Grab attention quickly — Hooks players instantly

🧠 Boost memorability — Makes the game easy to remember

🎮 Show core experience — Signals what gameplay feels like

📢 Strengthen marketing — Helps the game stand out

⚠️ A tagline must match the real game. Do not promise features or experiences your game doesn’t deliver.

⚖️ Your announcement must match what your game actually delivers.

Activity 5.1 Launch Announcement Agent

You will now create an honest, professional launch announcement using AI responsibly.

Notebook LM will help draft your launch announcement by using your Launch Brief and the provided agent template

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟦 Communication |🟪 Creativity

Open your  previous Launch Review Agent Notebook in Notebook LM .

Paste the 🔗Launch Announcement Agent Prompt into Notebook LM’s chat

Choose one of the generated taglines in Notebook LM ( or create your own)

Review the ethical check for honesty and accuracy

Review and save the final announcement draft as a note in Notebook LM.

Need help? Follow this video tutorial 🎥

Activity 5.1: Step 1 -  AI-Supported Tagline Drafting

A tagline is a short, memorable phrase that captures your game's essence.

It should be catchy but also honest and accurate.

Copy the prompt below and replace the bracketed placeholders with your game's details from Launch Brief

Ask the AI to generate 3 tagline options

Keep only taglines that match real gameplay.

Cross out anything that overpromises.

Select ONE tagline (you may edit it to better fit your game)

Skill Boxes Key: 🟪 Creativity | 🟩 Problem Solving 🟧 Computational Thinking

Write 3 short taglines (under 10 words each) for a [GENRE] game called [GAME TITLE] where [UNIQUE FACTOR]. The target audience is [PLAYER TYPE] aged [AGE RANGE]."

📌 AI suggests, humans decide. You are responsible for the accuracy and ethics of your final announcement.

lessons.description_of_activities:
Starter: Launch Decision Recap

In Lesson 4 , you made a Human Launch Decision- deciding whether your game was ready for a clean launch or required a conditional launch with disclaimers.

Mini Task: Launch Decision Reminder

Before creating your launch announcement, complete this quick check:

My Lesson 4 launch decision: ☐ 🚀 Clean Launch  ☐ ⚠️ Conditional Launch

If you have “Conditional Launch”, what are the known issues:

_______________________________________

ONE claim my announcement must be honest about:

(Even a clean launch has limits — what should you be careful not to overclaim?)

_______________________________________

_______________________________________

Remember: AI can help write — you are responsible for what is promised.

Explore: What Is a Launch Announcement?

A launch announcement is not just advertising - it’s a promise to players.It shapes what players expect before they ever play your game.

Have you ever played a game that wasn’t what the trailer promised?

Key purposes of a launch announcement

✅ Tell players what your game truly offers

✅ Attract the right audience

✅ Highlight real features (not imagined ones)

✅ Build trust through honest marketing

Why do players lose trust when marketing exaggerates features? Think about games you've been excited about that didn't deliver on their promises. How did that feel?

Explore: Game Tagline Importance

A tagline is a short, memorable phrase that captures the core idea or feeling of a game.

🎯 Grab attention quickly — Hooks players instantly

🧠 Boost memorability — Makes the game easy to remember

🎮 Show core experience — Signals what gameplay feels like

📢 Strengthen marketing — Helps the game stand out

⚠️ A tagline must match the real game. Do not promise features or experiences your game doesn’t deliver.

⚖️ Your announcement must match what your game actually delivers.

Activity 5.1 Launch Announcement Agent

You will now create an honest, professional launch announcement using AI responsibly.

Notebook LM will help draft your launch announcement by using your Launch Brief and the provided agent template

Paste the 🔗Launch Announcement Agent Prompt into Notebook LM’s chat

Review the ethical check for honesty and accuracy

Review and save the final announcement draft as a note in Notebook LM.

Need help? Follow this video tutorial 🎥

Activity 5.1: Step 1 -  AI-Supported Tagline Drafting

A tagline is a short, memorable phrase that captures your game's essence.

It should be catchy but also honest and accurate.

Copy the prompt below and replace the bracketed placeholders with your game's details from Launch Brief

Ask the AI to generate 3 tagline options

Keep only taglines that match real gameplay.

Cross out anything that overpromises.

Select ONE tagline (you may edit it to better fit your game)

Skill Boxes Key: 🟪 Creativity | 🟩 Problem Solving 🟧 Computational Thinking

Write 3 short taglines (under 10 words each) for a [GENRE] game called [GAME TITLE] where [UNIQUE FACTOR]. The target audience is [PLAYER TYPE] aged [AGE RANGE]."

📌 AI suggests, humans decide. You are responsible for the accuracy and ethics of your final announcement.

lessons.big_question: How can we promote our game clearly and honestly without misleading players?
lessons.uae_link: This lesson connects to UAE AI Ethics Guidelines on transparency and accountability in digital communication. Honest marketing builds trust, a core value in the UAE's Vision 2031 for digital innovation. Reflection Question: What could happen if your launch announcement includes AI-generated claims that are not true about your game?

I can critically justify marketing choices and defend how their announcement protects player trust and accountability.

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 5

Lesson 5:Launch Announcement Creation

‹#›

In this lesson, you’ll:

Create a launch announcement for your game.
Use AI responsibly to write honest marketing copy.
Communicate clearly and avoid overpromising.

UAE Link:  This lesson connects to UAE AI Ethics Guidelines on transparency and accountability in digital communication. Honest marketing builds trust, a core value in the UAE's Vision 2031 for digital innovation.
Reflection Question: What could happen if your launch announcement includes AI-generated claims that are not true about your game?

📁Required for Lesson
🔗Launch Brief (L2)
🔗Launch Decision(L4)

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How can we promote our game clearly and honestly without misleading players?

Lesson 5: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Apply Ethical Judgment
Evaluate marketing language to ensure it is accurate, fair, and does not overpromise beyond the game’s actual experience.

Structured Launch Announcement
Create a clear launch announcement using a provided template that communicates what the game is and who it is for.

Lesson 5: Success Criteria – Launch Announcement Creation

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Evidence of Ethical Review
Review our marketing ethically to ensure accuracy, honesty, and realistic expectations.

Complete Launch Announcement
Create a full launch announcement , including a title, tagline, description, and target audience.

Starter: Launch Decision Recap

‹#›

Lesson 4 Recap
In Lesson 4 , you made a Human Launch Decision- deciding whether your game was ready for a clean launch or required a conditional launch with disclaimers.

Mini Task: Launch Decision Reminder
Before creating your launch announcement, complete this quick check:
My Lesson 4 launch decision: ☐ 🚀 Clean Launch  ☐ ⚠️ Conditional Launch
If you have “Conditional Launch”, what are the known issues:
_______________________________________
ONE claim my announcement must be honest about:
(Even a clean launch has limits — what should you be careful not to overclaim?)
_______________________________________
Why:
_______________________________________

Remember: AI can help write — you are responsible for what is promised.

Explore: What Is a Launch Announcement?

A launch announcement is not just advertising - it’s a promise to players.It shapes what players expect before they ever play your game.

Have you ever played a game that wasn’t what the trailer promised?

Key purposes of a launch announcement

✅ Tell players what your game truly offers
✅ Attract the right audience
✅ Highlight real features (not imagined ones)
✅ Build trust through honest marketing

Discussion Prompt: 
Why do players lose trust when marketing exaggerates features? Think about games you've been excited about that didn't deliver on their promises. How did that feel?

‹#›

Explore: Game Tagline Importance

A tagline is a short, memorable phrase that captures the core idea or feeling of a game.

🎯 Grab attention quickly — Hooks players instantly
🧠 Boost memorability — Makes the game easy to remember
🎮 Show core experience — Signals what gameplay feels like
📢 Strengthen marketing — Helps the game stand out

⚠️ A tagline must match the real game. Do not promise features or experiences your game doesn’t deliver.

‹#›

⚖️ Your announcement must match what your game actually delivers.

Activity 5.1 Launch Announcement Agent

You will now create an honest, professional launch announcement using AI responsibly. 
Notebook LM will help draft your launch announcement by using your Launch Brief and the provided agent template

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟦 Communication |🟪 Creativity

Open your  previous Launch Review Agent Notebook in Notebook LM .
Paste the 🔗Launch Announcement Agent Prompt into Notebook LM’s chat 
Choose one of the generated taglines in Notebook LM ( or create your own)
Review the ethical check for honesty and accuracy
Review and save the final announcement draft as a note in Notebook LM.

Need help? Follow this video tutorial 🎥

‹#›

Analyse: Ethical Marketing Check

Before finalizing your announcement, you must conduct an ethical review. 
This is the most critical part of today's lesson-it's where you ensure your words match reality.

Answer and justify each question:

1

Does your tagline match the real game?

Remove exaggerations or features that don’t exist.

2

Is your description honest?

Fix any wording that oversells or misrepresents.

3

Would you proudly post this today?

If it makes you feel unsure — revise it.

✏️ Write one change you will make and explain why.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟪 Self & Social Awareness | 🟦 Critical Thinking

‹#›

Activity 5.1: Step 1 -  AI-Supported Tagline Drafting

A tagline is a short, memorable phrase that captures your game's essence. 

It should be catchy but also honest and accurate.

Instructions:

Open 🔗 Gemini 

Copy the prompt below and replace the bracketed placeholders with your game's details from Launch Brief 

Ask the AI to generate 3 tagline options

Keep only taglines that match real gameplay.
    Cross out anything that overpromises.

Select ONE tagline (you may edit it to better fit your game)

Skill Boxes Key: 🟪 Creativity | 🟩 Problem Solving 🟧 Computational Thinking

Example prompt: 
Write 3 short taglines (under 10 words each) for a [GENRE] game called [GAME TITLE] where [UNIQUE FACTOR]. The target audience is [PLAYER TYPE] aged [AGE RANGE]."

📌 AI suggests, humans decide. You are responsible for the accuracy and ethics of your final announcement.

Activity 5.1 Launch Announcement Package

You will now create your game's launch announcement using the provided template. 

This is a structured document that ensures you cover all essential information while maintaining ethical standards.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟦 Communication |🟪 Creativity

Download the 🔗 Launch Announcement Template

The template has three main sections: AI-supported tagline drafting completing the announcement fields and the ethical Check

1

AI-Supported Tagline Drafting

Generate tagline options using AI
 assistance (~5-6 mins)

2

Complete Launch Announcement

Fill in all fields with accurate 
information (~10 mins)

3

Ethical Check

Check that your announcement is
honest, accurate, and not misleading
(~3-5 mins)
.

Activity 5.1: Step 2 -  Complete the Launch Announcement

Now you'll complete all fields in the Launch Announcement Template. 
Each field serves a specific purpose in communicating with your audience.

1

Game Title

Your game's official name—keep it clear and memorable

2

Tagline

The short phrase you selected/edited from Step 1

3

What the Game Is

One sentence describing the core gameplay experience. Refer to your launch brief.

4

Who It's For

Your target audience—be specific about age, interests, skill level

5

Call to Action

Leave blank for now—this will be added in Lesson 6

Skill Boxes Key: 🟦 Communication| 🟩 Problem Solving

Need Help? Refer back to your🔗Launch Brief

Portfolio Entry 22: Launch Announcement Draft

Step 1 – Tagline

Step 2 – Launch Announcement

Step 3 –Ethical Check

Once you have created your Launch Announcement in Notebook LM	
Save your final Launch Announcement as a note and add screenshots of all 3 sections
 ( see examples below)

Your Tagline Selection:
________________________

One improvement I will make after my ethical check:
_____________________

‹#›

Stretch Activity: Tagline Variation Challenge

Create three versions of your tagline, each targeting a different ethical concern:

Which version would you actually use? Why? 
Discuss your reasoning with a partner and consider how word choice impacts player expectations.

‹#›

Deepen: AI in Marketing – Help or Risk?

AI tools like Gemini can speed up marketing writing and generate creative ideas.
But AI does not understand ethics, player trust, or long-term reputation.

✅ Humans must always make the final decision.

Discuss with a partner
Before posting your launch announcement, what claim must YOU verify is true about your game?


| How AI helps | Why humans must decide |
| --- | --- |
| Generates multiple options quickly | Only humans know if the game matches the promise |
| Saves time in initial drafting | Humans are responsible for what gets promised |
| Suggests different phrasings and angles | Player trust depends on human judgment |

‹#›

Extension Task: Peer Review Exchange

Instructions:
1.Pair up with a classmate and exchange launch announcements
2.Read your partner's announcement carefully, imagining you're a potential player
3.Provide written feedback on three dimensions: clarity, accuracy, and audience fit
4.Use Gemini to generate three follow-up questions about their target audience
5.Discuss your feedback and the AI-generated questions together

In professional game development, clear and honest marketing depends on feedback and collaboration.

✅ What is strong or effective?🔍 One improvement for clarity🎯 One improvement for accuracy👥 One improvement for audience fit
Be constructive, respectful, and specific.

AI questions should help clarify audience needs — not rewrite the announcement or invent features.

‹#›

Reflection: Ethical Release Decisions

Before a game is released publicly, words become promises. 
It's crucial that your launch announcement is honest, responsible, and accurate

Accuracy & Honesty

Identify ONE claim in your launch announcement that could mislead players if it’s not true.

What is the claim?
_______________________________

What real evidence from your game supports it?
(e.g. gameplay feature, test result, limitation)
_______________________________
_______________________________

Ethical Responsibility
Which of the following best describes your responsibility when using AI for marketing?
 AI decides what is okay to promise 
AI helps write, but I decide what is ethical
AI is responsible if players are misled
Explain why you selected this option (1–2 sentences):
_________________________________
_________________________________

Public Accountability

Imagine a real player reads your announcement and feels disappointed.

What part of your announcement would YOU be accountable for — and why? (Do not mention AI as responsible.)
_________________________________

_________________________________

‹#›

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I can create a clear launch announcement that accurately describes the game and target audience.

Most

I can evaluate and refine marketing language to ensure it is honest, realistic, and ethically responsible.

Some

I can critically justify marketing choices and defend how their announcement protects player trust and accountability.

Lesson 5 – Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 5.1

A student uses AI to generate a tagline that says:
“A completely bug-free adventure for everyone.”
However, their game still has minor bugs.

A. Use the tagline because AI generated it

B. Edit or replace the tagline to match real gameplay

C. Remove all mention of bugs from the announcement

D. Let players discover the issues after launch

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and responsibility)

What is the most responsible action?

‹#›

Which task is most appropriate for AI support during the launch announcement process?

A. Deciding whether the game should be released publicly

B. Taking responsibility for marketing promises

C. Generating multiple tagline ideas quickly

D. Approving the final announcement without review

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 5.2

Lesson 5– Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (AI supports drafting, not decisions)

‹#›

Why must humans always make the final decision on marketing language?

A. AI tools are not creative

B. AI understands player trust better than humans

C. Marketing creates promises that humans are accountable for

D. AI cannot generate text accurately

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 5.3

Lesson 5 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Role in Society
Code: K5.4 (Accountability and ethical responsibility)

‹#›

metadata.activity_description:
Starter: Player Expectations

Why do players get frustrated when marketing exaggerates?

Connection Task - 🎮 Would You Click This?

Imagine you see one of these on a game platform:

Option A:  “A fast-paced puzzle game with simple mechanics”

Option B:  “The most addictive puzzle game you’ll ever play”

Which one sets more honest expectations?

Players don’t just click — they judge.

Explore: Publishing your Game

Today marks an important milestone: you will release your game on a real platform and see exactly what players will experience.

This hands-on publishing experience will teach you the practical steps of game distribution.

Activity 6.1: Publishing your Game on EndStar

The following steps will guide you in publishing your game on Endstar

Search for your game and click edit.

Take screenshots of your game for Level and Game cover photos.

Add a Level Title, Level Description and the Level Thumbnail Screenshot

🎥 Follow this step-by-step video tutorial :🔗 Publishing your game to EndStar

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking| 🟦 Communication

6.  Open Game Editor and add Game Title, Game Thumbnail Game Description

Game Description : Paste your Final Launch Announcement from Notebook LM

7.  Click Publish and select a version of your game to publish.

8.  Return to the Lobby, select “Play”, search for your published game.

9.  Click on “Copy Share Link”

Activity 6.1: Publishing your Game on EndStar

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking| 🟦 Communication

Portfolio Entry 23 : Posted Launch Announcement

Add a screenshot of your Published Launch Announcement

Paste your EndStar Deeplink:

______________________________

🔗Shorten your deepLink URL

_____________________________

Stretch Task: Creating + Publishing a Game Website

1.Generate your website code

•Paste  the 🔗website building template into Claude.ai

•Paste your Launch Review Agent findings from NotebookLM.

•Answer each question into the chat, providing the information to claude.

•Download final generated index.html

2. Build the Folder structure (on your laptop)

•Add poster + game screenshots (File name must match Claude’s file names)

•Check images + buttons work

•Zip index.html + images into a folder

Register an account on itch.io + create a new project

•Upload to itch.io → ✅ Playable in browser

🎯 Landing page hosted. Game stays on Endstar.

Video Tutorial 1 - Creating webpage via Claude

Video Tutorial 2 - Publishing the webpage via Itch.io

Activity 6.2: Future Platform Planning

Now it's your turn to think strategically about distribution. You'll research platforms, consider your game's needs, and make an informed recommendation for where you might publish future projects.

Access your Future Platform Slide template here

Review the characteristics of Itch.io, Steam, and Endstar

Ask Gemini for recommendation

Use AI to generate initial platform suggestions based on your game and goals

Don't just accept AI's answer — justify your final choice with reasoning

Remember: AI suggests — you decide. Your critical thinking and justification matter most.

Portfolio Entry 24:  Future Platform Slide

Game Title: __________Genre: __________Target Audience: __________Goal: ☐ Portfolio ☐ Monetization ☐ Exposure

| Platform | Strengths for My Game | Challenges for My Game |

I will prioritize __________ because ____________________________________________.

_________________________________________________________

_____________________________________________________

Homework Task :  Game Feedback

Your homework assignment bridges today's launch with next lesson's playtesting focus by gathering real player feedback.

Share Endstar link with 1–2 people

Choose friends, family, or classmates who haven't played your game yet

Use 🔗this feedback form to gather structured responses about their experience

We'll analyze this feedback together in the next lesson

📌 Real player feedback is invaluable — it reveals things you can't see from inside the development process.

Make sure that students shorten their URL. Any tool can be used if the website is blocked.

Prompt students to identify any platforms they might already know.

This is a crucial task for the next lesson. External feedback is required.

Ensure that the students try to complete this task outside of the class room if possible.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.

This question helps students recognise that AI is best used for idea generation, not ownership of decisions.

Students must understand that humans are accountable for decisions made using AI support, especially after launch.

I can publish a live Endstar game with a working deeplink and an accurate launch announcement.
