Lesson 6

Lesson 6: Launch / Release Day

‹#›

In this lesson, you’ll:

Publish your game
Share your launch announcement
Explore future platforms. 

This lesson connects game creation to real-world distribution.

UAE Link:  The UAE encourages responsible digital publishing and entrepreneurship. Understanding where and how digital products are released helps creators build trust, plan sustainably, and grow audiences responsibly.
Reflection Question: How does choosing the right platform affect who plays your game and how it is perceived?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How do developers responsibly release a game today - and plan where it could go next?

Lesson 6: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Evaluate Publishing Platforms
Compare game platforms and critically assess an AI recommendation to plan future distribution.

Execute a Responsible Game Release
Publish your game to Endstar, create a working deeplink, and post an accurate launch announcement that reflects their launch decision.

Lesson 6: Success Criteria – Launch / Release Day

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Plan Future Distribution

I can  complete a Future Platform Slide that compares platforms and justifies a platform choice using critical evaluation.

Complete a Public Game Release
I can publish a live Endstar game with a working deeplink and an accurate launch announcement.

Starter: Player Expectations

‹#›

Discussion Question
Why do players get frustrated when marketing exaggerates?

Connection Task - 🎮 Would You Click This?
Imagine you see one of these on a game platform:

Option A:  “A fast-paced puzzle game with simple mechanics”
Option B:  “The most addictive puzzle game you’ll ever play”
Hands up:
Which one sets more honest expectations?

Players don’t just click — they judge.

Explore: Publishing your Game

1

Upload and Configure

2

Create Public Link

3

Post Announcement

Today marks an important milestone: you will release your game on a real platform and see exactly what players will experience. 
This hands-on publishing experience will teach you the practical steps of game distribution.

‹#›

Activity 6.1: Publishing your Game on EndStar

The following steps will guide you in publishing your game on Endstar

Open EndStar

Search for your game and click edit. 

Open your Level 1



Take screenshots of your game for Level and Game cover photos. 



Add a Level Title, Level Description and the Level Thumbnail Screenshot

🎥 Follow this step-by-step video tutorial :🔗 Publishing your game to EndStar

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking| 🟦 Communication

‹#›

6.  Open Game Editor and add Game Title, Game Thumbnail Game Description



Game Description : Paste your Final Launch Announcement from Notebook LM

	
7.  Click Publish and select a version of your game to publish. 




8.  Return to the Lobby, select “Play”, search for your published game. 




9.  Click on “Copy Share Link”

Activity 6.1: Publishing your Game on EndStar

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking| 🟦 Communication

‹#›

Video Demo: Publishing your Game on EndStar

‹#›

Slide + video needs to be created demonstrating how to post announcement to endless hub

This may also be moved to lesson 7 , allowing students to add their generated social media image to the announcement.

‹#›

Portfolio Entry 23 : Posted Launch Announcement

Add a screenshot of your Published Launch Announcement

Paste your EndStar Deeplink:

______________________________

🔗Shorten your deepLink URL

Shortened Deeplink URL

_____________________________

‹#›

Analyze: Where Else Can Games Be Published?

You’ve published your first game! Endstar is only one of many platforms developers use to share games worldwide. 

Different platforms reach different audiences and offer different ways to earn money.

Group Discussion (2–3 min)
Where have you heard of games being published or launched?
What platforms do you know? Which do you think is most popular?

‹#›

Game Launch Platform 1: 🔗Itch.io

Indie-focused platform

Built specifically for independent creators with a supportive community

Browser and desktop games

Supports HTML5 games that run in browsers plus downloadable builds

Flexible pricing

Choose free, paid, or pay-what-you-want 
models

0% default platform cut

Developers keep 100% by default, can
voluntarily share with platform

Any engine supported

Works with Unity, Unreal, GameMaker,
custom engines, and more

‹#›

Game Launch Platform 2: 🔗Steam

Largest PC platform

Over 120 million monthly active users worldwide

$100 listing fee

Recoupable after $1,000 in sales - filters out low-effort submissions

30% platform cut

Standard industry rate, decreases slightly at high revenue tiers

Massive audience

Unparalleled discoverability potential and built-in community features

‹#›

Game Launch Platform 3: 🔗 Endstar

Self-publishing focus

No approval process or listing fees — publish immediately

Deeplink sharing

Simple URL-based distribution makes sharing with classmates easy

Portfolio building

Great for showcasing student work and building a developer profile

Monetization planned

Revenue features may be added in future updates

‹#›

Stretch Task: Creating + Publishing a Game Website

1.Generate your website code
•Paste  the 🔗website building template into Claude.ai 
•Paste your Launch Review Agent findings from NotebookLM.
•Answer each question into the chat, providing the information to claude. 
•Download final generated index.html
2. Build the Folder structure (on your laptop)
GameLandingPage/
├─ index.html
└─ images/
•Add poster + game screenshots (File name must match Claude’s file names)
3. Test Locally
•Open index.html
•Check images + buttons work
4. Zip & Upload
•Zip index.html + images into a folder
Register an account on itch.io + create a new project
•Upload to itch.io → ✅ Playable in browser
🎯 Landing page hosted. Game stays on Endstar.

Video Tutorial 1 - Creating webpage via Claude

Video Tutorial 2 - Publishing the webpage via Itch.io

‹#›

Deepen : Self-Publishing vs Publisher

Developers must decide whether to publish independently or work with a publisher. Each option has advantages and trade-offs

Self-Publishing

🎛️More control

You make all creative and business decisions

💰Keep revenue

All profits (minus platform cut) go to you

📣Do your own marketing

Responsible for building audience and promotion

Publisher Partnership

🤝Funding and support

Publishers often provide development funding and expertise

🌍Bigger audience access

Leverage publisher's marketing reach and relationships

📉Larger revenue cut

Publishers typically take 30-50% of revenue

Many independent developers start with self-publishing to gain experience, then consider publishers for larger projects.

‹#›

Activity 6.2: Future Platform Planning

Now it's your turn to think strategically about distribution. You'll research platforms, consider your game's needs, and make an informed recommendation for where you might publish future projects.

Open template

Access your Future Platform Slide template here

Compare platforms

Review the characteristics of Itch.io, Steam, and Endstar

Ask Gemini for recommendation

Use AI to generate initial platform suggestions based on your game and goals

Critically evaluate

Don't just accept AI's answer — justify your final choice with reasoning

Remember: AI suggests — you decide. Your critical thinking and justification matter most.

‹#›

2. Platform Comparison

Portfolio Entry 24:  Future Platform Slide

1. Game Snapshot
Game Title: __________Genre: __________Target Audience: __________Goal: ☐ Portfolio ☐ Monetization ☐ Exposure


| Platform | Strengths for My Game | Challenges for My Game |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Endstar |  |  |
| Itch.io |  |  |
| Steam |  |  |

4. Critical Evaluation
My Final Decision:
I will prioritize __________ because ____________________________________________.
AI Evaluation:
I agree with the AI
I partially agree
I disagree
Reason:
_________________________________________________________

3. AI Recommendation
Gemini Recommendation:
_____________________________________________________

‹#›

Reflection: Launch & Decision Review

Reflect on your Lesson 4 launch decision. This reflection helps you improve future projects.

Reflection Questions   (2-3 mins)

What did the Launch Review Agent find that you agreed with?

2.     What did you override or decide differently?

3.     Did you publish with known issues?

4.     If yes, what is your plan to fix them?

Write a short response  for each question(1-2 sentences)
Be honest and specific - this is for learning, not judgment.
Use specific examples from your game.

‹#›

Homework Task :  Game Feedback

Your homework assignment bridges today's launch with next lesson's playtesting focus by gathering real player feedback.

Share Endstar link with 1–2 people

Choose friends, family, or classmates who haven't played your game yet

Collect playtest feedback

Use 🔗this feedback form to gather structured responses about their experience

Bring forms to L7

We'll analyze this feedback together in the next lesson

📌 Real player feedback is invaluable — it reveals things you can't see from inside the development process.

‹#›

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I published a working version of the game with an accurate launch announcement and deeplink.

Most

I compared publishing platforms and justified a distribution choice using critical evaluation.

Some

I critically assess AI recommendations and defend responsible human decision-making in public release and platform strategy

Lesson 6– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 6.1

A student publishes with a known bug and clearly discloses it, even though an AI tool suggested delaying the launch.

What does this show?

A. AI advice should always be ignored

B. Responsible human judgment balanced with transparency

C. Launching with bugs is always unethical

D. AI should make the final decision

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

‹#›

An AI recommends a large platform based on past success of similar games.

Why might this not fit a student’s project?

A. AI cannot compare platforms

B. Setting the final success criteria

C. AI only works for professional games

D. AI relies on past patterns, not the student’s readiness

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 6.2

Lesson 6 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (Pattern vs. reasoning).

‹#›

AI suggests adding fake features to a launch announcement.

What should the student do?

A. Revise the text to match the real game

B. Add the new features to attract players

C. Publish it unchanged

D. Exaggerate further

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 6.3

Lesson 6 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Role in Society
Code: K5.3 (Authorship)

‹#›

learning_objectives: Ethical Communication: Use AI tools appropriately to communicate player reactions without misrepresenting them
Feedback Collection: Collect structured playtest feedback from real players using consistent methods.
I collected structured playtest feedback and created social media posts that accurately reflect real player responses.

metadata.activity_description:
Starter: Once It's Live, Players Decide

Why might players notice problems your own team didn’t?

Think about a time someone used your game and noticed something you missed.

What positive feedback do you hope to hear?

What criticism are you afraid players might give?

Key Idea: Once a game is public, developers must listen - not defend.

Explore: Why Playtest Feedback Matters

Playtest feedback reveals how real players actually experience your game.It helps developers spot problems, improve gameplay, and make smarter decisions.

Find gameplay problems early

See where players get confused, stuck, or bored

Make fair, evidence-based improvements

Use real player data instead of personal opinions

Understand what players enjoy

Identify features worth keeping and expanding

Which of these surprised you most during your Term 2 playtest?

Activity 7.1 – In-Class Playtest Feedback

Share your Endstar deeplink with 3–4 classmates

Play each other's games (5–7 minutes per game)

Each tester completes the 6-question 🔗Playtest Feedback Form

Save each feedback form with a unique name (e.g. FeedbackMohammed_SpaceRunner.docx)

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟦 Communication

At least 4 completed forms total (2-3 classmates + 1-2 outside testers from your homework).

Activity7.2 : Preparing Feedback for Analysis

Upload all completed feedback forms and supporting project documents to your NotebookLM

workspace ( use the same workspace as previous tasks)

Quick Human Scan (2-3 mins)

Read through your feedback forms carefully. Pay attention to what players are saying.

Surprising comments that challenge your assumptions

Strong reactions (positive or negative)

Memorable quotes that capture the player experience

Step 1 — Verify your documents

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

Check if these are already uploaded. If not, upload them now:

☐ Launch Review Agent Findings

☐ Term 2 + Term 3 Portfolio

☐ Playtest Feedback Forms (4 minimum)

Activity 7.2: Social Media Posts from Feedback

Ask🔗Notebook LM to “list the top 5 positive player quotes + feedback”

Select 2 strong player quotes or feedback themes.

Upload a gameplay screenshot to 🔗 ChatGPT chat.

Modify, and paste in the 🔗prompt template with your game details.

Complete this for both of your chosen quotes and save each generated image

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Criticial Thinking | 🟦 Communication |🟪 Creativity

Remember to log into ChatGPT first using Google/Apple/Email Account

Need help? Follow this video tutorial 🎥

Portfolio Entry 25: Social Media Posts

Social Media Post Option 1

Upload both social media images you generated in Gemini in the spaces below.

Social Media Post Option 2

Extension Task: Feedback Pattern Investigator

1️⃣ Ask NotebookLM to group your feedback into themes(e.g., “confusing start,” “fun mechanic,” “too difficult,” “slow pacing”).

One pattern that appears more than once :

________________________________

One comment that surprised you :

________________________________

Which pattern matters most for your game’s long-term success — and why?

_____________________________________________________

What change (if any) should you actually make?

__________________________________

Why is that decision yours — not the AI’s?

__________________________________

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟦 Communication |🟧 Computational Thinking

More feedback reveals patterns. Patterns reveal truth.

Go beyond collecting feedback - investigate it.

Outline activities: Briefly walk through the three tasks — playtesting, uploading to NotebookLM, and creating social posts.

Emphasize structure: Remind students to use the feedback form carefully so results are clear and useful.

Guide reflection: Ask students the reflection question and invite 1–2 quick responses to spark thinking.

Transition: Move students into the playtesting activity and ensure everyone has their links/forms ready.

Ask the discussion question: Give students ~30 seconds silent thinking time before inviting answers.

Encourage honesty: Remind students that all developers miss things — this is normal.

Run the pair task: Guide students through Think → Pair → Share quickly to keep energy up.

Transition: Connect this mindset to the upcoming playtesting activity.

Explain the goal: Students are collecting useful, structured feedback, not just opinions.

Reinforce target: Check students are on track to reach at least 4 completed forms.

Explain the purpose: Students are organizing feedback so it can be analysed effectively with AI.

Stress completeness: Remind students missing files weaken their analysis later.

Focus attention: Encourage students to highlight surprising comments and strong emotions..

Explain the goal: Students are turning real feedback into ethical marketing content.

Guide image creation: Remind students to upload screenshots without altering gameplay.

Circulate support: Help students troubleshoot Gemini or prompt issues.

Inform students that they can prompt gemini to make changes.

Position as optional challenge: Offer this to fast finishers or students wanting deeper analysis.

Explain the aim: Students look for patterns, not just individual comments.

Encourage evidence: Ask students to base answers on repeated feedback.

Push for real evidence: Remind students to include an actual tester quote or summary.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.

This question helps students recognise that AI is best used for idea generation, not ownership of decisions.

Students must understand that humans are accountable for decisions made using AI support, especially after launch.

lessons.big_question: How can we collect honest feedback and use it responsibly after launch?
lessons.uae_link: UAE game developers use real player playtesting at events like the Abu Dhabi Gaming Festival to refine games before release. Reflection Question: How can player feedback change the way a game is improved or marketed? Introduce lesson purpose: Explain that today focuses on collecting real player feedback and using it to improve and promote games. Outline activities: Briefly walk through the three tasks — playtesting, uploading to NotebookLM, and creating social posts. Emphasize structure: Remind students to use the feedback form carefully so results are clear and useful. Connect to real world: Mention the UAE example to show that professional developers rely on player testing. Guide reflection: Ask students the reflection question and invite 1–2 quick responses to spark thinking.

Introduce lesson purpose: Explain that today focuses on collecting real player feedback and using it to improve and promote games.
Outline activities: Briefly walk through the three tasks — playtesting, uploading to NotebookLM, and creating social posts.
Emphasize structure: Remind students to use the feedback form carefully so results are clear and useful.
Connect to real world: Mention the UAE example to show that professional developers rely on player testing.
Guide reflection: Ask students the reflection question and invite 1–2 quick responses to spark thinking.
Transition: Move students into the playtesting activity and ensure everyone has their links/forms ready.
Guiding Computational Thinking Question: What if we don't have the right tools — how can we adapt and problem-solve in Endstar?

Collecting structured playtest feedback and transforming real player reactions into ethical social media posts aligns with UK D&T KS3 1d (evaluating outcomes and communicating design decisions), AQA 3.1.1 (impacts of digital technology and responsible use of AI-generated content), IB MYP Criterion C (creating and communicating a digital solution for an audience), and CSTA 3A-IC-25 (evaluating the accuracy, credibility, and responsible representation of digital content).

Collecting structured playtest feedback and transforming real player reactions into ethical social media posts aligns with UK D&T KS3 1d (evaluating outcomes and communicating design decisions), AQA 3.1.1 (impacts of digital technology and responsible use of AI-generated content), IB MYP Criterion C (creating and communicating a digital solution for an audience), and CSTA 3A-IC-25 (evaluating the accuracy, credibility, and responsible representation of digital content).

Set the tone: Explain this starter is about mindset — accepting feedback as part of development.
Ask the discussion question: Give students ~30 seconds silent thinking time before inviting answers.
Encourage honesty: Remind students that all developers miss things — this is normal.
Run the pair task: Guide students through Think → Pair → Share quickly to keep energy up.
Highlight the key idea: Emphasize that good developers listen and learn, not argue with players.
Transition: Connect this mindset to the upcoming playtesting activity.

Explain the goal: Students are collecting useful, structured feedback, not just opinions.
Review instructions: Ensure everyone shares links and understands the rotation system.
Time management: Keep playtesting tight (5–7 min per game) and announce time checks.
Monitor form quality: Circulate to ensure testers give specific, helpful comments.
Remind file naming: Stress saving forms clearly to avoid confusion later.
Reinforce target: Check students are on track to reach at least 4 completed forms.
Transition: Signal when to stop testing and move to uploading feedback.

Explain the purpose: Students are organizing feedback so it can be analysed effectively with AI.
Guide uploads: Check everyone uploads all required documents to NotebookLM.
Stress completeness: Remind students missing files weaken their analysis later.
Run the quick scan: Give 2–3 minutes quiet reading time to spot key reactions.
Focus attention: Encourage students to highlight surprising comments and strong emotions..

Explain the goal: Students are turning real feedback into ethical marketing content.
Model the process: Demonstrate how to ask NotebookLM for top quotes.
Emphasize selection: Encourage choosing quotes that are authentic and meaningful.
Guide image creation: Remind students to upload screenshots without altering gameplay.
Check prompt accuracy: Ensure placeholders are replaced with correct game details.
Circulate support: Help students troubleshoot Gemini or prompt issues.
Reinforce saving: Confirm both images are saved clearly for later showcase.

Inform students that they can prompt gemini to make changes.

Position as optional challenge: Offer this to fast finishers or students wanting deeper analysis.
Explain the aim: Students look for patterns, not just individual comments.
Model a theme example: Give a quick sample (e.g., “too hard at start”).
Encourage evidence: Ask students to base answers on repeated feedback.
Stress ownership: Emphasize that final decisions belong to the developer, not AI.

Frame the purpose: Explain this reflection builds awareness of blind spots and AI limits.
Keep it quiet and quick: Give 2–3 minutes focused individual writing time.
Push for real evidence: Remind students to include an actual tester quote or summary.
Prompt deeper thinking: Encourage honest answers about why they missed the issue.
Highlight ownership: Stress that AI can assist analysis, but decisions stay human.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

Teaching note: This question reinforces that AI can surface issues, but humans must prioritise based on player impact and context.
It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.
This is a practice question only, to be completed in Classkick.
Answer key: C

This question helps students recognise that AI is best used for idea generation, not ownership of decisions.
It reinforces that responsibility and accountability always remain with humans.
This is a practice question only, to be completed in Classkick.
Answer key: B

Teaching note: This question reinforces that AI does not take responsibility for outcomes.
Students must understand that humans are accountable for decisions made using AI support, especially after launch.
This is a practice question only, to be completed in Classkick.
Answer key: A

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 7

Lesson 7: First Feedback Collection

‹#›

In this lesson you’ll: 

Gather structured playtest feedback from classmates
Upload feedback to your NotebookLM knowledge base
Turn real player quotes into 2 social media posts.

UAE Link:  UAE game developers use real player playtesting at events like the Abu Dhabi Gaming Festival to refine games before release.
Reflection Question: How can player feedback change the way a game is improved or marketed?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How can we collect honest feedback and use it responsibly after launch?

Lesson 7: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Ethical Communication
Use AI tools appropriately to communicate player reactions without misrepresenting them

Feedback Collection
Collect structured playtest feedback from real players using consistent methods.

Lesson 7: Success Criteria – Player Feedback

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Authentic Posts
Create social media posts that accurately reflect real player responses

Quality Data
Gather multiple completed feedback forms using a consistent structure

Starter: Once It's Live, Players Decide

‹#›

💬 Discussion Question
Why might players notice problems your own team didn’t?
Think about a time someone used your game and noticed something you missed.

Pair Task 

🧠Think 
What positive feedback do you hope to hear?
👥 Pair 
What criticism are you afraid players might give?
📢 Share
Discuss with the class

Key Idea: Once a game is public, developers must listen - not defend.

Explore: Why Playtest Feedback Matters

Playtest feedback reveals how real players actually experience your game.It helps developers spot problems, improve gameplay, and make smarter decisions.

🐞

Find gameplay problems early

See where players get confused, stuck, or bored

📊

Make fair, evidence-based improvements

Use real player data instead of personal opinions

😄

Understand what players enjoy

Identify features worth keeping and expanding

Which of these surprised you most during your Term 2 playtest?

‹#›

Activity 7.1 – In-Class Playtest Feedback

Share your Endstar deeplink with 3–4 classmates

Play each other's games (5–7 minutes per game)

Each tester completes the 6-question 🔗Playtest Feedback Form

Save each feedback form with a unique name (e.g. FeedbackMohammed_SpaceRunner.docx)

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Problem Solving | 🟦 Communication

🎯Target
At least 4 completed forms total (2-3 classmates + 1-2 outside testers from your homework).

‹#›

Activity7.2 : Preparing Feedback for Analysis

Upload all completed feedback forms and supporting project documents to your NotebookLM
workspace ( use the same workspace as previous tasks)

Quick Human Scan (2-3 mins)
Read through your feedback forms carefully. Pay attention to what players are saying.
Look for:
Surprising comments that challenge your assumptions
Strong reactions (positive or negative)
Memorable quotes that capture the player experience

Step 1 — Verify your documents

Step 2 — Scan the feedback

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

Check if these are already uploaded. If not, upload them now:
☐ Launch Brief  
☐ Launch Review Agent Findings
☐ Term 2 + Term 3 Portfolio
☐ Playtest Feedback Forms (4 minimum)

‹#›

Analyze : Social Media for Game Announcement’s

Good game announcements must:

🎯 Grab attention quickly in crowded feeds
📢 Communicate what the game is about in seconds
🚀 Encourage players to try the game

Discussion prompts:
What grabs your attention first in each poster?
How quickly can you tell what the game is about?
What makes you want (or not want) to play?

‹#›

Activity 7.2: Social Media Posts from Feedback

Your Task
Ask🔗Notebook LM to “list the top 5 positive player quotes + feedback”

Select 2 strong player quotes or feedback themes.

Upload a gameplay screenshot to 🔗 ChatGPT chat. 

Modify, and paste in the 🔗prompt template with your game details. 

Complete this for both of your chosen quotes and save each generated image

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Criticial Thinking | 🟦 Communication |🟪 Creativity

Remember to log into ChatGPT first using Google/Apple/Email Account

Need help? Follow this video tutorial 🎥

‹#›

Exemplars: Social Media Posts

‹#›

Portfolio Entry 25: Social Media Posts

Social Media Post Option 1

Upload both social media images you generated in Gemini in the spaces below.

Social Media Post Option 2

‹#›
Example outputs

Extension Task: Feedback Pattern Investigator

1️⃣ Ask NotebookLM to group your feedback into themes(e.g., “confusing start,” “fun mechanic,” “too difficult,” “slow pacing”).
2️⃣ Identify:
One pattern that appears more than once : 
________________________________

One comment that surprised you :
________________________________
Which pattern matters most for your game’s long-term success — and why?
_____________________________________________________
3️⃣ Decide:
What change (if any) should you actually make?
__________________________________
Why is that decision yours — not the AI’s?
__________________________________

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟦 Communication |🟧 Computational Thinking

More feedback reveals patterns. Patterns reveal truth.
Go beyond collecting feedback - investigate it.

‹#›

Reflection: Feedback Blind Spots

Players noticed things you missed.
This reflection helps you understand why — and the limits of AI.

Reflection Table   (2-3 mins)


| What surprised me in the feedback | Evidence from testers (quote or summary) | Why didn’t I notice this before playtesting? | What I learned |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

What could AI help with in today’s feedback — and what could only you decide?
____________________________________________________________________

‹#›

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I collected structured playtest feedback and created social media posts that accurately reflect real player responses.

Most

I selected meaningful player quotes, represented them honestly, and ensured my posts did not exaggerate feedback.

Some

I identified feedback patterns, explained what they mean for my game’s future, and justified what I will or will not change.

Lesson 7– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 7.1

A student uploads 20 playtest feedback forms into an AI tool.The AI summarises them and recommends removing a level that some players found “confusing.”

What is the most responsible next step?

A. Remove the level because the AI analysed more data

B. Follow the AI recommendation exactly

C. Review the summary and decide whether the level supports the game’s goals

D. Ignore the feedback because players are not developers

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

‹#›

A student only collects playtest feedback from close friends who enjoy the same type of games. When NotebookLM analyses the feedback, it reports that players found the game "fun and well-balanced."

Why might this analysis be misleading?

A. NotebookLM cannot analyse playtest feedback accurately

B. The AI's conclusion reflects who was chosen to test — not all potential players

C. Friends always give dishonest feedback

D. AI tools only work with large datasets

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 7.2

Lesson 7 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reflects Human Choices
Code: K2.1 (Human design decisions).

‹#›

An AI tool groups playtest comments into themes like “confusing,” “fun,” and “frustrating.”

Which statement best describes what the AI is actually doing?

A. Detecting patterns in language across responses

B. Deciding which feedback matters most

C. Judging whether the game is enjoyable

D. Understanding how players feel emotionally

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 7.3

Lesson 7 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI's Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (Pattern Recognition v Ethical Reasoning/Emotion/Responsibility.)

‹#›

learning_objectives: Build Evidence-Based Plans: Create a clear, prioritised improvement plan using real player feedback and explain the reasons behind your decisions.
Analyse Feedback with AI: Use AI to review playtest feedback and apply your own judgment to improve your game.
I used AI to organise playtest feedback and created a prioritised Iteration Plan based on real evidence.
I explained why my top priorities matter and showed how my decisions align with my game goals.

metadata.activity_description:
Lesson 8: Feedback Analysis & Iteration Planning

Analyze real playtest feedback using NotebookLM

Prioritize what issues need to be fixed next and what do not.

In Lesson 7’s extension task, you might have explored patterns in

your feedback. Today we formalise that analysis and turn it into

prioritised design decisions.”

UAE Link: The UAE’s growing game industry, supported by Dubai Future Foundation, relies on player feedback and rapid iteration — the same process you’re practicing in this lesson.

Reflection Question: What did AI help you see in the feedback — and what did you decide using your own judgment?

Starter: Feedback ≠ Instructions

Players give feedback — should developers fix everything they say?

How might AI help sort feedback? What decisions must humans still make?

What problems might happen if developers try to fix every player complaint?

What happens if feedback is ignored completely?

Key Idea: Feedback provides signals - humans decide what matters.

Explore: Why Feedback Needs Context

Playtest feedback only makes sense when you understand the bigger picture. To make smart decisions, you need to understand it alongside:

This is why NotebookLM is used — all your data exists in one controlled knowledge base.

What were you trying to achieve?

Problems already identified by

Gaps between your intention

and what players experienced

The context of your original human choices

Activity 8.1 – Feedback Analysis with NotebookLM

Open NotebookLM with your feedback forms from Lesson 7

Copy this example agent prompt into Notebook LM’s chat.

Review the AI output critically:

✔ Match check — does it reflect the real feedback?

✔ Missing ideas — did AI overlook anything?

✔ Watch for Bias - Did repeated complaints overweight one issue unfairly

Output: A short list of 3–5 feedback themes you agree with.

Save the analysis as a Note

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

AI Reminder: This gives you organised data — not decisions.

Use AI to identify patterns, then apply human judgment to verify them.

Activity 8.1 - Agent Prompt to Use in NotebookLM

Copy this prompt into your NotebookLM session:

Categorise all playtest feedback as Positive, Negative, or Suggestion.

Cross-reference this feedback against:

- Top 3 Problems from the Launch Brief

- Launch Review Agent Findings

- Which issues were already known

- The top 3 patterns across all responses

| Priority | Issue / Problem Description | Source of Issue  (Feedback / AI / Gap / Known Issue) | Severity  (Low / Medium / High) | AI Recommendation | My Decision  (Do / Skip / Modify) | Why I Chose This |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| ⭐ | Players couldn’t find the start button. | Playtest feedback | High | Prioritize — blocks gameplay | Do | Players may quit if they’re confused |

Portfolio Entry 26 : Iteration Plan Template

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking | Problem Solving

List 5–8 issues you want to improve.

Use NotebookLM to help you understand the feedback.

Mark your top 1–2 priorities with a ⭐.

Explain your reasoning — don’t just copy the AI.

Extension Task: Iteration Plan Peer Review

Exchange your Iteration Plans with your partner

Check if priorities make sense based on their justifications

Find one missing or over-prioritised issue

Provide one written suggestion for improvement

🔁 What is one thing you will change in your own plan after feedback:

_______________________________________________

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟦 Communication |🟧 Computational Thinking

Partner with a classmate to review each other's Iteration Plans.

Instruct students to remove the example within the table.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.

This question helps students recognise that AI is best used for idea generation, not ownership of decisions.

Students must understand that humans are accountable for decisions made using AI support, especially after launch.

prioritised design decisions.”

Reflection Question: What did AI help you see in the feedback — and what did you decide using your own judgment?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How do we decide what to fix next — and what not to fix?

Lesson 8: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Build Evidence-Based Plans
Create a clear, prioritised improvement plan using real player feedback and explain the reasons behind your decisions.

Analyse Feedback with AI
Use AI to review playtest feedback and apply your own judgment to improve your game.

Lesson 8: Success Criteria – Feedback Analysis

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Justify Your Choices

Clearly explain iteration priorities, even when AI recommendations are not followed.

Identify Meaningful Patterns
Use AI to find feedback patterns and decide which matter most for improvement.

Starter: Feedback ≠ Instructions

‹#›

Discussion Question

Players give feedback — should developers fix everything they say?
How might AI help sort feedback? What decisions must humans still make?

Mini Task (pairs/ 2 mins)
Think 
What problems might happen if developers try to fix every player complaint?
Consider 
What happens if feedback is ignored completely?

Key Idea: Feedback provides signals - humans decide what matters.

Explore: Why Feedback Needs Context

Playtest feedback only makes sense when you understand the bigger picture. To make smart decisions, you need to understand it alongside:

This is why NotebookLM is used — all your data exists in one controlled knowledge base.

Launch Brief Goals

What were you trying to achieve?

Known Issues

Problems already identified by 
the Launch Review Agent

Design vs Reality

Gaps between your intention 
and what players experienced

Launch Decision

The context of your original human choices

‹#›

Activity 8.1 – Feedback Analysis with NotebookLM

Open NotebookLM with your feedback forms from Lesson 7

Copy this example agent prompt into Notebook LM’s chat. 

Review the AI output critically:
✔ Match check — does it reflect the real feedback?
✔ Missing ideas — did AI overlook anything?
✔ Watch for Bias - Did repeated complaints overweight one issue unfairly
Output: A short list of 3–5 feedback themes you agree with.
Save the analysis as a Note

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

AI Reminder: This gives you organised data — not decisions.

Your Task (15 mins)
Use AI to identify patterns, then apply human judgment to verify them.

Feedback Themes


_____________

_____________

_____________

_____________

‹#›

Activity 8.1 - Agent Prompt to Use in NotebookLM

Copy this prompt into your NotebookLM session:

Categorise all playtest feedback as Positive, Negative, or Suggestion.

Cross-reference this feedback against:
- Top 3 Problems from the Launch Brief
- Launch Review Agent Findings

Identify:
- Which issues were already known
- Which issues are new
- The top 3 patterns across all responses

‹#›

Analyze: AI Can Group — Humans Must Decide

Key Rule: Pattern detection ≠ priority decisions

AI Strengths

Groups similar feedback quickly
Detects repetition across responses
Cross-references documents fast

Human Responsibilities

Decide what fits the game vision
Decide what is worth fixing now
Decide what stays out of scope

‹#›

Deepen: Building an Iteration Plan

Now you'll create an Iteration Plan that brings together all your evidence sources. Use all four evidence sources to identify improvement priorities.

📂 Evidence Sources

Feedback analysis (NotebookLM)
Launch review findings (L4)
Gap analysis (Launch vs Design) (L4)
Known launch issues (L4)

How We Improve our Game
📂 Sources → 🎯 Prioritize Issues → 📝 Build Iteration Plan → ⭐ Fix Top 1–2

‹#›

🔁 Term 2: Empathise → Define → Ideate → Prototype
🚀 Term 3: Test → Evaluate → Iterate → Release

| Priority | Issue / Problem Description | Source of Issue  (Feedback / AI / Gap / Known Issue) | Severity  (Low / Medium / High) | AI Recommendation | My Decision  (Do / Skip / Modify) | Why I Chose This |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ⭐ | Players couldn’t find the start button. | Playtest feedback | High | Prioritize — blocks gameplay | Do | Players may quit if they’re confused |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

Portfolio Entry 26 : Iteration Plan Template

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking | Problem Solving

List 5–8 issues you want to improve.
Use NotebookLM to help you understand the feedback.
Mark your top 1–2 priorities with a ⭐.
Explain your reasoning — don’t just copy the AI.
(20 mins)

‹#›

Extension Task: Iteration Plan Peer Review

Peer Review(5–7 min)

🔄Swap Plans
     Exchange your Iteration Plans with your partner

🧠 Evaluate Priorities
     Check if priorities make sense based on their justifications

🔍 Identify Gaps
      Find one missing or over-prioritised issue

✍️ Give Feedback
      Provide one written suggestion for improvement

🔁 What is one thing you will change in your own plan after feedback:
_______________________________________________

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟦 Communication |🟧 Computational Thinking

Partner with a classmate to review each other's Iteration Plans.

‹#›

Reflection: Choosing My Next Fix

Step 1 — My Top Fix for Lesson 9

🛠 What is the #1 improvement I will make next?

 Write one clear fix:
_________________________________

Why is this the most important fix?(Use evidence from feedback or testing)
 ________________________________ _________________________________

Final Reflection Question
💬 Who had the final decision — me or the AI? Why is that important in game design? _____________________________________________________________

Step 2 — Human vs AI Decision

🤖 What did the AI recommend?
 _________________________________
🧠 Did I follow the AI suggestion?
Yes — I agreed with the AI
Partly — I adapted the AI idea
No — I chose a different approach
👉 Explain your decision: __________________________________________________________________

You are the game director now. Decide what changes next. (3-5 min)

‹#›

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I used AI to organise playtest feedback and created a prioritised Iteration Plan based on real evidence.

Most

I explained why my top priorities matter and showed how my decisions align with my game goals.

Some

I refined my Iteration Plan after peer review or extension analysis and defended my final priority choices as the game director.

Lesson 8– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 8.1

A student uses NotebookLM to analyse playtest feedback. The AI recommends prioritising an issue because it appears frequently, but the issue does not affect the core gameplay.

What is the most responsible action?

A. Use human judgment to decide whether the issue impacts the game's goals

B. Automatically prioritise the issue because many players mentioned it

C. Ignore the AI output completely

D. Let the AI decide because it analysed more data

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

‹#›

Why is it important for students to review NotebookLM's feedback categorisation before saving it as a note?

A. Because feedback should never be grouped

B. Because AI always exaggerates problems

C. Because AI is not allowed to analyse feedback, unless instructed by the human

D. Because AI may miscategorise feedback or overweight repeated complaints

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 8.2

Lesson 8 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reflects Human Choices
Code: K2.5 (Bias and limitations in AI outputs ).

‹#›

A team uses AI to summarize 150 playtest comments.
The AI reports: “Most players found Level 2 frustrating.”

What is the most responsible next step?

A. Immediately remove Level 2

B. Trust the AI summary without checking the original comments

C. Review the original feedback and decide whether changes are needed

D. Ignore the AI because it cannot understand emotions

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 8.3

Lesson 8 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation.)

‹#›

core_topics: Make the change in your game
Partly — it improved but still needs work
ai_focus: 🧠 THINK (1 min — silent)Which game task benefits most from AI?Which task still needs human control?
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
Teaching note: This question reinforces that AI does not take responsibility for outcomes.
learning_objectives: Evaluate Fix Effectiveness: Update your plan with clear evidence showing whether fixes were successful, partial, or still unresolved.
Implement Evidence-Based Improvements: Apply top-priority fixes from your Iteration Plan and test them effectively.
I implemented 1–2 priority fixes, tested them properly, and updated my Iteration Plan with evidence.
I verified my fixes through repeat testing and documented whether issues were resolved, partial, or still open.
I identified second-order effects of my fixes and adjusted my remaining priorities accordingly.
metadata.activity_description:
🧠 THINK (1 min — silent)Which game task benefits most from AI?Which task still needs human control?

👥 PAIR (2 min — discuss)Agree on:

👉 One task AI is best at👉 One task humans must control

🗣 SHAREPairs share one example.

AI can now generate 3D game assets in minutes.

Work that used to take days can happen instantly.

How could this change game development workflows?

Starter: AI Discovery Warm-Up

Answer the following questions based on the above scenario:

Which issue should be the top priority — and why?

__________________________________

Which suggestion is tempting but not urgent?

___________________________________

What’s the difference between:

_____________________________________

You received 10 pieces of feedback:

3 players said the controls feel slow

2 players said they didn’t understand the objective

4 players said they loved the core mechanic

1 player suggested adding multiplayer

2 players said the game crashes occasionally

You only have time to fix ONE thing today.

Starter: Fix the Right Thing

In Lesson 8, you created a comprehensive Iteration Plan identifying issues across your game.

Today , you're moving from planning to execution.

You are not fixing everything.You are not rushing changes without testing.You are not guessing what worked.

You are fixing 1–2 priority issues only.You are testing each change properly.You are recording evidence honestly.

Focused. Verified. Intentional improvement.

Professional studios prioritise impact over volume.

Fixing five small issues poorly creates confusion.

Fixing one or two high-impact issues well ,creates real improvement.

Explore: From Plan to Action: Focus on Impact

Activity 9.1 – Implement top-priority fixes

1️⃣ Open your Iteration Plan from Lesson 8

2️⃣ Focus only on your ★ top priorities	→ Choose 1–2 items maximum

Make the change in your game

Immediately play-test the affected section

Check if the issue is actually resolved.

4️⃣ Update your Iteration Plan:✔ Status After Testing✔ Add brief test evidence✔ Reassess priority if needed

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

👉 Fix → Test → Verify (don’t batch fixes)

My 1–2 Priority Fixes Today:

Activity 9.2 : Review Remaining Items

After implementing and testing your priority fixes, review the issues you didn’t address today.

Your game has changed — so your priorities might need to change too.

Some issues may no longer matter. Others may now be more important.

This ongoing prioritization reflects how professional teams work—constantly reassessing what matters most based on new information and completed work.

For each unresolved item in your Iteration Plan, decide:

This issue is still important.Keep it in your plan with its current priority.

Items marked “Still Relevant” will carry forward to Lesson 12: Scope Freeze.

🚫 Now Resolved / No Longer Relevant

Your recent fixes solved this issue indirectly.Mark it as closed.

The issue matters less now.Adjust its ranking accordingly.

lessons.big_question: How do we prove that a fix actually worked?
lessons.uae_link: Farcana, a Dubai-based multiplayer shooter game, ran public playtest events where real players tested early builds, enabling the team to identify bugs and gameplay issues and refine the game before wider release. Reflection Question: What problem from your iteration plan are you most confident you can fix today — and why?
success_criteria: Implement Evidence-Based Improvements
Apply top-priority fixes from your Iteration Plan and test them effectively.
Evidence-Based Updates
Accurately update the Iteration Plan with status (Done / Partial / Still Open) and test evidence.
Verified Improvements
Implement 1–2 priority fixes and test them to confirm they work as intended.

Push reflection question — students must choose a realistic fix.
Reinforce: focus = improvement, not adding features.
Guiding Computational Thinking Question: What if we don't have the right tools — how can we adapt and problem-solve in Endstar?

Curriculum Alignment
Implementing and testing priority fixes aligns with UK D&T KS3 1d (evaluating outcomes and improving designs through iteration), AQA 3.1.1 (evaluating and refining digital products), IB Design Criterion C (creating and iteratively improving digital solutions), and CSTA 3A-AP-17 (systematically testing and refining computational artifacts). Students apply structured problem-solving, evidence-based evaluation, and iterative development practices that mirror real-world game design workflows.

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 9

Lesson 9: Implement Priority Fixes

‹#›

In this lesson you’ll:

Implement their highest-priority iteration fixes,
Immediately test them
Document evidence directly in the Iteration Plan. 

The focus is real improvement, not feature creep.

UAE Link: Farcana, a Dubai-based multiplayer shooter game, ran public playtest events where real players tested early builds, enabling the team to identify bugs and gameplay issues and refine the game before wider release.
Reflection Question: What problem from your iteration plan are you most confident you can fix today — and why?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How do we prove that a fix actually worked?

Lesson 9: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Evaluate Fix Effectiveness
Update your plan with clear evidence showing whether fixes were successful, partial, or still unresolved.

Implement Evidence-Based Improvements
Apply top-priority fixes from your Iteration Plan and test them effectively.

Lesson 9: Success Criteria – Implement Priority Fixes

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Evidence-Based Updates
Accurately update the Iteration Plan with status (Done / Partial / Still Open) and test evidence.

Verified Improvements
Implement 1–2 priority fixes and test them to confirm they work as intended.

‹#›

Mini Task
🧠 THINK (1 min — silent)Which game task benefits most from AI?Which task still needs human control?

👥 PAIR (2 min — discuss)Agree on:
👉 One task AI is best at👉 One task humans must control

🗣 SHAREPairs share one example.

AI can now generate 3D game assets in minutes.
Work that used to take days can happen instantly.
How could this change game development workflows?

Starter: AI Discovery Warm-Up

‹#›

Team Task (5 mins) 
Answer the following questions based on the above scenario:
Which issue should be the top priority — and why?
__________________________________
Which suggestion is tempting but not urgent?
___________________________________
What’s the difference between:
🛠 Fixing a real problem
✨ Adding a new feature
_____________________________________

Scenario Discussion
You received 10 pieces of feedback:
3 players said the controls feel slow
2 players said they didn’t understand the objective
4 players said they loved the core mechanic
1 player suggested adding multiplayer
2 players said the game crashes occasionally
You only have time to fix ONE thing today.

Starter: Fix the Right Thing

‹#›

Want to learn more? Check out this video that shows how  Claude can integrate with Blender to support 3D creation. It highlights how AI can become part of a real creative production workflow.”

Stretch: Claude + Blender integration

In Lesson 8, you created a comprehensive Iteration Plan identifying issues across your game. 
Today , you're moving from planning to execution.

⚠️ What You Are NOT Doing

You are not fixing everything.You are not rushing changes without testing.You are not guessing what worked.

⭐ What You ARE Doing

You are fixing 1–2 priority issues only.You are testing each change properly.You are recording evidence honestly.
Focused. Verified. Intentional improvement.

Why this approach? 
Professional studios prioritise impact over volume.
Fixing five small issues poorly creates confusion.
Fixing one or two high-impact issues well ,creates real improvement.

Explore: From Plan to Action: Focus on Impact

‹#›

This is your focused build time.Work through one priority fix at a time.Verify each fix before moving on.

Make the Change

Change only what’s necessary .Be precise. Fix the specific issue — nothing more.

Play the Exact Section Affected

Go directly to where the issue occurred. Experience it exactly as a player would.

Test It At Least Twice

Once is not enough. Play the section twice (or more) to confirm consistent behavior.

Look for New Side Effects

Did the fix break something else?Did it change difficulty unexpectedly?Stay alert to unintended consequences.

⚠️ Critical Rule: Do not start your next priority until this fix is tested and verified. Professionals verify. Amateurs assume.

Analyze: Implement + Immediate Testing

‹#›

Activity 9.1 – Implement top-priority fixes

1️⃣ Open your Iteration Plan from Lesson 8

2️⃣ Focus only on your ★ top priorities	→ Choose 1–2 items maximum
3️⃣ For each fix:
Make the change in your game
Immediately play-test the affected section
Check if the issue is actually resolved.
4️⃣ Update your Iteration Plan:✔ Status After Testing✔ Add brief test evidence✔ Reassess priority if needed

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

👉 Fix → Test → Verify (don’t batch fixes)

🎯Your Task (25 mins)

My 1–2 Priority Fixes Today:

____________________

____________________

‹#›

‹#›

Deepen: Understanding Second-Order Effects

Games are connected systems.Fixing one issue can create unexpected changes — sometimes problems, sometimes improvements.That’s why professional developers test immediately after every fix.

Create a New Bug

Your fix might accidentally break something that was working before.

Change Game Difficulty

A small adjustment (like collision or movement) can make the game easier or harder.

Remove Another Issue

Fixing Priority 1 might solve Priority 3 if both came from the same root cause.

Improve Something Else

Some fixes have positive side effects — clearer tutorials might reduce later confusion.

🔎 Critical Question:
After fixing Priority 1, do your remaining priorities still make sense?Always reassess your plan after every successful fix.

Activity 9.2 : Review Remaining Items

After implementing and testing your priority fixes, review the issues you didn’t address today.
Your game has changed — so your priorities might need to change too.
Some issues may no longer matter. Others may now be more important.

This ongoing prioritization reflects how professional teams work—constantly reassessing what matters most based on new information and completed work.

Review Process (5 mins)
For each unresolved item in your Iteration Plan, decide:
✅ Still Relevant
This issue is still important.Keep it in your plan with its current priority.
Items marked “Still Relevant” will carry forward to Lesson 12: Scope Freeze.
🚫 Now Resolved / No Longer Relevant
Your recent fixes solved this issue indirectly.Mark it as closed.
⬇ Lower Priority
The issue matters less now.Adjust its ranking accordingly.

‹#›

Portfolio Entry 27 : Updated Iteration Plan


| Priority | Issue / Problem Description | Source of Issue  (Feedback / AI / Gap / Known Issue) | Severity  (Low / Medium / High) | AI Recommendation | My Decision  (Do / Skip / Modify) | Why I Chose This | Status After Testing (Done / Partial / Still Open) | Evidence / Test Result | Revised Priority  (Still Relevant / Lower Priority / No Longer Relevant) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ⭐ | Players couldn’t find the start button. | Playtest feedback | High | Prioritize — blocks gameplay | Do | Players may quit if they’re confused | Done | Created a bigger start button that is easier to see | No Longer Relevant |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

Return to your Iteration Plan and update the three new columns:

‹#›

Reflection: Did My Fixes Actually Work?

Part 1 – Evidence of Improvement
🛠 What was the most important fix I implemented today? 
—---------------------------------------------------------------
How did I test this fix?_____________________________________
Did it fully solve the problem?
Yes — the issue is resolved
Partly — it improved but still needs work
No — the problem remains
What evidence proves this?_____________________________________

Part 2 – Professional Testing Reflection
What did I learn about testing from this fix?
Select all that apply:
One test is enough
Testing must be repeatable
Evidence must be documented
Fixes can create new issues
Explain your answer:
_____________________________________
Did testing reveal anything unexpected?
_____________________________________

Today you didn’t just make changes — you tested whether they solved real player problems.

‹#›

Extension: Choosing My Next Fix

Implement One Additional Fix (10 mins)

If you have finished your priority fixes:

1️⃣ Choose one remaining issue from your Iteration Plan.2️⃣ Implement the fix carefully.
3️⃣ Test it immediately.
4️⃣ Update the three tracking columns:
Status After Testing
Evidence / Test Result
Revised Priority

🎯 Focus
Quality over quantity.Only implement the fix if you have enough time to test it properly.

‹#›

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I implemented 1–2 priority fixes, tested them properly, and updated my Iteration Plan with evidence.

Most

I verified my fixes through repeat testing and documented whether issues were resolved, partial, or still open.

Some

I identified second-order effects of my fixes and adjusted my remaining priorities accordingly.

Lesson 9– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 9.1

A student used AI to rewrite a confusing tutorial message. The AI suggests the new version is clearer. The student marks the issue “Done” without replaying the level.

What is the most responsible action?

A. Trust the AI’s suggestion

B. Replay the level and verify player clarity

C. Assume AI improved it automatically

D. Move on to a new feature

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

‹#›

Why is AI not sufficient for verifying a bug fix in this lesson?

A. AI cannot generate ideas

B. AI should only be used in marketing

C. AI refuses to evaluate gameplay

D. Fixes must be tested in the real game environment

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 9.2

Lesson 9 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (pattern vs. reasoning).

‹#›

A student uses AI to check whether a bug fix is complete. The AI says, “The issue appears resolved.” The student tests the game once, it works, and they mark it Done. Later, the bug reappears.

What does this demonstrate?

A. AI guarantees long-term reliability

B. One test is enough when AI confirms it

C. AI suggestions still require repeated human testing

D. AI should be responsible for bug tracking

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 9.3

Lesson 9 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (Understanding limitations of AI systems.)

‹#›

metadata.title: Product-Market Fit
core_topics: The AI labels this as “Strong Retention.”
ai_focus: Deepen: AI vs Human Decision Making
What emotional or creative signals might humans notice that AI cannot?
I critically evaluated AI-generated PMF conclusions and defended my final judgment using human interpretation of evidence.
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
Teaching note: This question reinforces that AI does not take responsibility for outcomes.
learning_objectives: Build Evidence-Based Conclusions: Use feedback analysis and iteration results to evaluate whether your game fits its target audience.
Apply the PMF Framework: Understand what Product–Market Fit means and why it requires real evidence.
I used real player feedback and data to assess whether my game shows signs of Product–Market Fit.
I justified my PMF classification using specific evidence from feedback, iteration results, and audience signals.
metadata.activity_description:
Starter: What Makes a Game “Successful”?

Rank these from MOST important [1] to LEAST important [6]

Write one sentence explaining #1 choice.

[ ] 🔁 High retention (players return)

[ ]📢 Recommended to friends

[ ]⏱ Long playtime per session

For your #1 choice - what real evidence would prove it?

___________________________________

If a game gets 500,000 downloads in the first week — is it successful?

What if it makes money but players leave bad reviews?

Explore: What is Product–Market Fit?

Product–Market Fit happens when your game clearly satisfies a real need or desire for a specific group of players.

🎯 PMF = What players repeatedly DO - not what you THINK they want.

❌ You Cannot Prove PMF With

Untested assumptions about players

AI-generated predictions without validation

Observable player behavior patterns

Authentic reactions and feedback

Measurable engagement and retention

Explore: Why PMF Comes After Launch

PMF cannot be proven before launch.Assumptions must be tested with real players.

Minimum Viable Product Launch

You completed this exact process: L6 → L7 → L8 → L9 → Now L10.

Real startups follow the same evidence-driven approach.

For your selected game, evaluate the following(5 mins):  	Game name ________________

| PMF Signal | What It Means | Your Answer |

| 🎯 Target Audience | Who the game is clearly made for |  |

| 💡 Core Need | The main desire/problem it satisfies (fun, competition, creativity, social play, challenge, etc.) |  |

| ❤️ Love Signal | Proof players value it (reviews, hype, fan art, community, streamers, high ratings) |  |

| 🔁 Retention Signal | Proof players come back (updates, progression, daily rewards, long lifespan) |  |

| ✨ Unique Value | What makes it different from similar games |  |

Activity 10.1: Identify Product–Market Fit Signals

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Problem Solving | 🟦Communication

You will analyze your favorite game to determine whether it shows clear Product–Market Fit

Activity 10.1: Identify Product–Market Fit Signals

You will analyze your favorite game to determine whether it shows clear Product–Market Fit

For your selected game, evaluate the following(5 mins):  	Game name ________________

Who is the intended player?

Is the audience clearly defined?

___________________ ______

What desire or problem does the game satisfy?

_________________________________

Why would this audience care?

_________________________________

What evidence proves players love it?

____________________________(Reviews? Community? Social media?)

What proves players return?

__________________________________(Updates? Long playtime? Competitive systems?)

What makes it different from competitors?

______________________________

Based on your analysis, classify the game:

☐ Strong Fit☐ Partial Fit☐ Weak Fit

Justify your decision with evidence.

___________________________________

| Evidence Type | Source | What It Shows | PMF Indicator |

| Target Audience Match | Launch Brief + Testers | Do real players match intended audience? | Audience validation |

| Satisfaction Signals | Positive Feedback | What do players appreciate most? | Value confirmation |

| Pain Points | Negative Feedback | Which issues were critical? | Problem areas |

| Retention Signal | Playtest Feedback Q6 | Would players play again? | Engagement strength |

| USP Clarity | L2 + Feedback | Did testers notice unique factor? | Differentiation proof |

Analyze: PMF Checklist Overview

This checklist in the next activity gathers real evidence to evaluate whether your game demonstrates Product–Market Fit.

📊 L8 Feedback Analysis (NotebookLM)

🔧 L9 Updated Iteration Plan

Portfolio Entry 28 – PMF Evidence Checklist

🧾Use real quotes, numbers, and specific examples.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

Complete this structured checklist using real data from your game development process (25 mins).

You’ll use this to draw final conclusions in Lesson 11.

| Section | Your Evidence & Details (Fill This In) |

| 1. Target Audience Match  Do real testers match your intended audience demographics and preferences? |  |

| 2. Player Satisfaction  Document 2–3 positive quotes showing genuine player appreciation. |  |

| 3. Pain Points Addressed  Which critical issues were fixed in the L9 iteration? |  |

| 4. Retention Signal  How many testers said: “Yes, I would play again”? (Include numbers or percentage.) |  |

| 5. Unique Selling Point Confirmation  Did testers independently mention your unique feature without being prompted? |  |

Extension: Real PMF Case Study

Task - Research: - One indie game that achieved Product–Market Fit - One indie game that failed to achieve PMF

Did AI provide reliable insight?

____________________________Where was human judgment necessary?

____________________________

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

Step 1 – Use Gemini (5 mins)

Who was the target audience?

Why did it succeed / fail?

Are there retention signals?

Step 2 – Verify Manually (10 mins)

✔ Find 2 real evidence points✔ Identify 1 AI limitation or inaccuracy

Step 3 – Compare Using PMF Lens

• Target Audience Match• Satisfaction Signals• Pain Points• Retention Evidence• USP Clarity

Classify each game:Strong Fit / Partial Fit / Weak Fit

This activity only for students who are finished the content /activities early. If there is no time, please skip this lesson

Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.

Curriculum Alignment – Lesson 10: Product–Market Fit (PMF)
Evaluating Product–Market Fit through structured evidence analysis aligns with UK D&T KS3 1d (evaluating outcomes and improving designs based on testing and user feedback), AQA 3.1.1 (evaluating and refining digital products using user data), IB Design Criterion C & D (creating solutions and evaluating their success against criteria), and CSTA 3A-AP-17 (systematically testing and refining computational artifacts).
