slides.content:
‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 10

Lesson 10: Product-Market Fit

‹#›

You've launched your game, collected feedback, analyzed patterns, and fixed critical issues.

This is the moment where evidence replaces assumption.
Your work now moves from “I think it works” to “I can prove it works.”

UAE Link: The UAE startup ecosystem (Hub71, Dubai Future District) encourages companies to launch MVPs, collect real user data, iterate quickly, and then assess market fit — the same process you are now applying to your published game.
Reflection Question: Why can’t you prove your game is successful before real players actually play it?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

Does my game actually fit its market — or do I just hope it does?

Lesson 10: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Build Evidence-Based Conclusions
Use feedback analysis and iteration results to evaluate whether your game fits its target audience.

Apply the PMF Framework
Understand what Product–Market Fit means and why it requires real evidence.

Lesson 10: Success Criteria – Product-Market Fit

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Justified Judgement
Explain whether my game shows signs of Product–Market Fit using specific evidence.

Evidence-Based Evaluation
Use real feedback, iteration results, and audience data to assess PMF.

TERM COMPETITION ANNOUNCEMENT

🎮 Endstar Game Performance Challenge

This term, your game is not just being assessed…It is entering a real performance competition.
At the end of term, prizes will be awarded to students with:
🥇 Most Game Downloads (via Endstar)
🥈 Most Game Plays
🥉 Strongest Player Engagement Evidence

Why This Matters: Success isn’t about thinking your game works — it’s about proving it with real downloads, real plays, and real player data.

Starter: What Makes a Game “Successful”?

‹#›

Mini Task
Rank these from MOST important [1] to LEAST important [6]
Write one sentence explaining #1 choice.
[ ]📥 Lots of downloads	
[ ]💰 Makes money
[ ] 🔁 High retention (players return)
[ ]⭐ Positive reviews
[ ]📢 Recommended to friends
[ ]⏱ Long playtime per session

For your #1 choice - what real evidence would prove it?
___________________________________

Discussion Questions
If a game gets 500,000 downloads in the first week — is it successful?
What if it makes money but players leave bad reviews?

Explore: What is Product–Market Fit?

Product–Market Fit happens when your game clearly satisfies a real need or desire for a specific group of players.

🎯 PMF = What players repeatedly DO - not what you THINK they want.

❌ You Cannot Prove PMF With
Personal opinions  
Gut feelings
Untested assumptions about players
AI-generated predictions without validation

✅ You Prove It With
Real player data 
Observable player behavior patterns
Authentic reactions and feedback
Measurable engagement and retention

‹#›

Explore: Why PMF Comes After Launch

PMF cannot be proven before launch.Assumptions must be tested with real players.

1

Before Launch

Make assumptions

2

Minimum Viable Product Launch

Launch MVP to real users

3

Collect Feedback

Gather player data

4

Iterate

Fix and improve

5

Assess Fit

Evidence-based conclusion

You completed this exact process: L6 → L7 → L8 → L9 → Now L10. 
Real startups follow the same evidence-driven approach.

‹#›

For your selected game, evaluate the following(5 mins):  	Game name ________________


| PMF Signal | What It Means | Your Answer |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 🎯 Target Audience | Who the game is clearly made for |  |
| 💡 Core Need | The main desire/problem it satisfies (fun, competition, creativity, social play, challenge, etc.) |  |
| ❤️ Love Signal | Proof players value it (reviews, hype, fan art, community, streamers, high ratings) |  |
| 🔁 Retention Signal | Proof players come back (updates, progression, daily rewards, long lifespan) |  |
| ✨ Unique Value | What makes it different from similar games |  |

Activity 10.1: Identify Product–Market Fit Signals

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Problem Solving | 🟦Communication

‹#›

You will analyze your favorite game to determine whether it shows clear Product–Market Fit

Activity 10.1: Identify Product–Market Fit Signals

You will analyze your favorite game to determine whether it shows clear Product–Market Fit 

For your selected game, evaluate the following(5 mins):  	Game name ________________

🔍 1. Target Audience
Who is the intended player?
 _________________________
Is the audience clearly defined?
___________________ ______

🎯 2. Need Fulfillment
What desire or problem does the game satisfy?
_________________________________
Why would this audience care?
_________________________________

❤️ 3. Love Signals
What evidence proves players love it?
____________________________(Reviews? Community? Social media?)

🔁 4. Retention Evidence
What proves players return?
__________________________________(Updates? Long playtime? Competitive systems?)

✨ 5. Unique Value
What makes it different from competitors?
______________________________

🎯 Final Decision
Based on your analysis, classify the game:
☐ Strong Fit☐ Partial Fit☐ Weak Fit
Justify your decision with evidence.
___________________________________

Analyze: PMF in the Real World

Discussion Questions:
Who is the audience? what need does it fulfil? 
How do we know it has PMF? (Think: High ratings, repeat play, strong community, long lifespan)

📊 PMF Evidence

🎯 Audience

💡 Core Need

‹#›

Stretch: Secrets to 10x your game’s PMF

Check out this video to see ways in which you can improve your PMF for your game

‹#›

| Evidence Type | Source | What It Shows | PMF Indicator |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Target Audience Match | Launch Brief + Testers | Do real players match intended audience? | Audience validation |
| Satisfaction Signals | Positive Feedback | What do players appreciate most? | Value confirmation |
| Pain Points | Negative Feedback | Which issues were critical? | Problem areas |
| Retention Signal | Playtest Feedback Q6 | Would players play again? | Engagement strength |
| USP Clarity | L2 + Feedback | Did testers notice unique factor? | Differentiation proof |

Analyze: PMF Checklist Overview

This checklist in the next activity gathers real evidence to evaluate whether your game demonstrates Product–Market Fit.

You must use:
📄 L2 Launch Brief
📊 L8 Feedback Analysis (NotebookLM)
🔧 L9 Updated Iteration Plan
📝 Playtest Feedback Forms

‹#›

Portfolio Entry 28 – PMF Evidence Checklist

🧾Use real quotes, numbers, and specific examples.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

Complete this structured checklist using real data from your game development process (25 mins). 
You’ll use this to draw final conclusions in Lesson 11.


| Section | Your Evidence & Details (Fill This In) |
| --- | --- |
| 1. Target Audience Match  Do real testers match your intended audience demographics and preferences? |  |
| 2. Player Satisfaction  Document 2–3 positive quotes showing genuine player appreciation. |  |
| 3. Pain Points Addressed  Which critical issues were fixed in the L9 iteration? |  |
| 4. Retention Signal  How many testers said: “Yes, I would play again”? (Include numbers or percentage.) |  |
| 5. Unique Selling Point Confirmation  Did testers independently mention your unique feature without being prompted? |  |

‹#›

Deepen: AI vs Human Decision Making

Should artificial intelligence decide whether your game has Product-Market Fit?
 
This critical question touches on AI capabilities, limitations, and the irreplaceable value of human judgment in creative decisions.

Before answering, think about the evidence you actually have:

Launch Review Agent from L4
Raw player feedback data
Your interpretation and context
Pattern recognition vs. understanding

Discussion Questions
If AI says your game has PMF but testers didn’t enjoy it — who do you trust? Why?
Can AI detect why players are frustrated — or only that they are?
What emotional or creative signals might humans notice that AI cannot?

‹#›

Reflection: Do I Have Product–Market Fit?

PMF Evidence Check

📊 Based on my evidence so far…
Does my game have Product–Market Fit?
Yes — Strong Fit
Partial Fit
Not Yet

 Why? Use real evidence (quotes, numbers, testing results):
______________________________________________________________________

Final Reflection Question
If you had to present your game to a real investor tomorrow…would your evidence convince them?
Why or why not?____________________________________________________________

🟢Strongest Evidence
What is the strongest signal that players value your game?
__________________________________
Why is this strong?
__________________________________

🔴 Weakest Evidence
⚠ What is the weakest signal or biggest doubt about PMF?
___________________________________
What might this mean for your next decision?
_____________________________

Today you didn’t just improve your game:
You evaluated whether players truly want it.

‹#›

Extension: Real PMF Case Study

Task - Research: - One indie game that achieved Product–Market Fit - One indie game that failed to achieve PMF

Reflection
Did AI provide reliable insight?
____________________________Where was human judgment necessary?
____________________________

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking

‹#›

Step 1 – Use Gemini (5 mins)
Ask Gemini:
Who was the target audience? 
Why did it succeed / fail? 
What do reviews say
Are there retention signals?

Step 2 – Verify Manually (10 mins)
Check:
Steam reviews
Player comments
Articles or interviews
✔ Find 2 real evidence points✔ Identify 1 AI limitation or inaccuracy

Step 3 – Compare Using PMF Lens
For both games, evaluate:
• Target Audience Match• Satisfaction Signals• Pain Points• Retention Evidence• USP Clarity

Classify each game:Strong Fit / Partial Fit / Weak Fit

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I used real player feedback and data to assess whether my game shows signs of Product–Market Fit.

Most

I justified my PMF classification using specific evidence from feedback, iteration results, and audience signals.

Some

I critically evaluated AI-generated PMF conclusions and defended my final judgment using human interpretation of evidence.

Lesson 10– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 10.1

Why can't AI alone determine Product-Market Fit for your game?

A. AI cannot generate comprehensive reports

B. AI cannot access internet data sources

C. AI refuses to evaluate games

D. PMF requires human interpretation of evidence

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI CAPABILITIES & LIMITS
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation)

A team uses AI to summarise 50 playtest responses.
The AI reports: “Players love the game.”

A. Conclude the game has strong Product–Market Fit

B. Publish immediately

C. Review the original feedback and check for specific evidence.

D. Ask the AI to confirm its conclusion

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 10.2

Lesson 10 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation).

What is the most responsible next step?

An AI tool detects that 70% of testers said they would “maybe” play again.
The AI labels this as “Strong Retention.”

What does this demonstrate?

A. AI understands player motivation

B. AI detects patterns, but humans must interpret what they mean

C. Testing must be repeatable and documented

D. AI should define PMF automatically

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 10.3

Lesson 10 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: : AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (AI detects patterns but does not understand context or goals)

metadata.title: PMF Conclusion & Showcase Opener
core_topics: Modify the details to suit your game
Make it sound like you, not a robot
Based on my evidence so far…
Yes — the evidence clearly supports it
No — I disagreed based on context
ai_focus: This metacognitive step strengthens your analytical thinking and AI literacy skills.
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
Knowledge Area: : AI ETHICS & RESPONSIBILITY
Code: K4.3 (The capability of generative AI to generate human-like content)
Teaching note: This question reinforces that AI does not take responsibility for outcomes.
learning_objectives: Craft a Persuasive Showcase Opener: Use AI responsibly to draft and refine a 30-second spoken opener grounded in real evidence.
Evidence-Based PMF Conclusion: Use your completed PMF Evidence Checklist to make a justified judgement (Strong / Partial / Weak)
I completed my PMF checklist, wrote a clear PMF conclusion, and drafted a 30-second showcase opener.
I justified my PMF conclusion with specific evidence and refined my AI-generated opener to sound authentic and accurate.
metadata.activity_description:
Mini Individual Task (5 mins)

No FitJustify (1–2 sentences)

“This is a ______ fit because ______.”

Use at least one number (e.g., 30%) and explain what it means.

What might be influencing you?

10 testers played your game.

5 said they would not play again.

Explore: Finalizing Your PMF Evidence Checklist

In the last lesson, you drafted your PMF Evidence Checklist.You are not starting from scratch.

Now your task is to synthesize and evaluate the evidence you’ve already gathered.

This is about:✔ Updating evidence✔ Strengthening weak sections✔ Checking for consistent signals✖ Not collecting new data

Strong PMF cases have evidence in multiple categories, not just one or two.

🔄 Integrate These Sources:

L10 PMF Checklist: Your existing evidence framework

L9 Fix Results: Data from your iteration improvements

L8 Feedback: NotebookLM synthesis of player responses

Fill Gaps: Add any missing evidence categories

Activity 11.1 : Finalize Your PMF Evidence Checklist

Is the evidence consistent across categories?

By the end of 8 minutes, your PMF checklist should clearly show:

Is there consistent evidence of product–market fit — or not?

Open the following documents side by side:  L10 PMF Evidence Checklist, L9 Updated Iteration Plan , L8 Feedback Analysis (NotebookLM) (8 mins)

Step 2 – Strengthen with Evidence

Replace vague statements with:• Real quotes• Numbers (e.g., 7/10 would play again)• Specific examples

Weak: “Players liked it.”Strong: “8/10 testers said they would play again.”

Step 1 -Update with Fix Results

Review your top 1–2 priority fixes from L9• Did satisfaction improve?• Were key pain points reduced or removed?• Did retention signals change?

👉 Update the relevant sections of your PMF checklist.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Problem Solving | 🟦Communication

Activity 11.2 : Using NotebookLM for PMF Assessment

NotebookLM has access to your full project knowledge base

Now you will use it to generate an objective assessment - but you remain the final decision-maker.

Open your NotebookLM workbook (with all your project files uploaded)

Upload your  Updated PMF Checklist, Iteration Plan

Paste in the sample prompt below.

Review the response and critically evaluate it:

What context does it miss?

What developer insight does AI lack?

On the next slide , write 1 paragraph choosing:

🟢 Strong PMF - Testers enjoy the game, would replay, core mechanic validated.🟡 Partial PMF - Game works but has gaps. Retention mixed. Some frustration remains.🔴 Weak PMF - Testers confused or unlikely to replay. Core misalignment.

2.   Justify your decision with real evidence — not just the AI’s opinion.

"Based on all playtest feedback, iteration results, and the PMF Evidence Checklist—does my game have product–market fit? Summarize evidence for and against."

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Problem Solving | 🟦Communication

Portfolio Entry 29: PMF Evidence Checklist with Conclusion

Use your UPDATED checklist after reviewing the AI assessment and complete the justification paragraph.

Step 1 – Quick Evidence Check (5–8 mins)

Open your L10 PMF Evidence Checklist.

✔ Update any sections affected by your L9 fixes✔ Add missing numbers or quotes✔ Ensure retention % is accurate

⚠ Do NOT rewrite the whole table — only update if needed.

Step 2 - PMF Conclusion Paragraph

( Choose one 🟢 Strong PMF, 🟡 Partial PMF ,🔴 Weak PMF, and justify why)

_____________________________________________________________________________________________

____________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________

⚠️ Use specific quotes, numbers, and iteration results — not general opinions.

Activity 11.3: Generate Your Opener with Gemini

Now you'll use Gemini to draft your showcase opener.

Unlike the PMF assessment, this is a creative marketing task where Gemini excels—it can generate persuasive language quickly.

Use the prompt template below

Modify the details to suit your game

Make it sound like you, not a robot

Say it out loud to hear how it flows

"I'm pitching [GAME TITLE], a [GENRE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here's my PMF conclusion: [paste your conclusion]. Write a 30-second showcase opener that explains: what's the game, who's it for, why they should care, and includes one piece of evidence from my PMF work."

💡 Why Gemini here? This is a generative marketing task requiring creative language, not analytical assessment. Gemini is purpose-built for this type of content creation.

Skill Boxes Key: Boxes🟪Creativity| 🟧 Critical Thinking | 🟦Communication

Portfolio Entry 30: 30-Second Showcase Opener

Paste your completed Showcase Opener in the space provided below

Partner with another student to refine your showcase openers.

This professional feedback process mirrors how real startups test their pitches before investor presentations.

Listen: Partner delivers their 30-second opener

Evaluate Clarity: Can you understand the game, audience, and value immediately?

Assess Hook Strength: Does it grab attention and make you want to learn more?

Suggest One Improvement: Provide specific, actionable feedback.

Extension: Peer Review Workshop

🔄 Switch roles and repeat. Both students should give and receive feedback.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.

This question helps students recognise that AI is best used for idea generation, not ownership of decisions.

Students must understand that humans are accountable for decisions made using AI support, especially after launch.

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 11

Lesson 11: PMF Conclusion & Showcase Opener

‹#›

You’ve gathered evidence. You’ve tested. You’ve iterated.
Now you must decide: 
	Does your game actually fit its market?

UAE Link: The UAE’s innovation strategy encourages startups to test ideas with real users before scaling. Founders are expected to make data-driven decisions — not rely on assumptions. Today, you are applying that same evidence-based approach to assess your game’s Product–Market Fit.
Reflection Question: If your game does not have strong PMF, is that failure — or insight?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

What does my Product–Market Fit evidence really mean- and how do I communicate it professionally?

Lesson 11: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Craft a Persuasive Showcase Opener
Use AI responsibly to draft and refine a 30-second spoken opener grounded in real evidence.

Evidence-Based PMF Conclusion
Use your completed PMF Evidence Checklist to make a justified judgement (Strong / Partial / Weak)

Lesson 11: Success Criteria – PMF Conclusion & Marketing

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Ethical Marketing Alignment
Create a 30-second showcase opener that accurately represents the game and its real evidence.

Justified PMF Decision
Clearly explain whether our game shows Strong, Partial, or Weak PMF using specific quotes, numbers, and iteration results.

Starter: Hope vs Evidence

‹#›

Mini Individual Task (5 mins)
Decide the PMF Level
Strong Fit
Partial Fit
No FitJustify (1–2 sentences)
“This is a ______ fit because ______.”
Use at least one number (e.g., 30%) and explain what it means.
Check Your Bias
What might be influencing you?
Focusing on positives
Small sample size
Enjoyment ≠ retention
Personal attachment

Example Scenario
10 testers played your game.
5 said they enjoyed it.
5 said they would not play again.

Explore: Finalizing Your PMF Evidence Checklist

In the last lesson, you drafted your PMF Evidence Checklist.You are not starting from scratch.
Now your task is to synthesize and evaluate the evidence you’ve already gathered.

This is about:✔ Updating evidence✔ Strengthening weak sections✔ Checking for consistent signals✖ Not collecting new data

Strong PMF cases have evidence in multiple categories, not just one or two.

🔄 Integrate These Sources:

L10 PMF Checklist: Your existing evidence framework
L9 Fix Results: Data from your iteration improvements
L8 Feedback: NotebookLM synthesis of player responses
Fill Gaps: Add any missing evidence categories

‹#›

Activity 11.1 : Finalize Your PMF Evidence Checklist

Step 3 – Fill Any Gaps
Check each category:
Target Audience
Satisfaction
Pain Points
Retention
Unique Value
Is the evidence consistent across categories?

🎯 Goal
By the end of 8 minutes, your PMF checklist should clearly show:
Is there consistent evidence of product–market fit — or not?

Open the following documents side by side:  L10 PMF Evidence Checklist, L9 Updated Iteration Plan , L8 Feedback Analysis (NotebookLM) (8 mins)

Step 2 – Strengthen with Evidence
Replace vague statements with:• Real quotes• Numbers (e.g., 7/10 would play again)• Specific examples
Weak: “Players liked it.”Strong: “8/10 testers said they would play again.”

Step 1 -Update with Fix Results
Review your top 1–2 priority fixes from L9• Did satisfaction improve?• Were key pain points reduced or removed?• Did retention signals change?
👉 Update the relevant sections of your PMF checklist.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Problem Solving | 🟦Communication

‹#›

Analyze: Making Your PMF Decision

Based on all your evidence, you must now classify your game’s Product–Market Fit status.
⚠️ This is NOT pass/fail.It is a diagnostic decision that determines your next steps.

🟢 Strong PMF

Clear evidence of player engagement, positive feedback across multiple metrics, successful iterations that solved problems, and validation from your target audience. Ready to scale.

🟡 Partial PMF

Some positive signals but mixed results. Players like certain aspects but critical issues remain. Core concept works but execution needs refinement before launch.

🔴 Weak PMF

Limited positive feedback, low engagement, or misalignment with target audience needs. Highlights important learning that informs your next, stronger iteration.

How This Impacts Your Work: 
Your PMF level directly influences your L12 Scope Freeze decisions and shapes the story you tell in your L15 Pitch Deck. Honest assessment now prevents problems later.

‹#›

Activity 11.2 : Using NotebookLM for PMF Assessment

NotebookLM has access to your full project knowledge base
Now you will use it to generate an objective assessment - but you remain the final decision-maker.

Part 1
Open your NotebookLM workbook (with all your project files uploaded)
Upload your  Updated PMF Checklist, Iteration Plan
Paste in the sample prompt below. 
Review the response and critically evaluate it:
Is it fair and balanced?
What context does it miss?
What developer insight does AI lack?
Part 2
On the next slide , write 1 paragraph choosing:
🟢 Strong PMF - Testers enjoy the game, would replay, core mechanic validated.🟡 Partial PMF - Game works but has gaps. Retention mixed. Some frustration remains.🔴 Weak PMF - Testers confused or unlikely to replay. Core misalignment.

2.   Justify your decision with real evidence — not just the AI’s opinion.

Example Prompt:
"Based on all playtest feedback, iteration results, and the PMF Evidence Checklist—does my game have product–market fit? Summarize evidence for and against."

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Problem Solving | 🟦Communication

‹#›

Portfolio Entry 29: PMF Evidence Checklist with Conclusion

Use your UPDATED checklist after reviewing the AI assessment and complete the justification paragraph.

Step 1 – Quick Evidence Check (5–8 mins)
Open your L10 PMF Evidence Checklist.
✔ Update any sections affected by your L9 fixes✔ Add missing numbers or quotes✔ Ensure retention % is accurate
⚠ Do NOT rewrite the whole table — only update if needed.
Step 2 - PMF Conclusion Paragraph

( Choose one 🟢 Strong PMF, 🟡 Partial PMF ,🔴 Weak PMF, and justify why)
_____________________________________________________________________________________________
____________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________

⚠️ Use specific quotes, numbers, and iteration results — not general opinions.

‹#›

Deepen: 30-Second Showcase Opener

Your Showcase Opener is the first thing your audience hears in Week 10 stage pitch.

🎯 Hook attention
🎮 Explain your game clearly
📊 Show real PMF evidence
🚀 Create curiosity

It is not a full pitch.It is the opening moment that sets the tone.
It will also become:
 🎙 The voiceover script for your trailer (L13 – ElevenLabs)
 🎤 The opening of your live stage pitch

Opener Example:
“Most players quit puzzle games after 5 minutes.Our game keeps 80% of testers playing past Level 3.Why? Because every level introduces one clear mechanic at a time.Let me show you why players kept coming back.”

‹#›

Activity 11.3: Generate Your Opener with Gemini

Now you'll use Gemini to draft your showcase opener. 
Unlike the PMF assessment, this is a creative marketing task where Gemini excels—it can generate persuasive language quickly.

1

Generate with Gemini

Use the prompt template below
Modify the details to suit your game

2

Edit for Authenticity

Make it sound like you, not a robot

3

Rehearse Once

Say it out loud to hear how it flows

4

Time It

Must be under 30 seconds

Your Prompt Template:
"I'm pitching [GAME TITLE], a [GENRE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here's my PMF conclusion: [paste your conclusion]. Write a 30-second showcase opener that explains: what's the game, who's it for, why they should care, and includes one piece of evidence from my PMF work."

💡 Why Gemini here? This is a generative marketing task requiring creative language, not analytical assessment. Gemini is purpose-built for this type of content creation.

Skill Boxes Key: Boxes🟪Creativity| 🟧 Critical Thinking | 🟦Communication

‹#›

Portfolio Entry 30: 30-Second Showcase Opener

Showcase Opener

Paste your completed Showcase Opener in the space provided below

‹#›

Partner with another student to refine your showcase openers. 

This professional feedback process mirrors how real startups test their pitches before investor presentations.
Listen: Partner delivers their 30-second opener
Evaluate Clarity: Can you understand the game, audience, and value immediately?
Assess Hook Strength: Does it grab attention and make you want to learn more?
Suggest One Improvement: Provide specific, actionable feedback.

Extension: Peer Review Workshop

🔄 Switch roles and repeat. Both students should give and receive feedback.

‹#›

Reflection: Understanding Your Decision

📊 Project Market Fit Decision

Based on my evidence so far…
Does my game have PMF
Yes — Strong Fit
Partial Fit
Not Yet
My strongest piece of evidence is:
(Use a real number, quote, or result)
__________________________________________________________

Take time to reflect deeply on your PMF decision-making process. 
This metacognitive step strengthens your analytical thinking and AI literacy skills.

🤖 AI vs Human Judgment

What did AI conclude about my PMF?
__________________________________________________________
Did I fully agree with the AI?
Yes — the evidence clearly supports it
Partly — I adjusted its interpretation
No — I disagreed based on context
Why was human judgment necessary here?
__________________________________________________________

🎤Responsible Communication

Does my 30-second showcase opener:
Accurately reflect real evidence
Slightly overpromise
Need revision before public sharing
One claim I personally verified as true:
_____________________________
_____________________________

‹#›

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I completed my PMF checklist, wrote a clear PMF conclusion, and drafted a 30-second showcase opener.

Most

I justified my PMF conclusion with specific evidence and refined my AI-generated opener to sound authentic and accurate.

Some

I critically evaluated the AI assessment, strengthened my conclusion with insight, and delivered a confident, evidence-based 30-second opener.

Lesson 11– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 11.1

A student copies three positive tester quotes into Gemini and asks:
"Does my game have product–market fit?"

Why is this flawed?

A. AI struggles with qualitative data.

B. The evidence is incomplete and biased.

C. PMF requires financial metrics.

D. Gemini cannot assess games.

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reflects Human Choices
Code: K2.4 (Pattern selection)

‹#›

NotebookLM says your game has Strong PMF, but replay data is mixed. What should you do?

A. Accept the AI’s conclusion.

B. Adjust the replay data.

C. Ignore the AI entirely.

D. Review the evidence and justify your own conclusion.

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 11.2

Lesson 11 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment and evaluation).

‹#›

Gemini writes: “Almost all players would replay.”
Your real replay rate is 60%.

What is responsible?

A. Keep the stronger claim.

B. Edit it to match the real data.

C. Remove statistics completely.

D. Ask for a better statistic.

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 11.3

Lesson 11 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: : AI ETHICS & RESPONSIBILITY
Code: K4.3 (The capability of generative AI to generate human-like content)

‹#›

metadata.activity_description:
Starter: From Conclusion to Action

Answer ONE question based on your PMF result:

🟢 Strong PMF → What ONE improvement would make your game undeniable?

🟡 Partial PMF → What is the ONE gap stopping you from Strong PMF?

🔴 Weak PMF → What ONE change would get testers to say “I’d play again”?

✍ Write your answer below:

____________________________________________________

____________________________________________________

____________________________________________________

____________________________________________________

In Lesson 11, you made a Product–Market Fit decision.Today, that decision drives your Scope Freeze..

You will use this decision today to:

🔒 Finalise your Scope Freeze

Explore: What Is a Scope Freeze?

A scope freeze is a professional commitment that the product is complete enough to promote.

Studios freeze scope before trailers and marketing.

It protects the product — and the team.

If you add a new feature after your trailer is made — what risks does that create?

✔ Only essential small fixes

Focus shifts: Building 🡪 Presenting

Activity 12.1 : Scope Freeze Decision Table

🛠 FIX limit: max 1–2 small items only. The "Who Decided?" column matters — own your choices.

Open your L9 Updated Iteration Plan and L11 PMF Conclusion.

For each remaining issue, make a decision using your evidence.

| Issue / Feature | Decision  (DONE / FIX / CUT) | Who Decided?  (ME / AI / BOTH) | PMF Evidence (quotes, numbers, results) |

| Level restart bug | FIX | BOTH | 3/5 testers flagged as critical |

DONE: Fixed and tested in L9

FIX (HW): Small, important fix (max 1–2). If not small → CUT.

CUT: Not fixing. Document why (time, cosmetic, not core).

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Communication

Take 3 minutes. Count your ME / AI / BOTH totals from your Scope Freeze table.

Human judgment was primary. Strong ownership of your product.

If this dominates - reflect on what that means for your accountability.

Ideal balance — AI as partner, human as decision-maker.

Reflection (Write 1–2 sentences)

My decisions were mostly led by _______.This shows that ___________________________________.One improvement I could make in how I use AI is ___________________________.

Activity 12.2 : Self-Audit

Activity 12.3: Trailer Pre-Production Plan

🎯 Before You StartAsk yourself:What should players feel in the first 3 seconds?

1. Download  the  🔗 Trailer Pre-Production Plan Template

Save it with your Game Title.

2. Complete All Sections (15–18 mins)

🎵 Suno Prompt	Write 2–3 versions (Mood • Genre • Energy)

🎙 Voice Plan	Pick 1–2 lines + choose tone

🎮 Shot List	Hook | Gameplay | End Card

🖼 Poster Plan	Title | Tagline | Visual | Quote

3. Screenshot each section and add the picture to the portfolio slide.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Creativity

Activity 12.3: Trailer Pre-Production Plan Agent

🎯 Before You StartAsk yourself:What should players feel in the first 3 seconds?

Open your 🔗NotebookLM workbook (all project files uploaded)

Copy the following 🔗system configuration template and add it to 🎥Notebook LM’s configuration.

Type in the chat “Help me create my Trailer Pre-Production Plan”

NotebookLM will ask you some questions via the chat and help generate your Pre-Production Plan.

Once completed, save the outputted tables 🎥 as a note.

Screenshot each section’s table and paste the images in your portfolio

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Creativity

Screenshot each table of your Pre-Production Plan and add it to the below spaces

Portfolio Entry 31: Trailer Pre-Production Plan

From your Scope Freeze , complete your 1–2 items marked “FIX.”

Make the agreed changes only

If possible, get 1 quick tester check

3.  Record EvidenceWrite 3–4 sentences:

__________________________________________________________________

__________________________________________________________________

__________________________________________________________________

Your short evidence summary

HOMEWORK – Implement Your “FIX” Items Before L13

Explain scope freeze as a formal, intentional decision — not just "stopping." Use the analogy of a restaurant menu: chefs don't add new dishes on the night of a dinner service. Ask: "When does building become a way of avoiding launching?" Pause for responses. This concept may feel counterintuitive to students who love adding features — acknowledge that tension directly.

Give students 10–12 minutes for this activity. Circulate and challenge any student marking more than 2 items as FIX. Ask: "Is this PMF-backed or is it personal preference?" Encourage students to be ruthless. The "Who Decided?" column is critical — flag any student where ALL decisions are AI. This feeds directly into the next Analyse slide on decision governance.

Do not shame students who over-relied on AI, but make clear that professional accountability requires owning your choices.

Give students exactly 3 minutes to count and record their ME/AI/BOTH totals. Then ask for a show of hands: "Who had more than half as AI?"

Tell students: "The work you do TODAY saves you hours in L13–L14."

Walk students through each of the four sections briefly before they begin.

Circulate and prompt students who are stuck with: "What feeling do you want players to have in the first 3 seconds?" Remind students that L13 is when they record — today is all about planning and deciding.

Walk students through each of the four sections briefly before they begin.

Circulate and prompt students who are stuck with: "What feeling do you want players to have in the first 3 seconds?" Remind students that L13 is when they record — today is all about planning and deciding.

This is a stretch task for students who finish early or need challenge.

Ask students to write their accept/reject justification in full sentences. If time allows, share 1–2 student justifications with the class. This connects back to the Decision Governance theme from slides 8–9.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. teacher_notes.notes: Use this slide to open a short whole-class discussion. Ask: "Has anyone ever kept tweaking something and made it worse?" Draw the connection between over-engineering and loss of clarity. Emphasize that constraints are a professional superpower, not a limitation. Spend 3–5 minutes here before moving to objectives.

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 12

Lesson 12: Scope Freeze & Trailer Pre-Production

‹#›

Today You Will:
Make final DONE / FIX / CUT decisions
Freeze your game scope based on evidence
Create a structured plan for your game trailer
Shift from building → presenting

UAE Link: The UAE startup ecosystem expects founders to make data-driven decisions before scaling. Successful teams freeze scope to protect quality
Reflection Question: Why might adding "just one more feature" before launch actually weaken your final product?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How do professional developers decide what to stop building — and start promoting?

Lesson 12: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Plan Creative Assets Strategically
Design a complete trailer and poster pre-production plan — before any recording or editing begins.

Apply Professional Scope Control
Use PMF evidence to categorize every remaining issue as DONE, FIX, or CUT — with clear justification.

Lesson 12: Success Criteria – Scope Freeze & Trailer Pre-Production

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Complete Pre-Production Planning
Produce a structured Trailer Plan including: soundtrack prompt, voiceover lines, shot list, and poster content — ready for L13.

Justify Scope Decisions
Clearly explain why each issue was marked DONE, FIX (max 1–2), or CUT — citing PMF evidence as the basis.

Starter: From Conclusion to Action

‹#›

Reflection Task (3 mins)

Answer ONE question based on your PMF result:
🟢 Strong PMF → What ONE improvement would make your game undeniable?
🟡 Partial PMF → What is the ONE gap stopping you from Strong PMF?
🔴 Weak PMF → What ONE change would get testers to say “I’d play again”?

✍ Write your answer below:
____________________________________________________
____________________________________________________
____________________________________________________
____________________________________________________

In Lesson 11, you made a Product–Market Fit decision.Today, that decision drives your Scope Freeze..

Why This Matters
You will use this decision today to:
✅ Decide what to FIX
❌ Decide what to CUT
🔒 Finalise your Scope Freeze

Explore: What Is a Scope Freeze?

A scope freeze is a professional commitment that the product is complete enough to promote.

It means:
Studios freeze scope before trailers and marketing.
It protects the product — and the team.

Discussion Question
If you add a new feature after your trailer is made — what risks does that create?

✔ No new features
✔ No new mechanics
✔ Only essential small fixes

Focus shifts: Building 🡪 Presenting

‹#›

Activity 12.1 : Scope Freeze Decision Table

🛠 FIX limit: max 1–2 small items only. The "Who Decided?" column matters — own your choices.

Open your L9 Updated Iteration Plan and L11 PMF Conclusion. 
For each remaining issue, make a decision using your evidence.


| Issue / Feature | Decision  (DONE / FIX / CUT) | Who Decided?  (ME / AI / BOTH) | PMF Evidence (quotes, numbers, results) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Level restart bug | FIX | BOTH | 3/5 testers flagged as critical |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

FIX (HW): Small, important fix (max 1–2). If not small → CUT.
CUT: Not fixing. Document why (time, cosmetic, not core).

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Communication

‹#›

By Lesson 12, AI has touched nearly every major decision point in your project.

Core Principle: AI informs. Humans decide.

Game Review

Lesson 4: AI-assisted evaluation

Feedback Analysis

Lesson 8: Automated sentiment & themes

Iteration Priorities

Lesson 8: AI-ranked improvement list

PMF Assessment

Lesson 11: AI-driven product-market fit

Did you use AI as a tool?

You directed, evaluated, and owned every conclusion.

Or did you outsource judgment?

AI led — you followed without critical evaluation.

Ask yourself honestly:

‹#›

Analyze: Decision Governance: Did You Lead — or Follow?

Take 3 minutes. Count your ME / AI / BOTH totals from your Scope Freeze table.

🧠ME

You led

Human judgment was primary. Strong ownership of your product.

🤖AI

AI led

If this dominates - reflect on what that means for your accountability.

🤝BOTH

Collaborative

Ideal balance — AI as partner, human as decision-maker.

Reflection (Write 1–2 sentences)
My decisions were mostly led by _______.This shows that ___________________________________.One improvement I could make in how I use AI is ___________________________.

‹#›

Activity 12.2 : Self-Audit

❌ Without Planning
Wasted recording sessions.
Inconsistent tone. 
Re-shoots. 
Poor pacing. 
A trailer that doesn't represent your game.

Check it out: Fortnite Gameplay TrailerNotice how the editing builds excitement and shows key mechanics clearly — this clarity is the result of planning before recording.”🎥 Watch here: Fortnite Battle Royale – Gameplay Trailer

Professionals separate the Decision Phase from the Production Phase. 
Today is your Decision Phase — L13 and L14 are when you create.

Deepen: Why Trailer Pre-Production Matters

‹#›

⚡ Hook (2–3 sec)
Grabs attention
Creates curiosity
Makes viewer stay

🌟 Highlight Moments
Mechanics
Best gameplay scenes
Shows unique feature
Proves it’s fun

A trailer creates a powerful first impression, showcases your game’s genre, mood, and quality, and turns viewers into players by driving downloads and plays.

📖 Story / Context
Gives meaning to the gameplay.
World setting
Character goal
Conflict or challenge

📣Call to Action (Social Poster)
Tells the viewer what to do next.
“Play Now”
“Download Today”
Store link or QR code

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

Lesson 12– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 12.1

During the Scope Freeze, a student marks most items as “AI” in the “Who decided?” column because they followed the NotebookLM recommendation without changing it.

What is the most important AI literacy concern here?

A. The AI may generate slower responses over time.

B. The student may be outsourcing human judgment to the AI.

C. The AI might consume too many computational resources.

D. The student did not use enough AI tools in the process.

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment)

‹#›

A student says, “NotebookLM decided my game has Strong PMF, so I don’t need to review the evidence myself.”

A. AI outputs depend on patterns in provided data, not full contextual reasoning.

B. AI tools are unable to process written feedback.

C. AI systems cannot summarize information accurately.

D. AI always prioritizes cosmetic improvements first.

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 12.2

Lesson 12 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (Pattern recognition vs. reasoning).

Which AI limitation is most relevant to challenge this statement?

‹#›

Students use Suno to generate soundtrack options and ElevenLabs for voiceover lines in their trailer pre-production plan.

Which statement best reflects responsible AI authorship in this context?

A. The AI is the sole creator because it generated the audio output.

B. Authorship does not apply when AI tools are involved.

C. The AI owns the final trailer because it produced the assets.

D. The student remains the author because they directed, selected, and refined the outputs.

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 12.3

Lesson 12 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: : AI’s Role in Society
Code: K5.3 (Authorship & ownership)

‹#›

metadata.activity_description:
Lesson 13: Gameplay Capture & Audio Assets

• Verify bug fixes• Capture gameplay footage• Generate soundtrack (Suno AI)• Produce voiceover (ElevenLabs)

UAE Link: UAE studios like Ubisoft Abu Dhabi lock pre-production decisions before execution to protect deadlines and production quality

Reflection Question: What happens to quality, deadlines, and team efficiency when creative decisions are made during production instead of before it?

Explore: The Professional Production Pipeline

Professional studios use structured pipelines to move from concept to launch. Each phase has a purpose. Mixing them creates chaos.

Creative decisions locked. Shot list, prompts, and scope freeze confirmed.

Generate audio. Execute the plan exactly.

Assemble footage and audio into a polished trailer using your captured assets.

Showcase your trailer and final build to

⚠️ Today = Build. No redesigning mechanics. No adding features.

Second-guessing steals production time.

Activity 13.1 : Homework Bug Verification

Launch your game version with the homework fixes applied.

Directly test the exact issues you fixed.Recreate the original problem situation.

Confirm Outcome✔ Does the issue no longer happen?✔ Does the game still run properly overall?

✅ Working → Ready for trailer capture

❌ Still Bugged → Write a short note describing the issue

⚠️ No debugging now. If it failed, document it and move forward.

Open the 1–2 scope-freeze bug fixes you completed as homework from Lesson 12.

This is a rapid verification check — not a debugging session.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Problem Solving

Activity 13.2:  Gameplay Capture

Open your L12 Shot List and capture the following three essential moments. These are the building blocks your editor needs in Lesson 14:

📌 This footage is your raw material for Lesson 14's editing session.

Unlabeled or corrupted files will cost you editing time you don't have.

Gameplay Recording (12 mins)

The first 3 seconds that stop a viewer from scrolling. Must be visually arresting.

A clear demonstration of what makes your game unique and fun.

One strong, beautiful or exciting moment that defines your game's aesthetic.

- Record clean. No glitches. No lag.

- Keep gameplay smooth and intentional.

- If it’s not good — re-record immediately.

- Save everything in your project folder.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking |🟦Creativity

Activity 13.3: Your Suno Workflow

Open your NotebookLM L12 prompt.Do NOT rewrite it.

Paste into Suno. (log in first)Create 2–3 versions.

3.  Choose Best ( v4.5-all are free)• Does it match my game’s tone?• Does it fit my hook moment?

Change one small detail only → regenerate.5.  Export

Wait for sound to fully process.

Download the file. Save as Soundtrack_Final.mp3.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Creativity

Activity 13.4: Trailer Voiceover with ElevenLabs

Open ElevenLabs → Text to SpeechPaste your 1–2 voiceover lines from your L12 plan.Do NOT rewrite.

Select a voice that matches your game’s tone(confident, energetic, calm, dramatic).

3. Adjust Tone (If Needed)

Slightly tweak stability or clarity sliders.Generate 2 versions.

• Does it sound natural?• Does it match my game’s energy?• Would my audience believe this voice?

Download as MP3.Save as: Voiceover_Final.mp3

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Creativity

Stretch Task: AI Trailer Visual Enhancement

Main character, boss, prop, or iconic object.Must already exist in your game.

Sketch or simple blockout (Nano Banana optional).Screenshot clearly.

Upload image or use text prompt.Generate refined 3D version.Keep it accurate to your real game.

Place original vs AI version side-by-side.Does it improve the trailer?Is it still honest?

Use Meshy AI to create a cinematic version of a character or prop from your game to enhance your trailer.

• Original screenshot• Meshy version• 1-sentence reflection on authenticity

______________________________________

Extension Task: Alternative Asset Exploration

Generate alternative versions of your audio assets and evaluate them against your primary selections.

Return to Suno AI and generate one alternative soundtrack with a deliberately different genre, tempo, or mood. Do not use the same prompt — intentionally shift one variable and observe how it changes the emotional impact of your trailer concept.

Return to ElevenLabs and apply a different voice style — more authoritative, more playful, or more urgent — to the same voiceover lines. Compare the two versions side by side.

💬 Comparison Question: Which combination of soundtrack and voiceover tone most authentically matches your target audience's expectations — and why? Write a one-sentence justification for your primary selection.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Explain scope freeze as a formal, intentional decision — not just "stopping." Use the analogy of a restaurant menu: chefs don't add new dishes on the night of a dinner service. Ask: "When does building become a way of avoiding launching?" Pause for responses. This concept may feel counterintuitive to students who love adding features — acknowledge that tension directly.

Do not shame students who over-relied on AI, but make clear that professional accountability requires owning your choices.

Give students exactly 3 minutes to count and record their ME/AI/BOTH totals. Then ask for a show of hands: "Who had more than half as AI?"

Give students exactly 3 minutes to count and record their ME/AI/BOTH totals. Then ask for a show of hands: "Who had more than half as AI?"

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.

This question helps students recognise that AI is best used for idea generation, not ownership of decisions.

Students must understand that humans are accountable for decisions made using AI support, especially after launch.

‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 13

Lesson 13: Gameplay Capture & Audio Assets

‹#›

Today’s Production Tasks
• Verify bug fixes• Capture gameplay footage• Generate soundtrack (Suno AI)• Produce voiceover (ElevenLabs)

UAE Link: UAE studios like Ubisoft Abu Dhabi lock pre-production decisions before execution to protect deadlines and production quality
Reflection Question: What happens to quality, deadlines, and team efficiency when creative decisions are made during production instead of before it?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How do we turn a finished game into a professional marketing asset?

Lesson 13: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Use Generative AI Responsibly
Use Suno AI and ElevenLabs strategically while maintaining human control over tone, accuracy, and final decisions.

Produce Marketing Assets
Capture clean gameplay footage and generate professional-quality audio assets aligned with your pre-production plan

Lesson 13: Success Criteria – Gameplay Capture & Audio Assets

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Complete Pre-Production Planning
Evaluate AI-generated outputs and select the version that best represents the game honestly and effectively.

Professional Execution
Produce gameplay footage, a soundtrack, and a voiceover that match the L12 Trailer Plan exactly.

Starter: Trailer Hook Challenge

‹#›

🧠 THINK (1 min – Silent):
“The first 10 seconds work because __________.”

👥 PAIR (2 mins)
Discuss and agree on ONE main reason:
Gameplay moment
Music
Pace / speed
Visual impact
Energy

🗣 SHARE (1–2 mins)
Each pair gives ONE different reason.
(No repeating answers.)

🎥 Watch Carefully (Fortnite Official Trailer)
As you watch the first 10 seconds, focus on:
What do you see first?
What do you hear first?
What emotion does it create?

Explore: The Professional Production Pipeline

Professional studios use structured pipelines to move from concept to launch. Each phase has a purpose. Mixing them creates chaos.

1

📋 L12 — Plan

Creative decisions locked. Shot list, prompts, and scope freeze confirmed.

2

🛠 L13 — Build

Generate audio. Execute the plan exactly.

3

🎞 L14 — Edit

Assemble footage and audio into a polished trailer using your captured assets.

4

🚀 L15 — Present

Showcase your trailer and final build to 
your class audience.

⚠️ Today = Build. No redesigning mechanics. No adding features. 
Second-guessing steals production time.

‹#›

Activity 13.1 : Homework Bug Verification

Launch your game version with the homework fixes applied.
Directly test the exact issues you fixed.Recreate the original problem situation.
Confirm Outcome✔ Does the issue no longer happen?✔ Does the game still run properly overall?
Record Status
✅ Working → Ready for trailer capture
❌ Still Bugged → Write a short note describing the issue

⚠️ No debugging now. If it failed, document it and move forward.

⚙️ Core Mechanic

A clear demonstration of what makes your game unique and fun.

✨ Visual Highlight

One strong, beautiful or exciting moment that defines your game's aesthetic.

🎥Recording Tips
- Record clean. No glitches. No lag.
- Keep gameplay smooth and intentional.
- If it’s not good — re-record immediately.
- Save everything in your project folder.
- Keep all takes.
- Name files exactly:

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking |🟦Creativity

‹#›

AI Discovery: Suno AI – Teacher Demo

Suno is an AI-powered music generation platform that creates full songs from text prompts.
You simply describe the type of music you want — and Suno generates

How to use Suno
1️⃣ Click Create2️⃣ Enter a clear, detailed prompt3️⃣ Select instrumental or vocal4️⃣ Generate 2 versions5️⃣ Listen critically6️⃣ Download the best fit

‹#›

Activity 13.3: Your Suno Workflow

1. Copy Your Prompt
Open your NotebookLM L12 prompt.Do NOT rewrite it.
2.  Generate
Paste into Suno. (log in first)Create 2–3 versions.
3.  Choose Best ( v4.5-all are free)• Does it match my game’s tone?• Does it fit my hook moment?
Select the strongest.
4. Refine (If Needed)
Change one small detail only → regenerate.5.  Export
Wait for sound to fully process.
Download the file. Save as Soundtrack_Final.mp3.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Creativity

‹#›

AI Discovery: ElevenLabs– Teacher Demo

ElevenLabs is an AI voice generation platform that creates realistic voiceovers from text.You enter a script, choose a voice, and ElevenLabs generates natural-sounding speech

How to use ElevenLabs
1️⃣ Select Text to Speech2️⃣ Add your voiceover text3️⃣ Select a suitable voice based on your requirements4️⃣ Modify the configuration if required5️⃣Download the voiceover clip

‹#›

Activity 13.4: Trailer Voiceover with ElevenLabs

1. Paste Your Script
Open ElevenLabs → Text to SpeechPaste your 1–2 voiceover lines from your L12 plan.Do NOT rewrite.
2.  Choose a Voice
Select a voice that matches your game’s tone(confident, energetic, calm, dramatic).
3. Adjust Tone (If Needed)
Slightly tweak stability or clarity sliders.Generate 2 versions.
4.  Listen Critically
• Does it sound natural?• Does it match my game’s energy?• Would my audience believe this voice?
Choose the strongest.
5.  Export
Download as MP3.Save as: Voiceover_Final.mp3

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking | 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Creativity

‹#›

Deepen: AI in Creative Production

✅ What AI Can Do

Generate Audio

Produce music and voiceover at speed and scale no human could match alone

Adjust Tone

Shift style, mood, and energy based on prompt parameters

Offer Variations

Give you multiple interpretations of a single creative brief quickly

❌ What AI Cannot Do

Know Your Brand Identity

AI has no memory of what your game is or what makes it unique

Understand Creative Intent

It generates statistically probable outputs — not meaningful choices

Accept Responsibility

If AI output misrepresents your game, you are accountable — not the tool

🎯 Core Principle: AI is a production accelerator. Humans decide what honestly and effectively represents the game. You are the creative director — AI is your assistant.

‹#›

Stretch Task: AI Trailer Visual Enhancement

Step-by-Step Workflow
1.  Choose the Asset
Main character, boss, prop, or iconic object.Must already exist in your game.
2. Create a Reference
Sketch or simple blockout (Nano Banana optional).Screenshot clearly.
3.  Enhance in Meshy
Upload image or use text prompt.Generate refined 3D version.Keep it accurate to your real game.
4.  Compare & Decide
Place original vs AI version side-by-side.Does it improve the trailer?Is it still honest?

Use Meshy AI to create a cinematic version of a character or prop from your game to enhance your trailer.

📂 Evidence
• Original screenshot• Meshy version• 1-sentence reflection on authenticity
______________________________________

‹#›

Reflection: Human Creativity Check

What parts of my game are uniquely human — things no AI tool could create for me?
Think about:
🎮 A mechanic inspired by your personal experience
💬 Dialogue that reflects your humour or personality
🎨 Design choices based on your taste or culture
💡 A problem you solved in your own creative way
❤️ An emotion you wanted players to feel
Write 3 Specific Examples - explain WHY AI could not generate this without knowing you.
____________________________________
____________________________________
____________________________________

AI can generate ideas, assets, and suggestions.
But your game exists because of you.

Game studios use AI to speed up production.
But creative vision, emotional direction, and final decisions remain human responsibilities.

‹#›

Extension Task: Alternative Asset Exploration

Generate alternative versions of your audio assets and evaluate them against your primary selections.

🎵 Alternative Soundtrack

Return to Suno AI and generate one alternative soundtrack with a deliberately different genre, tempo, or mood. Do not use the same prompt — intentionally shift one variable and observe how it changes the emotional impact of your trailer concept.

🎙 Alternative Voice Tone

Return to ElevenLabs and apply a different voice style — more authoritative, more playful, or more urgent — to the same voiceover lines. Compare the two versions side by side.

💬 Comparison Question: Which combination of soundtrack and voiceover tone most authentically matches your target audience's expectations — and why? Write a one-sentence justification for your primary selection.
________________________________________________________________________________________________________________________

‹#›

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I captured gameplay and created a Suno soundtrack and ElevenLabs voiceover that followed my L12 plan. All files are saved correctly.

Most

I compared multiple AI outputs and chose the version that best matches my game’s tone and target audience — not just the most impressive one.

Some

I refined my prompts strategically when results missed the mark, improving quality through deliberate iteration.

Lesson 13– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 13.1

AI generates three soundtrack options for your trailer. One sounds exciting and energetic — but it doesn't match the tone of your game at all.

What is the most responsible action?

A. Use the most exciting one because trailers should always feel energetic, regardless of game tone

B. Let AI choose automatically based on the highest engagement score

C. Select the option that aligns with the game's actual identity, tone, and target audience

D. Generate 10 more automatically until one feels perfect without further evaluation

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Human Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human judgment)

‹#›

The AI-generated voiceover for your trailer sounds great — but it exaggerates gameplay features that your game doesn't actually have.

A. Keep it for maximum marketing impact — trailers are always aspirational

B. Revise the voiceover text to accurately reflect actual gameplay

C. Keep generating new AI versions until one happens to be both exciting and accurate

D. Remove the voiceover entirely to avoid any risk of inaccuracy

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 13.2

Lesson 13 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: AI's Role in Society
Code: K5.4 (Accountability and ethical responsibility ).

What should you do?

‹#›

Why is it important that humans complete the Trailer Pre-Production Plan before using AI tools like Suno or ElevenLabs?

Select the best answer:

A. So AI tools generate longer and more detailed outputs

B. So fewer AI prompts need to be written

C. So AI can automatically correct poor gameplay design

D. So humans define creative intent before AI generates assets

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 13.3

Lesson 13 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: : AI Reshapes Work & Human Roles
Code: K3.3 (Human responsibility for final decisions)

‹#›

metadata.activity_description:
🔍 Stage 1 – Individual Reaction (2 mins)

Would you trust AI to generate your entire trailer?

_____________________________________________

Why or why not?_____________________________________________

📊 Stage 2 – Evidence Builder (3 mins)

In pairs, complete the below table for each trailer type.

Runway ML is an AI-powered creative tool that helps you generate and edit videos, images, and animations using simple text prompts.

Watch the Runway video demonstration and complete the task below.

| Trailer Type | Advantages | Risks |

| AI-Generated Trailer | 1. ________________ 2. ________________ | 1. ________________ 2. ________________ |

| Manually Edited Trailer | 1. ________________ 2. ________________ | 1. ________________ 2. ________________ |

Key Insight: Automation ≠ Ownership. AI can assist, but the creative vision behind a strong marketing asset must be human-led.

🤖 AI-Generated (Runway ML)

Fast production turnaround

Automated visual generation

Risk of generic, template output

Limited creative ownership

✂️ Manual Editing (CapCut)

Authentic gameplay footage

Intentional pacing decisions

Time-intensive but purposeful

Explore: AI-Generated Video vs. Manual Editing

Trim & split to tighten pacing

🎯 Focus on pacing and clarity — not effects.

Explore: Teacher Demo – CapCut

Detailed Steps to Create Your Game Trailer (CapCut) (25 mins)

Open CapCut → tap New Project

Social Media Poster/end card image

3.  Arrange clips to match this structure:

Game Details (3–5s): Title + Tagline + Deeplink  (Social Poster)

Add soundtrack and voiceover to timeline

Sync audio to each section (Hook / Gameplay / Details)

Add your poster/end card at the end (3–5s)

Final check: text readable + audio levels balanced

8.   Export your trailer (1080p)

✂️ Split clips to remove boring parts

Cut menus, pauses, loading screens

⚡Speed up slow moments (1.2x–1.5x)

Keep clips short and punchy

🎥 Use quick, clean transitions

Match cuts to the music beat

🔊 Lower music under voiceover

🎥Check out this Video with the clear steps you will need.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Creativity | 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Communication

Activity 14.1 : Build your Trailer

Explain scope freeze as a formal, intentional decision — not just "stopping." Use the analogy of a restaurant menu: chefs don't add new dishes on the night of a dinner service. Ask: "When does building become a way of avoiding launching?" Pause for responses. This concept may feel counterintuitive to students who love adding features — acknowledge that tension directly.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

It helps students practise evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale.

This question helps students recognise that AI is best used for idea generation, not ownership of decisions.

Students must understand that humans are accountable for decisions made using AI support, especially after launch.

I can explain the trade-offs between AI-generated video and manual editing, and justify my creative choices.

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 3

Lesson 14

Lesson 14: Video Trailer Creation

‹#›

You have a frozen scope, polished gameplay, and audio assets. 

Today, you build the final trailer — your most powerful marketing deliverable.

UAE Link: Dubai’s GameForward accelerator supports Emirati game developers and showcases top projects at the Dubai Esports and Games Festival — proving that strong trailers and marketing are essential for success in the UAE gaming industry.
Reflection Question:Why can a great game fail without strong marketing? Think of a title you loved that nobody talked about — what was missing?

Required Files for Today

🔗Recorded Gameplay Video’s
🔗Suno Soundtrack
🔗ElevenLabs Voice Over
🔗Social Media Poster

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

Is your trailer just a video…or is it a strategic marketing tool?

Lesson 14: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

AI vs. Manual Creative Control
Evaluate the trade-offs between AI-generated video and manual creative control.

Structured Game Trailer 
Construct a 15–20 second structured game trailer using CapCut and pre-planned assets.

Lesson 14: Success Criteria – Video Trailer Creation

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

AI Literacy & Judgment
I can explain why manual editing provides more control than AI-generated video.

Creative Execution
I can build a structured trailer that follows my L12 plan and integrates gameplay, soundtrack, and voiceover.

‹#›

Mini Task
🔍 Stage 1 – Individual Reaction (2 mins)
Would you trust AI to generate your entire trailer?
_____________________________________________
Why or why not?_____________________________________________
📊 Stage 2 – Evidence Builder (3 mins)
In pairs, complete the below table for each trailer type.

Runway ML is an AI-powered creative tool that helps you generate and edit videos, images, and animations using simple text prompts.
Watch the Runway video demonstration and complete the task below.


| Trailer Type | Advantages | Risks |
| --- | --- | --- |
| AI-Generated Trailer | 1. ________________ 2. ________________ | 1. ________________ 2. ________________ |
| Manually Edited Trailer | 1. ________________ 2. ________________ | 1. ________________ 2. ________________ |

Starter: Watch & Discuss

Key Insight: Automation ≠ Ownership. AI can assist, but the creative vision behind a strong marketing asset must be human-led.

🤖 AI-Generated (Runway ML)
Fast production turnaround
Automated visual generation
Lower effort to produce
Risk of generic, template output
Limited creative ownership

✂️ Manual Editing (CapCut)
Full creative control
Authentic gameplay footage
Stronger brand identity
Intentional pacing decisions
Time-intensive but purposeful

Explore: AI-Generated Video vs. Manual Editing

‹#›

Features to Demo

Interface overview
Upload gameplay clips
Drag clips onto timeline
Trim & split to tighten pacing
Align voiceover
Preview & export

🎯 Focus on pacing and clarity — not effects.

Explore: Teacher Demo – CapCut

‹#›

Now you will build your game trailer using CapCut. 
Follow your Lesson 12 structure exactly. Every second of your trailer should be intentional.

1

HOOK

2–3 seconds. Create immediate curiosity or tension.

2

GAMEPLAY

8–12 seconds. Show your core mechanic clearly.

3

Social Media Poster(🔗L7)

3–5 seconds. Brand name, tagline, Endstar deeplink.

🎵 Suno Soundtrack

🎙️ Layer Voiceover

🔗 Add Social Poster

▶️ Preview + Refine

‹#›

Analyze: Your Trailer Structure

Detailed Steps to Create Your Game Trailer (CapCut) (25 mins)
Open CapCut → tap New Project
Import:
Gameplay clips
Suno soundtrack
ElevenLabs voiceover
Social Media Poster/end card image
3.  Arrange clips to match this structure:
Hook (2–3s)
Key Gameplay (8–12s)
Game Details (3–5s): Title + Tagline + Deeplink  (Social Poster)
Add soundtrack and voiceover to timeline
Sync audio to each section (Hook / Gameplay / Details)
Add your poster/end card at the end (3–5s)
Final check: text readable + audio levels balanced
8.   Export your trailer (1080p)

Editing Tips
✂️ Split clips to remove boring parts
Cut menus, pauses, loading screens
⚡Speed up slow moments (1.2x–1.5x)
Keep clips short and punchy
🎥 Use quick, clean transitions
Match cuts to the music beat
🔊 Lower music under voiceover
Add smooth fade in/out
⏱ 15–20 seconds max
If it feels slow, cut it

Need More Help?
 🎥Check out this Video with the clear steps you will need.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Creativity | 🟧 Computational Thinking | 🟦Communication

Activity 14.1 : Build your Trailer

‹#›

‹#›

Example Gameplay Trailer

Analyze: AI content Attribution Checklist


| AI Tool Used | What the Tool Generated | How I Modified / Curated the Output |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Suno AI | ____________________ | _____________________ |
| ElevenLabs | ____________________ | _____________________ |
| Gemini | ____________________ | _____________________ |
| ChatGPT | ____________________ | _____________________ |
| NotebookLM | ____________________ | _____________________ |
| CapCut AI | ____________________ | _____________________ |

📝 Attribution Statement
“I used ____________________________to generate ____________________. I then modified/refined it by ____________________ to ensure it accurately represents my game and reflects my own creative decisions and does not misrepresent actual gameplay.”

Skill Boxes Key: 🟩 Critical Thinking

‹#›

Take 3 minutes to reflect on your final trailer.

🎯 Does my trailer clearly communicate my game’s core idea?
_____________________________________________
⚡ Is the pacing engaging from the first 3 seconds?
_____________________________________________
🔧 What is one improvement I would make next time?
_____________________________________________

A trailer is not gameplay - it is persuasion.
Your job is to make players want to play.

🤖 AI Reflection Question
 Where did AI support my trailer workflow — and where did I make the final decisions?
__________________________________________________

‹#›

Reflection: Trailer Creation

Homework: Finalize Your Game Trailer

🎬 What You Must Do:
Finish editing your trailer (15–20 seconds)
Tighten pacing (cut unnecessary clips)
Ensure audio is aligned (voiceover + soundtrack)
Check that visuals clearly represent your gameplay
Export the final version

‹#›

Your trailer must be fully completed and polished before next lesson.

Extension: Cinematic Enhancement Challenge

For those ready to push further: use Runway ML or CapCut AI effects to enhance your trailer — then critically evaluate the result.

Enhance
     Use Runway ML or CapCut AI to add 1–2 subtle cinematic improvements (lighting, transitions, pacing).
ExportSave your original version and your AI-enhanced version.
CompareWatch both side-by-side. Which looks stronger?
4.   Reflect (3 sentences)       Did AI improve quality or reduce authenticity?       Which version better represents your game?

⚖️ Ensure AI enhancements do not misrepresent actual gameplay

🔗 Check out this video for tips on how to create more cinematic videos

‹#›

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I produced a structured 15–20 second trailer using my planned assets and Lesson 12 structure.

Most

I edited confidently in CapCut, synced audio correctly, and produced a polished trailer that clearly shows my game’s value.

Some

I can explain the trade-offs between AI-generated video and manual editing, and justify my creative choices.

Lesson 14– Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 14.1

A student prompts an AI video tool to “create an epic trailer for my desert exploration game.” The output includes dramatic combat scenes that do not exist in the real game.

Why did this likely happen?

A) The AI misunderstood the file format

B) AI predicts patterns from prompts, not real game knowledge.

C) The AI intentionally adds features to improve marketing

D) The AI has creative imagination

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: The Nature of AI / AI’s Capabilities & Limits
Code: K1.3 K4.1 (Generative AI / Pattern v Reasoning)

‹#›

An AI voiceover tool consistently produces a specific accent and tone when generating narration.
